    For Hollerith: Recurring revenue instead of one-time sales. Cust= omers who needed long-term data processing work were locked in =E2=80=94 th= ey depended on Hollerith for both machines and cards. The rental payments o= ver multiple years far exceeded what a one-time sale price would have been.=

    For customers: Lower initial cost =E2=80=94 renting was cheaper = than buying outright, which made the technology accessible to organizations= that could not afford a large capital purchase. But it created dependency = =E2=80=94 you could not own the machine, could not modify it, could not use= it without Hollerith's ongoing cooperation.

    IBM co= ntinued and extended this leasing model throughout the 20th century. For de= cades, IBM did not sell computers =E2=80=94 it leased them, controlled the = software, sold the consumables (punch cards, printer ribbons), and provided= maintenance services. The customer owned nothing. IBM owned everything. Th= e customer paid forever.

    This b= usiness model =E2=80=94 hardware and software as services, not products =E2= =80=94 is the direct ancestor of the modern cloud computing model. Amazon W= eb Services, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure all rent computing capacity rath= er than selling hardware. The customer pays by the hour or the month. The m= odel is 130 years old, originating in Hollerith's decision in the 1890s.

    The fallout with= the Census Bureau =E2=80=94 and what it reveals

    Holler= ith's relationship with the Census Bureau eventually soured badly, and the = story reveals something important about the economics of proprietary techno= logy.

    For th= e 1900 census, Hollerith rented his machines at prices the Census Bureau co= nsidered exorbitant. Since the Census Bureau had built its entire operation= around Hollerith's system =E2=80=94 trained staff, established procedures,= accumulated knowledge =E2=80=94 switching to a different approach was extr= emely difficult. Hollerith knew this and priced accordingly.

    The Ce= nsus Bureau's director, William Merriam, was furious. He commissioned one o= f his own statisticians =E2=80=94 James Powers =E2=80=94 to develop an alte= rnative punched card system that the Census Bureau could own outright. Powe= rs succeeded =E2=80=94 his system, using mechanical rather than electrical = sensing, worked well and was used for the 1910 census.

    Holler= ith lost his most important customer.

    But th= e episode illustrates a pattern that recurs throughout computing history: t= he vendor of a proprietary system that becomes essential infrastructure gai= ns enormous pricing power, which eventually motivates customers to develop = alternatives. IBM faced this repeatedly throughout the 20th century. Micros= oft faced it with PC operating systems. Google faces it with search adverti= sing. The pattern is structural.

    The railroad con= nection =E2=80=94 computing leaves the government

    The ce= nsus is the event that made Hollerith famous, but the railroads are the eve= nt that made his company profitable.

    Americ= an railroads in the 1890s were large, complex organizations generating enor= mous amounts of data: freight manifests, passenger counts, route statistics= , car inventory, revenue by route, payroll for thousands of employees. Mana= ging this data by hand was slow, expensive, and error-prone.

    Holler= ith's system was a perfect fit. The New York Central Railroad, the Pennsylv= ania Railroad, and others adopted Hollerith tabulating machines for their a= ccounting and statistical operations. The railroad market was larger than t= he government market and more stable =E2=80=94 railroad companies needed da= ta processing year-round, not just once a decade.

    This t= ransition =E2=80=94 from government census tabulation to private sector bus= iness data processing =E2=80=94 was the moment computing became an industry= rather than a government project. The customers were now companies with on= going commercial needs, not government agencies with decennial tasks. The m= arket was permanent.

    This i= s the birth of the data processing industry =E2=80=94 computing as a commer= cial service sold to businesses. Everything that followed =E2=80=94 IBM mai= nframes for corporate accounting, minicomputers for business operations, PC= software for office work, cloud computing for enterprise applications =E2= =80=94 is the continuation of the market Hollerith created with the railroa= ds in the 1890s.

    The company line= age =E2=80=94 from Hollerith to IBM

    The co= rporate history is worth tracing because IBM's influence on computing for m= ost of the 20th century was total:

    1896 =E2=80=94 Hollerith founds the Tabulating Machine Company i= n Washington DC. Manufactures and rents punched card tabulating systems.

    1911 =E2=80=94 The Tabulating Machine Company merges with the Co= mputing Scale Company (which made weighing scales for retail stores) and th= e International Time Recording Company (which made time clocks for employee= attendance). The merged entity is called Computing Tabulating Recording Co= mpany, or CTR. Hollerith stays on as a consultant. He sells his share and r= etires a wealthy man.

    1914 =E2=80=94 Thomas Watson Sr. joins CTR as general manager. W= atson is a salesman of extraordinary talent =E2=80=94 he transforms CTR fro= m a loose collection of product lines into a disciplined, sales-focused org= anization. His philosophy: the product is secondary to the salesforce. Sell= the customer a solution, not a machine.

    1924 =E2=80=94 Watson renames CTR as International Business Mach= ines =E2=80=94 IBM. The name signals ambition: not a local tabulating compa= ny but an international computing enterprise.

    1930s=E2=80=931950s =E2=80=94 IBM dominates the punched card dat= a processing market. Almost every large American corporation, government ag= ency, and university uses IBM equipment. IBM's punched card machines proces= s payroll, accounting, inventory, insurance records, tax records.

    1950s =E2=80=94 IBM transitions from punched card machines to el= ectronic computers. Its mainframe computers =E2=80=94 the IBM 701, 702, and= eventually the legendary System/360 =E2=80=94 become the backbone of corpo= rate computing worldwide.

    1960s=E2=80=931970s =E2=80=94 IBM's System/360, introduced in 19= 64, is the most important computer product ever manufactured. It establishe= s the concept of a compatible family of computers =E2=80=94 programs writte= n for one System/360 model run on all others. It creates the software indus= try as a separate entity. It dominates global computing.

    1981 =E2=80=94 IBM introduces the IBM Personal Computer =E2=80= =94 the IBM PC. Its open architecture =E2=80=94 published specifications th= at any company could build compatible hardware from =E2=80=94 inadvertently= creates the PC industry. Every Windows PC sold today is a descendant of th= e IBM PC.

    The li= ne from Hollerith's mercury cups and punched cards in 1890 to the PC on you= r desk is direct and continuous. Every company, institution, and technology= in that chain traces back to a man who watched the 1880 census being tabul= ated by hand and thought there had to be a better way.

    What electricity= plus punched cards produced =E2=80=94 the conceptual advance

    We hav= e been building a picture of how the key concepts accumulated across histor= y. Hollerith's contribution to this picture is specific and important.

    Before= Hollerith, punched cards were program storage =E2=80=94 Jacquard's cards t= old a machine what operations to perform. The cards were instructions.

    Holler= ith used punched cards as data storage =E2=80=94 each card held facts about= one person. The cards were records.

    This d= istinction =E2=80=94 program versus data =E2=80=94 maps onto the distinctio= n Ada Lovelace made when noting that the Analytical Engine used separate op= eration cards and variable cards. But Hollerith demonstrated it at massive = scale: millions of cards, each holding a structured record, processed autom= atically.

    He als= o demonstrated something about the economics of data: when you encode data = in a physical medium that machines can read, the cost of processing that da= ta scales with machine speed rather than human labor. Human labor costs inc= rease with quantity =E2=80=94 more data means more clerks means more cost. = Machine processing costs are largely fixed =E2=80=94 the machine costs the = same whether it processes a thousand cards or a million. The per-record cos= t of machine processing falls as volume increases.

    This i= s the economic argument for computing at every scale: fixed infrastructure = cost, falling per-unit processing cost, improving economics as volume grows= . It is the same argument that drives cloud computing today. Hollerith demo= nstrated it first.

    The connection t= o Boole and Shannon =E2=80=94 completing the circuit

    There = is a direct conceptual line connecting Boole's Boolean algebra to Hollerith= 's mercury cups.

    Each H= ollerith card hole is a bit =E2=80=94 1 (hole present) or 0 (hole absent).<= /p>

    Each p= in is a sensor =E2=80=94 it reads one bit.

    Each c= ompleted circuit is a logical TRUE =E2=80=94 the condition "hole present at= this position" is true.

    Each c= ounter advancing is the execution of an operation conditional on a Boolean = value =E2=80=94 if this bit is 1, increment this counter.

    The en= tire Hollerith system is Boolean logic made physical, 47 years before Shann= on formally proved that Boolean algebra maps to electrical circuits.

    Holler= ith did not know he was implementing Boolean algebra. He was building a pra= ctical machine to solve a practical problem. But the structure of what he b= uilt =E2=80=94 binary data encoding, electrical sensing producing binary si= gnals, conditional counting based on binary values =E2=80=94 is exactly wha= t Boolean algebra describes.

    This i= s how technology often works: the mathematical theory and the practical imp= lementation develop on parallel tracks, sometimes for decades, before someo= ne =E2=80=94 Shannon =E2=80=94 recognizes that they are describing the same= thing.

    What this = adds to the story

    The ta= lly bone gave us encoding.

    The cl= ay tablet gave us structured persistent storage.

    The ab= acus gave us assisted processing.

    The An= tikythera Mechanism gave us autonomous fixed computation.<= /p>

    Al-Khw= arizmi gave us the algorithm as a separable concept.

    Al-Jaz= ari gave us programmability.

    Pascal= gave us general mechanical addition.

    Leibni= z gave us complete arithmetic and binary.

    Jacqua= rd gave us stored portable programs.

    The Di= fference Engine gave us proof of reliable automatic computation.

    The An= alytical Engine gave us the complete architecture of the general-pu= rpose programmable computer.

    Ada Lo= velace gave us the first program and the symbolic manipulation insi= ght.

    Boole = gave us the mathematical bridge between logic and physical circuits= .

    Holler= ith gives us three things:

    First,= the first large-scale demonstration that electro-mechanical data p= rocessing works =E2=80=94 at the scale of an entire national popul= ation, producing results faster and more accurately than any human method.<= /p>

    Second= , the punched card as a data record =E2=80=94 not just a p= rogram (Jacquard) but a data storage medium, each card a structured record = of facts about a real-world entity.

    Third,= the commercial data processing industry =E2=80=94 the dem= onstration that information processing by machine has enormous economic val= ue, which created the market that IBM would dominate, which funded the deve= lopment of modern computing.

    One se= ction remains before the electronic age begins. Turing =E2=80=94 who will d= efine what computation is, prove what it cannot do, and give the electronic= computer its theoretical foundation.

    =EE= =84=9D

    You said: Go ahead!

    =

    Go ahead!

    <= /div>
    Jun= 8
    <= button type=3D"button" data-cds=3D"Button" class=3D"cds-reset group/btn rel= ative isolate inline-flex shrink-0 items-center justify-center gap-1.5 whit= espace-nowrap select-none cursor-[var(--cds-cursor-interactive)] aria-disab= led:cursor-default data-[disabled]:cursor-default border-0 outline-none rou= nded h-control font-sans text-body font-medium [&:disabled:not([aria-bu= sy])]:opacity-50 disabled:pointer-events-none transition-shadow duration-fa= st focus-visible:shadow-focus text-primary aria-pressed:text-accent aspect-= square w-control px-0 !text-muted hover:!text-primary" aria-label=3D"Copy" = data-testid=3D"action-bar-copy" id=3D"base-ui-_r_o1_">=EE=81= =96=EE=80=BB<= div class=3D"flex items-center">
    <= /div>

    Clau= de responded: Turing's Universal Machine =E2=80=94 1936=E2=80=931937

    Turing's Univ= ersal Machine =E2=80=94 1936=E2=80=931937

    The sectio= n text

    "A= thought experiment with a tape and rules. The theoretical foundation of al= l modern computers."

    And th= e expanded detail:

    "A= lan Turing's 1936 paper On Computable Numbers was not about building a comp= uter =E2=80=94 it was about answering Hilbert's Entscheidungsproblem: is th= ere an algorithm that can determine if any mathematical statement is provab= le? To answer this, Turing imagined the simplest possible computing device:= an infinite tape of cells (each 0 or 1), a read/write head, a state regist= er, and a lookup table of rules. This 'Turing Machine' could, in principle,= compute anything computable =E2=80=94 it captured the essence of computati= on itself. He proved that no such machine could solve the halting problem (= you can't always predict if a program will stop) =E2=80=94 meaning some pro= blems are fundamentally incomputable. As a byproduct, he gave us the formal= definition of algorithm and computation. Shannon's 1937 thesis then connec= ted Boolean algebra to circuits. The theoretical and physical foundations w= ere now both in place."

    Every term= and word, explained

    Alan Turing =E2=80=94 An English mathematician, logician, crypta= nalyst, and theoretical computer scientist, born 1912 in London, died 1954 = at age 41. One of the most important intellectual figures of the 20th centu= ry. His 1936 paper founded theoretical computer science. During World War I= I he led the codebreaking effort at Bletchley Park that cracked the German = Enigma cipher =E2=80=94 work that historians credit with shortening the war= by two to four years and saving millions of lives. After the war he worked= on early electronic computers, proposed the Turing Test for machine intell= igence, and did foundational work in mathematical biology. He was prosecute= d by the British government in 1952 for homosexuality =E2=80=94 then a crim= inal offense in Britain =E2=80=94 chemically castrated as a condition of av= oiding prison, and died two years later from cyanide poisoning in circumsta= nces officially ruled a suicide. In 2013, he received a royal pardon. In 20= 21, his face was placed on the British fifty-pound banknote.

    Thought experiment =E2=80=94 An experiment conducted entirely in= the imagination rather than in physical reality. You imagine a scenario, a= pply logical reasoning to it, and derive conclusions =E2=80=94 without buil= ding anything or measuring anything. Thought experiments have been central = to physics and mathematics throughout history. Einstein famously used thoug= ht experiments to develop relativity. Turing's Turing Machine is a thought = experiment =E2=80=94 an imagined device used to derive mathematical conclus= ions about computation.

    Tape =E2=80=94 A long, narrow strip of material =E2=80=94 here, = imagined as infinitely long, divided into cells like the squares of a ruled= notebook. Each cell holds one symbol. The tape is both the input medium (d= ata is written on it before computation begins), the working memory (the ma= chine writes intermediate results on it during computation), and the output= medium (the final result is written on it when computation ends). The tape= is infinite in both directions =E2=80=94 it never runs out of space.

    Cells =E2=80=94 Individual squares or units on the tape. Each ce= ll holds exactly one symbol at a time. In the simplest version of the Turin= g Machine, cells hold either 0 or 1 =E2=80=94 binary. In more general versi= ons, cells can hold any symbol from a finite alphabet.

    Read/write head =E2=80=94 A device positioned over the tape that= can perform two operations: read the symbol currently in the cell beneath = it, or write a new symbol into that cell, replacing whatever was there. The= head can also move left or right along the tape, one cell at a time. The h= ead is the machine's only point of contact with the tape.

    State register =E2=80=94 A component of the machine that holds i= ts current state =E2=80=94 a label indicating what mode or condition the ma= chine is currently in. The state is chosen from a finite set of possible st= ates. The state determines how the machine responds to what it reads from t= he tape. Think of state as the machine's current context =E2=80=94 what it = is in the middle of doing.

    State =E2=80=94 A label representing the machine's current inter= nal condition. States are usually given names or numbers =E2=80=94 state 1,= state 2, state 3, and so on, up to some finite number. The state, combined= with the symbol currently being read from the tape, together determine wha= t the machine does next.

    Lookup table =E2=80=94 A table that maps inputs to outputs =E2= =80=94 given a specific combination of inputs, the table specifies the corr= esponding output. You look up your input in the table and read off the outp= ut. No computation required =E2=80=94 just look it up. Turing's machine use= d a lookup table mapping (current state, symbol read) pairs to (symbol to w= rite, direction to move, new state) triples.

    Rules =E2=80=94 The entries in the lookup table. Each rule says:= IF the machine is in state X AND reads symbol Y from the tape, THEN write = symbol Z, move left or right, AND transition to state W. The complete set o= f rules defines the machine's behavior for every possible situation.

    Computable =E2=80=94 Capable of being computed by a mechanical p= rocedure =E2=80=94 a finite, definite sequence of steps that terminates wit= h a result. A number is computable if there exists an algorithm that, given= enough time and tape, will calculate it exactly. A function is computable = if there exists an algorithm that, given any valid input, will produce the = correct output in finite time.

    Essence =E2=80=94 The fundamental, irreducible nature of somethi= ng =E2=80=94 what it is at its most basic, stripped of all non-essential de= tails. The Turing Machine captures the essence of computation by being the = simplest possible model that still retains every capability that computatio= n requires.

    Hilbert =E2=80=94 David Hilbert, a German mathematician, born 18= 62, died 1943. One of the most influential mathematicians of the late 19th = and early 20th centuries. In 1900 he presented a list of 23 unsolved mathem= atical problems that he believed were the most important for mathematics to= solve in the 20th century. Several of these shaped the course of mathemati= cs for decades. His tenth problem =E2=80=94 and the related Entscheidungspr= oblem =E2=80=94 directly motivated Turing's work.

    Entscheidungsproblem =E2=80=94 German for "decision problem." Pr= onounced roughly: ent-SHIGH-dungs-pro-blem. The problem Hilbert posed in 19= 28: is there a general algorithm =E2=80=94 a mechanical procedure =E2=80=94= that can take any mathematical statement as input and decide, in finite ti= me, whether that statement is provable from the axioms of mathematics? In o= ther words: can mathematics itself be mechanized? Can a machine determine m= athematical truth?

    Provable =E2=80=94 Capable of being established as necessarily t= rue by a valid chain of logical reasoning from accepted starting points (ax= ioms). In mathematics, a statement is provable if you can construct a valid= proof of it =E2=80=94 a finite sequence of logical steps, each following f= rom previous steps or from axioms, leading to the statement as a conclusion= .

    Halting problem =E2=80=94 The question of whether, given any pro= gram and any input, it is possible to determine in advance =E2=80=94 by a g= eneral algorithm =E2=80=94 whether that program will eventually stop runnin= g or will run forever. Turing proved this is impossible: no general algorit= hm can solve the halting problem for all possible programs.

    Incomputable =E2=80=94 Not capable of being computed by any algo= rithm. Some problems are incomputable =E2=80=94 no algorithm can solve them= , not because we have not yet found one, but because it can be proved mathe= matically that no such algorithm exists or ever could exist.

    Formal definition =E2=80=94 A definition that is mathematically = precise and unambiguous =E2=80=94 stated in terms of exactly defined concep= ts with no room for interpretation. Before Turing, "algorithm" and "computa= tion" were intuitive concepts =E2=80=94 everyone had a sense of what they m= eant but there was no rigorous mathematical definition. Turing provided for= mal definitions =E2=80=94 precise enough to use in mathematical proofs.

    Cryptanalyst =E2=80=94 A person who analyzes and breaks codes an= d ciphers =E2=80=94 who figures out how to read encrypted messages without = having the key. From Greek kryptos (hidden) + analysis (b= reaking down).

    Enigma =E2=80=94 A series of electro-mechanical cipher machines = used by Nazi Germany to encrypt military communications during World War II= . Enigma produced ciphertext =E2=80=94 scrambled messages =E2=80=94 that we= re considered unbreakable. Turing's mathematical insights were central to d= eveloping methods that could decrypt Enigma messages, giving the Allied for= ces the ability to read German military communications in near-real-time.

    Bletchley Park =E2=80=94 A country estate in Buckinghamshire, En= gland, that served as the main British codebreaking center during World War= II. Thousands of people worked there, including mathematicians, linguists,= chess champions, and crossword puzzle enthusiasts. Turing was one of its c= entral figures.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    Where we are in = the story

    Holler= ith demonstrated that electro-mechanical data processing works at scale. Th= e technology path from Hollerith to electronic computers is now visible =E2= =80=94 bigger machines, faster switching, more complex circuits.

    But th= ere is a foundational question that has not been answered =E2=80=94 a quest= ion that lurks beneath all the mechanical and electrical ingenuity we have = been accumulating:

    What, precisely, is computation? What can, in principle, be computed? Wha= t cannot?

    These = questions matter enormously. Building faster and faster machines is useful.= But if you do not know what computation is =E2=80=94 formally, precisely, = mathematically =E2=80=94 you cannot reason about what machines can and cann= ot do. You cannot prove that a machine is doing what you intend. You cannot= determine whether a problem is solvable at all, or whether you are wasting= effort trying to solve something that cannot be solved.

    Turing= 's 1936 paper answers these questions. It does not answer them by building = a machine. It answers them by thinking =E2=80=94 by a sustained, rigorous, = mathematical thought experiment that defines computation precisely and deri= ves consequences from that definition.

    The co= nsequences include some of the most important and surprising results in all= of mathematics.

    The mathematical= context =E2=80=94 what Hilbert wanted

    To und= erstand why Turing wrote what he wrote, you need to understand the mathemat= ical crisis of the early 20th century.

    In the= late 19th and early 20th centuries, mathematicians discovered that mathema= tics had a problem. Several paradoxes =E2=80=94 most famously Bertrand Russ= ell's paradox in 1901 =E2=80=94 revealed that naive set theory, which was t= he foundation of most mathematics, was inconsistent. You could derive contr= adictions from seemingly reasonable assumptions.

    This w= as genuinely alarming. If mathematics was inconsistent =E2=80=94 if you cou= ld prove both a statement and its opposite =E2=80=94 then mathematics prove= d nothing. Every result was suspect.

    David = Hilbert's response was ambitious: rebuild mathematics from scratch on an un= shakeable foundation. He proposed that mathematics should be:

    Complete =E2=80=94 Every true mathematical statement should be p= rovable from the axioms.
    Consistent =E2=80=94 No contradiction should be derivable = from the axioms.
    Decidable =E2=80=94 There should be a mechanical procedure= =E2=80=94 an algorithm =E2=80=94 that could determine, for any mathematica= l statement, whether it is provable or not.

    The th= ird property =E2=80=94 decidability =E2=80=94 is the Entscheidungsproblem. = Hilbert believed all three properties could be established. He was optimist= ic about the foundations of mathematics.

    He was= wrong on all three counts.

    Kurt G=C3=B6del, an Austrian mathematician, destroyed the first = two in 1931 with his incompleteness theorems:

    • = First incompleteness theorem: In any consistent mathematic= al system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic, there exist true sta= tements that cannot be proved within the system. Mathematics is necessarily= incomplete =E2=80=94 there are mathematical truths that no proof can reach= .
    • = Second incompleteness theorem: No consistent mathematical = system can prove its own consistency. You cannot establish that mathematics= is free of contradictions using only mathematics itself.

    G=C3= =B6del's results were devastating to Hilbert's program. But the third quest= ion =E2=80=94 decidability =E2=80=94 remained open in 1931. Could there at = least be an algorithm that decides provability?

    This i= s the question Turing took on in 1936. And to answer it, he first had to an= swer a prior question: what is an algorithm, formally?

    The prior questi= on =E2=80=94 what is an algorithm, precisely?

    We hav= e used the word "algorithm" since Al-Khwarizmi. We have a good intuitive se= nse of what it means: a finite, ordered sequence of steps that solves a pro= blem.

    But "f= inite, ordered sequence of steps" is not precise enough for mathematical pu= rposes. What counts as a "step"? Could a single step be infinitely complex?= Could there be steps that require human judgment? Could there be steps tha= t themselves invoke other algorithms of arbitrary complexity?

    If you= want to prove theorems about algorithms =E2=80=94 what they can and cannot= compute =E2=80=94 you need a mathematically rigorous definition. A definit= ion precise enough that you can reason about all possible algorithms, not j= ust specific ones.

    Turing= 's approach was to ask: what does a human computer actually do when they co= mpute?

    He ana= lyzed the process of a human following an algorithm =E2=80=94 someone sitti= ng with paper, pencil, and a written set of instructions, working through a= computation step by step. He identified the minimal set of atomic operatio= ns that such a person performs:

    1. = Read one symbol from the current position on the paper
    2. = Depending on what was read and the current mental state, write a symbol (or= erase and write nothing)
    3. = Move attention one position left or right on the paper
    4. = Update the mental state

    That i= s all. Every computation a human can do can be decomposed into these four a= tomic operations, applied repeatedly. Nothing more is needed.

    This a= nalysis is not obvious =E2=80=94 it required careful thought to convince on= eself that these four operations are truly sufficient. Turing argued it car= efully. The key insights:

    • = A human computer works on one symbol at a time =E2=80=94 they do not percei= ve the entire paper at once
    • = The mental state is finite =E2=80=94 a human can only be in a finite number= of distinct states of mind relevant to the computation
    • = The rules for what to do in each state are finite =E2=80=94 there are finit= ely many possible (state, symbol) combinations
    • = Every step is entirely determined by the current state and the current symb= ol =E2=80=94 no randomness, no external input

    From t= hese observations, Turing abstracted the Turing Machine.

    The Turing Machi= ne =E2=80=94 the formal model

    The Tu= ring Machine is not a physical machine. It is a mathematical object =E2=80= =94 defined abstractly, existing only on paper.

    A Turi= ng Machine consists of:

    1. An infinite tape divided into cells, each holding one symbol = from a finite alphabet. In the simplest binary case, the alphabet is {0, 1,= blank}. The tape can be read and written. It is infinite =E2=80=94 the mac= hine never runs out of space.

    2. A read/write head that is always positioned over exactly one = cell of the tape. At each step, the head reads the symbol in the current ce= ll. It can also write a new symbol to that cell. After reading and writing,= it moves one cell left or one cell right.

    3. A state register holding the machine's current state =E2=80= =94 a label from a finite set of states. Two states are special: the start = state (where computation begins) and the halt state (where computation ends= ). When the machine enters the halt state, it stops.

    4. A transition function =E2=80=94 the complete set of rules. Fo= r every possible combination of (current state, symbol read), the transitio= n function specifies:

    • = What symbol to write in the current cell (replacing what was there)
    • = Which direction to move the head (left or right)
    • = What state to transition to next

    The tr= ansition function is the program. A specific Turing Machine is defined enti= rely by its transition function =E2=80=94 different transition functions co= mpute different things.

    Operat= ion is purely mechanical:

    1. = Read the symbol under the head
    2. = Look up (current state, symbol read) in the transition function
    3. = Write the specified symbol
    4. = Move the head in the specified direction
    5. = Transition to the specified new state
    6. = Repeat from step 1
    7. = Stop when the halt state is reached

    No int= erpretation. No judgment. No ambiguity. At every step, exactly one rule app= lies, and it fully specifies what happens next.

    A simple example= =E2=80=94 a Turing Machine that adds one

    Let us= trace through a concrete Turing Machine to make this tangible. We will bui= ld one that takes a binary number on the tape and adds 1 to it.

    Binary= addition of 1 works from right to left:

    • = If the rightmost bit is 0, change it to 1 and stop
    • = If the rightmost bit is 1, change it to 0 and carry =E2=80=94 move left and= repeat

    So to = add 1 to 0110 (binary for 6) we should get 0111 (binary for 7).
    To add 1 to 0111 (binary for 7) we should get 1000 (binary for 8) =E2=80=94= the carry propagates through three positions.

    States: START, CARRY, DONE

    Transition rules:

    ANOT A
    01
    10
    Current StateSymbol ReadWriteMoveNe= w State
    START00RightSTART
    START11RightSTART
    STARTblankblankLeftC= ARRY
    CARRY10L= eftCARRY
    CARRY0= 1LeftDONE
    CARRY= blank1LeftDONE
    = DONEanythingsame=E2=80=94Trace for 0110 + 1:

    Start:= tape =3D ...0110..., head on first 0, state =3D START

    • = Read 0, state START =E2=86=92 write 0, move right, stay START
    • = Read 1, state START =E2=86=92 write 1, move right, stay START
    • = Read 1, state START =E2=86=92 write 1, move right, stay START
    • = Read 0, state START =E2=86=92 write 0, move right, stay START
    • = Read blank, state START =E2=86=92 write blank, move left, =E2=86=92 CARRY

    Now in= CARRY state, head on rightmost digit (0):

    • = Read 0, state CARRY =E2=86=92 write 1, move left, =E2=86=92 DONE

    Now DO= NE =E2=86=92 HALT

    Final = tape: 0111. Correct =E2=80=94 0110 + 1 =3D 0111 =3D 7.

    Trace for 0111 + 1 (with carry propagation):

    After = the START phase, head is on rightmost digit (1), state =3D CARRY:

    • = Read 1, state CARRY =E2=86=92 write 0, move left, stay CARRY
    • = Read 1, state CARRY =E2=86=92 write 0, move left, stay CARRY
    • = Read 1, state CARRY =E2=86=92 write 0, move left, stay CARRY
    • = Read 0, state CARRY =E2=86=92 write 1, move left, =E2=86=92 DONE =E2=86=92 = HALT

    Final = tape: 1000. Correct =E2=80=94 0111 + 1 =3D 1000 =3D 8.

    This t= iny machine =E2=80=94 three states, a handful of rules =E2=80=94 correctly = adds 1 to any binary number of any length, handling all possible carry casc= ades automatically. The machine does not know how long the number is. It do= es not know how many carries there will be. The rules handle all cases.

    The Universal Tu= ring Machine =E2=80=94 the concept of the stored program

    Here i= s where the Turing Machine becomes not just a theoretical tool but the blue= print for the modern computer.

    Turing= showed that it is possible to build a special Turing Machine =E2=80=94 cal= led the Universal Turing Machine =E2=80=94 that can simulate any other Turi= ng Machine.

    How? T= he Universal Turing Machine's tape contains two things:

    1. = A description of the Turing Machine to be simulated =E2=80=94 its states, a= lphabet, and transition rules, encoded as a sequence of symbols
    2. = The input data for that Turing Machine to process

    The Un= iversal Turing Machine reads the description of the machine to simulate, re= ads the input data, and then carries out exactly the steps that the describ= ed machine would carry out =E2=80=94 using its own tape as working space.

    The Un= iversal Turing Machine is one fixed machine that can run any program. The p= rogram is just data on its tape.

    This i= s the stored-program concept =E2=80=94 the architecture of every modern com= puter:

    • = The hardware (the Universal Turing Machine) is fixed and general
    • = The program (the description of another Turing Machine) is data stored in m= emory
    • = Different programs make the same hardware do different things
    • = Programs can be loaded, changed, and replaced without touching the hardware=

    John v= on Neumann, designing the architecture for one of the first electronic comp= uters in 1945, arrived at the same concept =E2=80=94 CPU plus stored progra= m in RAM =E2=80=94 through his own analysis of what a computing machine nee= ded. But the idea had been formally established by Turing's Universal Turin= g Machine in 1936.

    The vo= n Neumann architecture =E2=80=94 the architecture of essentially every comp= uter built since =E2=80=94 is the physical implementation of the Universal = Turing Machine.

    The halting prob= lem =E2=80=94 what computation cannot do

    This i= s one of the most profound results in all of mathematics. And it comes from= a technique called diagonalization =E2=80=94 a clever sel= f-referential argument.

    Stating the problem precisely

    The ha= lting problem asks: is there a general algorithm H that, given any program = P and any input I, can determine =E2=80=94 in finite time, by examining P a= nd I =E2=80=94 whether P will eventually halt (stop running) when given inp= ut I, or whether P will run forever?

    H must= work for all possible programs and all possible inputs. It cannot just try= running P and see what happens =E2=80=94 that would take forever if P runs= forever.

    Why we want such an algorithm

    A halt= ing detector would be enormously useful. Programmers spend enormous effort = trying to determine whether their programs will terminate correctly. Compil= ers and debugging tools would benefit from knowing whether any given code p= ath would run forever. Many unsolved mathematical problems could be reduced= to halting questions.

    Why it cannot exist =E2=80=94 the proof

    Turing= proved no such algorithm H can exist. The proof is elegant and self-refere= ntial.

    Assume= , for contradiction, that H exists =E2=80=94 a program that correctly decid= es the halting problem for all programs and inputs.

    Now co= nstruct a new program D:

    D take= s any program P as input. D does the following:

    1. = Run H on (P, P) =E2=80=94 ask H: does program P halt when given itself as i= nput?
    2. = If H says P halts on input P: loop forever =E2=80=94 run an infinite loop a= nd never halt
    3. = If H says P does not halt on input P: halt immediately

    D is a= valid program =E2=80=94 we can construct it using H.

    Now as= k: what happens when you run D on input D? =E2=80=94 when D is given itself= as input?

    Case 1: Suppose H says D halts on input D.
    Then by step 2, D loops forever on input D.
    But H said D halts. Contradiction.

    Case 2: Suppose H says D does not halt on input D.
    Then by step 3, D halts immediately on input D.
    But H said D does not halt. Contradiction.

    Both c= ases produce a contradiction. The only way to avoid contradiction is if H d= oes not exist =E2=80=94 no program can correctly solve the halting problem = for all programs and inputs.

    The pr= oof is self-referential: it works by constructing a program that does the o= pposite of what H predicts about it. H cannot predict its own behavior cons= istently. No algorithm can.

    Why the halting = problem matters =E2=80=94 the deeper consequences

    The ha= lting problem is not just a curiosity. It is the source of an entire family= of results showing that specific, important problems cannot be solved algo= rithmically.

    Undecidability is everywhere in mathematics =E2=80=94 G=C3=B6del= 's incompleteness theorems (1931) are closely related. G=C3=B6del showed th= at some mathematical statements cannot be proved. Turing showed that some q= uestions about programs cannot be answered. They are both instances of the = same phenomenon: self-reference producing undecidability.

    Many program analysis questions are undecidable =E2=80=94 Does t= his program ever produce output? Does this program reach this specific line= of code? Are these two programs equivalent? All of these reduce to the hal= ting problem and are therefore undecidable in general. This is why perfect = automated program verification =E2=80=94 a tool that could check any progra= m for any bug =E2=80=94 is impossible in principle, not just in practice.

    The limits of automation =E2=80=94 The Entscheidungsproblem aske= d: can mathematics be automated? Hilbert believed yes. Turing answered no = =E2=80=94 not completely. There are mathematical questions that no algorith= m can answer. Mathematics is not fully mechanizable. Human mathematical ins= ight =E2=80=94 creative leaps that go beyond mechanical rule-following =E2= =80=94 is genuinely necessary.

    What computers cannot do =E2=80=94 Every computer built is a phy= sical implementation of a Turing Machine =E2=80=94 or more precisely, of a = bounded version of one (with finite rather than infinite memory). Turing's = undecidability results therefore apply to all computers. There are problems= that no computer can solve, regardless of how fast it is or how much memor= y it has. Speed and memory are irrelevant when the problem is undecidable.<= /p>

    Church's thesis = =E2=80=94 the universality claim

    Around= the same time as Turing, Alonzo Church =E2=80=94 an American mathematician= at Princeton =E2=80=94 independently arrived at a different but equivalent= formal model of computation: the lambda calculus. Church also proved the u= ndecidability of the Entscheidungsproblem using his model.

    Turing= and Church then showed that their two models =E2=80=94 Turing Machines and= lambda calculus =E2=80=94 were equivalent: anything computable by one was = computable by the other.

    This l= ed to what is now called the Church-Turing thesis: every f= unction that is intuitively computable =E2=80=94 that could be computed by = any mechanical procedure, by any machine, by any algorithm =E2=80=94 is com= putable by a Turing Machine.

    The Ch= urch-Turing thesis is not a mathematical theorem =E2=80=94 it cannot be pro= ved, because "intuitively computable" is not a formal concept. It is a clai= m about the relationship between a formal model and an informal notion.

    But it= has never been falsified. Every model of computation that has ever been pr= oposed =E2=80=94 lambda calculus, recursive functions, register machines, c= ellular automata, quantum computers, DNA computers =E2=80=94 has turned out= to be equivalent to Turing Machines in computational power. None has been = shown to compute anything that a Turing Machine cannot.

    The Ch= urch-Turing thesis is, practically speaking, the claim that Turing Machines= are the right model of computation =E2=80=94 that Turing captured the esse= nce of what computation is, completely and correctly.

    Turing's other c= ontributions =E2=80=94 briefly

    The 19= 36 paper is Turing's most fundamental contribution, but his life produced s= everal others that deserve mention.

    Codebreaking at Bletchley Park (1939=E2=80=931945) =E2=80=94 Tur= ing's work breaking the German Enigma cipher was his most practically conse= quential contribution. The Enigma machine produced ciphertext by passing ea= ch letter through a series of rotating scrambler wheels =E2=80=94 with the = settings changed daily, producing a different cipher each day. Turing devel= oped the Bombe =E2=80=94 an electro-mechanical device that exploited weakne= sses in Enigma operation to search through possible settings and find the d= aily key. The intelligence produced =E2=80=94 codenamed Ultra =E2=80=94 gav= e the Allies a profound advantage. Some historians argue it was the decisiv= e factor in the Allied victory in the Atlantic.

    Early computer design (1945=E2=80=931948) =E2=80=94 After the wa= r, Turing worked on the design of actual electronic computers. He produced = a detailed design for the Automatic Computing Engine (ACE) for the National= Physical Laboratory, and later worked at Manchester University on the Manc= hester Mark 1 =E2=80=94 one of the first stored-program electronic computer= s.

    The Turing Test and machine intelligence (1950) =E2=80=94 In a 1= 950 paper titled Computing Machinery and Intelligence, Turing aske= d the question "Can machines think?" He proposed an operational test for ma= chine intelligence =E2=80=94 now called the Turing Test =E2=80=94 in which = a machine is considered intelligent if its responses in a text conversation= cannot be distinguished from a human's responses. He discussed and respond= ed to objections =E2=80=94 including, explicitly, Ada Lovelace's objection = that machines can only do what they are programmed to do. This paper founde= d the field of artificial intelligence as a research discipline.

    Mathematical biology (1952) =E2=80=94 Turing wrote a paper on mo= rphogenesis =E2=80=94 how biological organisms develop their patterns and s= hapes. He proposed that reaction-diffusion equations =E2=80=94 systems of d= ifferential equations describing how chemicals spread and interact =E2=80= =94 could explain how animals develop patterns like stripes and spots. This= paper has proved deeply influential in developmental biology.

    The tragedy =E2= =80=94 and the injustice

    Turing= 's personal story is inseparable from his historical significance because t= he circumstances of his death represent one of the most unjust episodes in = the history of science.

    In Jan= uary 1952, Turing reported a burglary at his home to the police. During the= investigation, he disclosed that he had been in a relationship with a man.= In 1952 Britain, homosexuality was a criminal offense. Turing was arrested= , tried, and convicted of "gross indecency."

    He was= given a choice: prison or chemical castration =E2=80=94 a course of inject= ions of synthetic estrogen intended to suppress sexual drive. He chose the = injections to avoid prison, reportedly because he wanted to continue his re= search.

    On Jun= e 7, 1954, Turing was found dead. A half-eaten apple was beside him. Cyanid= e poisoning was identified as the cause. The inquest ruled suicide. Some hi= storians dispute this =E2=80=94 his mother believed it was accidental, the = result of casual chemical experiments he conducted at home. The truth is un= known.

    He was= 41 years old.

    The ma= n who had perhaps done more than anyone else to help Britain win World War = II =E2=80=94 whose mathematical insights had broken the Nazi codes that pro= tected their most sensitive military communications =E2=80=94 was chemicall= y mutilated and driven to death by the government he had served.

    In 200= 9, British Prime Minister Gordon Brown issued a formal apology on behalf of= the government. In 2013, Queen Elizabeth II granted him a royal pardon. In= 2021, his face was placed on the fifty-pound note.

    These = gestures are meaningful. They are also 60 years too late.

    Shannon's 1937 t= hesis =E2=80=94 the physical foundation completes

    The se= ction text notes that Shannon's 1937 thesis =E2=80=94 connecting Boolean al= gebra to electrical circuits =E2=80=94 appeared at approximately the same t= ime as Turing's 1936 paper.

    This j= uxtaposition is not accidental. Together, these two works established the c= omplete theoretical and physical foundation for electronic computing:

    Turing (1936): Defined computation formally. Established what ca= n and cannot be computed. Showed that a universal computing machine is poss= ible =E2=80=94 a machine that can run any program. Provided the theoretical= architecture: stored program, sequential instruction execution, conditiona= l branching, unbounded memory.

    Shannon (1937): Showed that Boolean algebra =E2=80=94 the algebr= a of true/false logic =E2=80=94 maps directly onto electrical switching cir= cuits. Provided the physical implementation path: any logical or arithmetic= computation can be built from transistors or relay switches arranged to im= plement Boolean logic gates.

    Turing= answered: what is computation, and what does a computing machine need to d= o?

    Shanno= n answered: how do you build it in electronics?

    Betwee= n them, the path from abstract computation to physical machine was fully ma= pped. What remained was engineering =E2=80=94 actually building the machine= s, which the 1940s delivered.

    Turing completen= ess =E2=80=94 closing the loop with Babbage

    We int= roduced the term Turing-complete when discussing the Analytical Engine. Now= we can understand it fully.

    A syst= em is Turing-complete if it can simulate any Turing Machine. This means it = can compute anything computable.

    The re= quirements, as we now understand them formally:

    • = Arbitrary data storage (the tape =E2=80=94 implemented as RAM)
    • = Read and write to storage (the read/write head =E2=80=94 implemented as mem= ory load/store instructions)
    • = Conditional branching (the transition function's ability to go to different= states based on what is read =E2=80=94 implemented as if/branch instructio= ns)
    • = The ability to loop =E2=80=94 repeat operations =E2=80=94 (the machine runs= rule after rule until it halts =E2=80=94 implemented as jump instructions)=
    • = In principle, unbounded memory (real computers are bounded, but the bound i= s large enough for practical purposes)

    Babbag= e's Analytical Engine, as Ada Lovelace described it in 1843, had all of the= se. It was Turing-complete before Turing defined what that meant.

    Every = computer built since =E2=80=94 every smartphone, every server, every embedd= ed chip in a microwave =E2=80=94 is also Turing-complete. They can all comp= ute the same set of things. They differ in speed, memory size, and power co= nsumption =E2=80=94 but not in what they can compute.

    A comp= utation that can be done on a 1940s ENIAC can, in principle, be done on a T= uring Machine made of paper and pencil. The difference is that the ENIAC mi= ght take seconds and the paper-and-pencil machine might take longer than th= e age of the universe. Speed and memory matter enormously in practice. In p= rinciple =E2=80=94 in the mathematical sense Turing was working in =E2=80= =94 they are all the same machine.

    What this = adds to the story

    The ta= lly bone gave us encoding.

    The cl= ay tablet gave us structured persistent storage.

    The ab= acus gave us assisted processing.

    The An= tikythera Mechanism gave us autonomous fixed computation.<= /p>

    Al-Khw= arizmi gave us the algorithm as a separable concept.

    Al-Jaz= ari gave us programmability.

    Pascal= gave us general mechanical addition.

    Leibni= z gave us complete arithmetic and binary.

    Jacqua= rd gave us stored portable programs.

    The Di= fference Engine gave us proof of reliable automatic computation.

    The An= alytical Engine gave us the complete architecture of the general-pu= rpose programmable computer.

    Ada Lo= velace gave us the first program and the symbolic manipulation insi= ght.

    Boole = gave us the mathematical bridge between logic and physical circuits= .

    Holler= ith gave us large-scale electro-mechanical data processing and the = commercial computing industry.

    Turing= gives us three things of incomparable importance:

    First,= the formal definition of computation =E2=80=94 what an al= gorithm is, precisely, and what computation means, mathematically. For the = first time, "computable" has an exact, rigorous meaning.

    Second= , the Universal Turing Machine =E2=80=94 the theoretical p= roof that a single general machine can run any program, given that program = as input data. The stored-program computer is not just an engineering idea = =E2=80=94 it is a mathematical necessity, the only architecture that achiev= es universality.

    Third,= the limits of computation =E2=80=94 the halting problem a= nd undecidability. Some problems cannot be solved by any algorithm. Mathema= tics is not fully mechanizable. These limits are not engineering failures = =E2=80=94 they are mathematical truths about the nature of computation itse= lf.

    The th= eoretical foundation is complete. The physical foundation =E2=80=94 Boolean= algebra mapping to electrical circuits =E2=80=94 is complete via Shannon. = The conceptual lineage from tally bone to universal computer is complete.

    One se= ction remains: the first electronic computers. The moment when all of this = =E2=80=94 45,000 years of accumulated insight =E2=80=94 finally converges i= nto a machine that actually runs. The age of gears ends. The age of electro= ns begins.

    =EE=83=B9
    <= /div>

    You said: Go ahead!

    Go ahead!

    Jun 9

    Claude responded: The First Electron= ic Computers =E2=80=94 1937=E2=80=931945

    The First Electronic Computers =E2=80=94 = 1937=E2=80=931945

    The sectio= n text

    "W= ar drives it home. Vacuum tubes replace gears. Colossus, ENIAC, and the dig= ital age begin."

    And th= e expanded detail:

    "W= orld War II created urgent computational problems =E2=80=94 codebreaking, b= allistic tables, nuclear calculations =E2=80=94 that mechanical computers w= ere too slow to solve. Atanasoff-Berry Computer (1937=E2=80=9342): First el= ectronic digital computer, using vacuum tubes and capacitor memory. Special= -purpose, not programmable. Colossus (1943, UK): Built at Bletchley Park to= break German Lorenz cipher. Programmed by patch panels and switches. The f= irst large-scale electronic computer. ENIAC (1945, USA): 18,000 vacuum tube= s, 30 tons, 1,800 sq ft. First general-purpose electronic computer. Program= med by physically rewiring cables =E2=80=94 the 'program' was the circuit t= opology. Ran 1,000=C3=97 faster than any mechanical calculator."

    Every term= and word, explained

    Vacuum tubes =E2=80=94 Electronic components, also called thermi= onic valves, that were the dominant switching and amplifying technology bef= ore transistors. A vacuum tube is a sealed glass tube from which almost all= air has been removed =E2=80=94 creating a vacuum inside. Inside the tube a= re metal electrodes. When a filament inside is heated by electrical current= , it emits electrons =E2=80=94 a process called thermionic emission. These = electrons travel through the vacuum to other electrodes. By applying small = voltages to a control electrode called the grid, you can control the flow o= f electrons =E2=80=94 turning the current on or off, or amplifying a signal= . In computing, vacuum tubes were used as switches =E2=80=94 on or off, con= ducting or not conducting, 1 or 0. They were large =E2=80=94 roughly the si= ze of a light bulb =E2=80=94 consumed considerable power, generated heat, a= nd failed frequently. But they switched electronically =E2=80=94 thousands = of times per second =E2=80=94 rather than mechanically, making them vastly = faster than relay switches or gear mechanisms.

    Thermionic emission =E2=80=94 The process by which electrons are= released from a metal surface when it is heated to high temperatures. = Thermionic from Greek thermos (heat) + ion (a charge= d particle). When a metal filament inside a vacuum tube is heated by electr= ical current passing through it, the thermal energy gives electrons enough = energy to escape the metal surface. These free electrons can then flow thro= ugh the vacuum inside the tube toward positively charged electrodes.

    Electrodes =E2=80=94 Conductors =E2=80=94 usually metal pieces o= r wires =E2=80=94 through which electrical current enters or leaves a devic= e. In a vacuum tube, there are multiple electrodes with different functions= : the cathode (from which electrons are emitted), the anode or plate (which= attracts electrons), and the grid (which controls electron flow). From Gre= ek elektron (amber, associated with electricity) + hodos = (way, path).

    Grid =E2=80=94 The control electrode in a vacuum tube, typically= a wire mesh or coil placed between the cathode and anode. Small changes in= voltage applied to the grid have a large effect on how many electrons flow= from cathode to anode. This control function is what makes vacuum tubes us= eful as switches and amplifiers.

    Amplifier =E2=80=94 A device that takes a weak signal and produc= es a stronger version of it. An amplifier does not create energy =E2=80=94 = it uses a separate power supply. A small input signal controls the release = of larger amounts of energy from the power supply, producing an output sign= al that is a scaled-up version of the input. Audio amplifiers in a music sy= stem work this way. In computing, amplification was less important than swi= tching =E2=80=94 but the same vacuum tubes could do both.

    Transistors =E2=80=94 Semiconductor devices that replaced vacuum= tubes as the basic switching component in electronics. A transistor is mad= e from semiconductor material =E2=80=94 typically silicon =E2=80=94 and can= act as an electrically controlled switch. Applying a small voltage to one = terminal (the gate or base) controls whether current flows between the othe= r two terminals. Transistors are smaller, faster, more reliable, and consum= e far less power than vacuum tubes. The transistor was invented at Bell Lab= s in 1947 by John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley =E2=80=94 = for which they received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1956.

    Semiconductor =E2=80=94 A material whose electrical conductivity= is between that of a conductor (like copper, which conducts electricity ea= sily) and an insulator (like glass, which does not conduct). Silicon is the= most important semiconductor. The conductivity of a semiconductor can be p= recisely controlled by adding small amounts of other elements =E2=80=94 a p= rocess called doping =E2=80=94 and by applying voltages. This controllabili= ty is what makes semiconductors useful for transistors. Semi from = Latin for half or partial.

    Ballistic tables =E2=80=94 Mathematical tables used by artillery= gunners to aim their weapons accurately. Ballistic relates to the= flight of projectiles =E2=80=94 shells, bullets, missiles. Hitting a targe= t with an artillery shell requires calculating the shell's trajectory =E2= =80=94 the curved path it follows under gravity, air resistance, and wind = =E2=80=94 from the gun position to the target. These calculations depend on= the shell's initial velocity, the angle of the gun, atmospheric conditions= , and the distance to the target. The calculations are complex and must be = done for many different combinations of these variables. The results are co= mpiled into tables =E2=80=94 for each combination of range, elevation, and = conditions, the table gives the correct gun settings. Producing these table= s required enormous amounts of computation.

    Nuclear calculations =E2=80=94 Mathematical calculations require= d to design nuclear weapons =E2=80=94 specifically, to understand the behav= ior of nuclear chain reactions. The Manhattan Project =E2=80=94 the America= n program to develop atomic bombs =E2=80=94 required calculations about neu= tron behavior in fissile materials, the geometry of implosion lenses, and t= he critical mass of uranium and plutonium. These calculations were initiall= y done by teams of human computers and were a driving motivation for the de= velopment of electronic computers.

    Codebreaking =E2=80=94 The process of analyzing encrypted messag= es and finding ways to decrypt them without having the original encryption = key. Codes and ciphers protect communications by transforming messages into= forms that cannot be read without the key. Codebreaking =E2=80=94 also cal= led cryptanalysis =E2=80=94 finds ways to recover the original message desp= ite not having the key, usually by exploiting mathematical weaknesses in th= e encryption method or patterns in the encrypted text.

    Atanasoff-Berry Computer (ABC) =E2=80=94 The first electronic di= gital computer, built between 1937 and 1942 by John Vincent Atanasoff, a pr= ofessor of physics and mathematics at Iowa State College, with assistance f= rom graduate student Clifford Berry. The ABC used vacuum tubes for computat= ion and capacitors for memory. It was designed to solve systems of simultan= eous linear equations =E2=80=94 a specific mathematical task =E2=80=94 and = could not be reprogrammed for other tasks. It was never completed to full w= orking order before Atanasoff left Iowa State for war work.

    John Vincent Atanasoff =E2=80=94 An American physicist and inven= tor, born 1903, died 1995. The son of a Bulgarian immigrant. A professor at= Iowa State College who conceived the basic principles of electronic digita= l computation during a long winter drive in 1937, stopping at a roadhouse i= n Illinois where he sketched his ideas on a napkin. He is the legal invento= r of the electronic digital computer =E2=80=94 a US federal court ruled in = 1973 that the patent on ENIAC was invalid because Atanasoff had disclosed h= is ideas to ENIAC's inventors first.

    Clifford Berry =E2=80=94 Graduate student at Iowa State who work= ed with Atanasoff to actually build the ABC. Berry handled much of the phys= ical construction and engineering. He died in 1963, before the ABC's histor= ical significance was fully recognized.

    Capacitor =E2=80=94 An electronic component that stores electric= al charge =E2=80=94 a small amount of electrical energy =E2=80=94 in the el= ectric field between two conducting surfaces separated by an insulating mat= erial. Capacitors can be charged (storing a 1) or discharged (representing = a 0). They are not as fast as modern memory technologies and their charge l= eaks away over time =E2=80=94 they must be refreshed periodically. The ABC = used capacitors as its memory =E2=80=94 the first use of electronic memory = in a computing device.

    Capacitor memory =E2=80=94 Memory implemented using capacitors. = Because capacitors lose their charge over time, capacitor memory requires p= eriodic refreshing =E2=80=94 reading the stored value and rewriting it befo= re the charge leaks away completely. This refresh requirement is still pres= ent in modern DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) =E2=80=94 the main memory= in your computer. DRAM stores each bit as a charge in a tiny capacitor. Re= freshing happens automatically thousands of times per second.

    Special-purpose =E2=80=94 Designed for one specific task, incapa= ble of being reprogrammed for other tasks. The ABC could only solve systems= of linear equations. It had no general programming capability.

    Colossus =E2=80=94 A series of electronic computing machines bui= lt at Bletchley Park, Britain, during World War II. Two versions were built= : Colossus Mark 1, operational in February 1943, and Colossus Mark 2, opera= tional in June 1944. They were designed specifically to break the Lorenz ci= pher =E2=80=94 a German teleprinter encryption system used for Hitler's hig= h-level communications with his military commanders.

    Lorenz cipher =E2=80=94 A German encryption system used during W= orld War II for the most sensitive communications =E2=80=94 between Hitler = and his field commanders, between German Army Groups, for strategic-level t= raffic. Named after the Lorenz company that manufactured the machines. More= complex than the Enigma cipher and initially considered unbreakable. Break= ing it gave the Allies access to Hitler's own strategic thinking.

    Tommy Flowers =E2=80=94 The British Post Office engineer who des= igned and built Colossus. Born 1905, died 1998. A telephone exchange engine= er with deep knowledge of valve (vacuum tube) technology. He recognized tha= t vacuum tubes, if run continuously without being switched on and off for n= on-operational reasons, were far more reliable than previously believed. Th= is insight made building a large-scale vacuum tube computer feasible. He us= ed his own money =E2=80=94 about =C2=A31,000 =E2=80=94 to fund parts of the= construction when the government was slow to provide resources.

    ENIAC =E2=80=94 Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer. Bu= ilt at the University of Pennsylvania's Moore School of Electrical Engineer= ing by John Eckert and John Mauchly, funded by the US Army. Construction be= gan in 1943, completed in late 1945, publicly unveiled in 1946. The first g= eneral-purpose electronic computer =E2=80=94 programmable for different tas= ks, not limited to one type of computation.

    John Eckert =E2=80=94 American electrical engineer, born 1919, d= ied 1995. The primary hardware designer of ENIAC =E2=80=94 responsible for = making 18,000 vacuum tubes work together reliably. His key insight was that= the reliability of vacuum tubes depended critically on the voltages they o= perated at =E2=80=94 running them at lower voltages than their rated maximu= m dramatically extended their lifespan. He implemented rigorous engineering= practices that kept ENIAC running despite its enormous component count.

    John Mauchly =E2=80=94 American physicist, born 1907, died 1980.= The primary conceptual force behind ENIAC =E2=80=94 he had the vision of a= general-purpose electronic computer for scientific calculations and convin= ced the US Army to fund its construction. He had met Atanasoff before desig= ning ENIAC, which later became the basis of a major patent dispute.

    Patch panels =E2=80=94 Boards with arrays of sockets into which = cables can be plugged to connect different components. Telephone switchboar= ds are a familiar example =E2=80=94 operators plugged cables between socket= s to connect one caller to another. In Colossus, patch panels allowed some = degree of reconfiguration =E2=80=94 changing which circuits were connected = to which =E2=80=94 without physically rebuilding the machine.

    Circuit topology =E2=80=94 The arrangement and connections of co= mponents in a circuit =E2=80=94 which components are connected to which, an= d in what pattern. Topology from Greek topos (place) + logos (study). In ENIAC's case, the "program" was the physical wirin= g =E2=80=94 which accumulator connected to which, which function table was = addressed by which control unit. Changing the program meant changing the ph= ysical connections.

    Accumulators =E2=80=94 In ENIAC, the main computing units =E2=80= =94 20 of them, each capable of storing a 10-digit decimal number and perfo= rming addition. The term comes from "accumulate" =E2=80=94 to gather and ad= d up. An accumulator adds incoming values to whatever it currently holds, a= ccumulating a running total. ENIAC's accumulators could also be connected t= o perform subtraction, multiplication (implemented as repeated addition), a= nd division.

    Function tables =E2=80=94 Components in ENIAC that stored pre-se= t numerical values =E2=80=94 essentially read-only lookup tables. ENIAC had= three function tables, each holding a set of numbers that could be read du= ring computation. They were used to store constants, mathematical table val= ues, or intermediate data.

    Master programmer =E2=80=94 A component of ENIAC that controlled= the sequencing of operations =E2=80=94 determining which operations happen= ed in which order, and how many times loops repeated. It was ENIAC's rudime= ntary control unit =E2=80=94 the equivalent of the program counter and inst= ruction sequencer in a modern CPU.

    Rewiring =E2=80=94 Physically changing the connections between c= omponents =E2=80=94 disconnecting cables from some sockets and reconnecting= them to different sockets. Reprogramming ENIAC required rewiring its patch= panels and cable connections =E2=80=94 a process that took days.

    1,000=C3=97 faster =E2=80=94 One thousand times faster than. ENI= AC could perform approximately 5,000 additions per second. The fastest mech= anical calculators of the era could perform roughly 5 additions per second.= The speedup was approximately 1,000-fold for addition, and larger for more= complex operations.

    Manhattan Project =E2=80=94 The US government research program, = begun in 1942, to develop atomic bombs. Conducted at multiple sites across = the United States and Canada, with the main research laboratory at Los Alam= os, New Mexico. It employed approximately 130,000 people at its peak. The f= irst atomic bomb was tested in July 1945. Bombs were dropped on Hiroshima a= nd Nagasaki in August 1945. The computational demands of the project =E2=80= =94 modeling nuclear chain reactions, calculating implosion geometries =E2= =80=94 were among the driving forces behind the development of ENIAC.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    Where we are in = the story

    Turing= defined computation formally and established what computers can and cannot= do in principle.

    Shanno= n showed that Boolean algebra maps directly to electrical switching circuit= s =E2=80=94 giving the physical implementation path.

    Holler= ith demonstrated that electro-mechanical data processing works at scale.

    Everyt= hing is in place =E2=80=94 theoretically, mathematically, and conceptually.= The architecture is known. The mathematical foundation is established. The= physical medium =E2=80=94 electrical switching =E2=80=94 is identified.

    What r= emains is the engineering: actually building machines that implement all of= this in electronics, at sufficient scale and reliability to do useful work= .

    The en= gineering was done under the most urgent possible circumstances. A world wa= r created computational demands that no existing technology could meet =E2= =80=94 and the nations that solved these problems first would have decisive= military advantages.

    War is= a terrible forcing function. But it is an extremely effective one.

    Why mechanical c= omputers were not enough =E2=80=94 the speed problem

    To und= erstand why electronic computers were needed, you need to feel the scale of= the computational problems that World War II created.

    Ballistic tables =E2=80=94 The US Army's Ballistic Research Labo= ratory needed firing tables for each new artillery weapon deployed. Each ta= ble covered a range of firing conditions =E2=80=94 different angles, differ= ent propellant charges, different atmospheric conditions =E2=80=94 and gave= the correct gun settings for each. A single table required approximately 7= 50 trajectories to be calculated. Each trajectory required approximately 75= 0 multiplications to solve the differential equations describing the shell'= s flight. One trajectory therefore required about 750 multiplications =E2= =80=94 which took a trained human computer about 20 hours of calculation.

    One co= mplete firing table: 750 trajectories =C3=97 20 hours each =3D 15,000 perso= n-hours =3D roughly 2.5 months for one human computer working full time.

    The Ar= my needed hundreds of tables. They employed over 200 human computers at the= University of Pennsylvania, working constantly. They still fell behind. Ne= w weapons were deployed faster than tables could be produced. Artillery was= being used without accurate firing data.

    A diff= erential analyzer =E2=80=94 the best mechanical computer available =E2=80= =94 could reduce trajectory calculation time to about 20 minutes. Better, b= ut still not enough for the volume required.

    ENIAC,= running at electronic speeds, could calculate one trajectory in approximat= ely 30 seconds. The bottleneck dissolved.

    Codebreaking =E2=80=94 The Enigma and Lorenz ciphers changed the= ir settings daily. The Germans changed the Lorenz settings every day at mid= night. Breaking each day's settings required searching through enormous num= bers of possible configurations =E2=80=94 testing hypotheses about what the= settings might be, rejecting ones that were inconsistent with intercepted = messages, narrowing down to the actual settings.

    The Bo= mbe machines Turing designed could break Enigma settings in hours =E2=80=94= fast enough to produce usable intelligence the same day. But Lorenz was mo= re complex. The statistical analysis required to break Lorenz settings need= ed to count the frequency of specific symbol patterns in extremely long int= ercepted messages =E2=80=94 hundreds of thousands of counting operations pe= r message, performed against multiple hypotheses.

    A huma= n team with mechanical counters could do this =E2=80=94 in weeks. By which = time the settings had changed, the intelligence was stale, and the effort w= as wasted.

    Coloss= us could do it in hours. Hours was fast enough for the intelligence to be a= ctionable.

    Nuclear calculations =E2=80=94 Designing an implosion-type atomi= c bomb =E2=80=94 where conventional explosives compress fissile material to= supercritical density =E2=80=94 required solving complex three-dimensional= partial differential equations describing how shock waves traveled through= different materials. These calculations had no closed-form solution =E2=80= =94 they could only be solved numerically, step by step, approximating the = continuous physics with discrete calculations on a grid.

    A team= of human computers at Los Alamos, working with mechanical calculators, cou= ld advance the simulation by one timestep per day. A problem requiring thou= sands of timesteps took years. Some problems were simply not calculable at = human computation speeds =E2=80=94 the mathematics was known, but the compu= ting power to apply it was not available.

    Electr= onic computers made these calculations feasible.

    The Atanasoff-Be= rry Computer =E2=80=94 the first electronic digital computer

    John A= tanasoff had been struggling for years with the problem of solving systems = of simultaneous linear equations =E2=80=94 problems that arose constantly i= n physics. The mechanical calculators available to him were too slow for th= e large systems he needed to solve.

    In the= winter of 1937, driving through Illinois one night in a state of frustrati= on after a long day of unproductive thought, he stopped at a roadhouse, ord= ered a drink, and began sketching ideas on a napkin. By the time he left, h= e had the basic principles of a new kind of computing machine:

    1. = It would use electronic components =E2=80=94 vacuum tubes =E2=80=94 rather = than mechanical parts
    2. = It would use binary arithmetic rather than decimal
    3. = It would use capacitors for memory
    4. = It would perform calculations by direct logical action of electronics, not = by counting

    These = four principles together describe the fundamental architecture of electroni= c digital computing. Atanasoff arrived at all four in one evening.

    He ret= urned to Iowa State and began building the machine with graduate student Cl= ifford Berry. Construction proceeded from 1939 to 1942. The completed porti= ons of the machine could solve one equation per two to three minutes =E2=80= =94 primitive by later standards, but orders of magnitude faster than mecha= nical methods for this specific task.

    The AB= C used vacuum tubes in a novel way =E2=80=94 not just as amplifiers, as was= their primary use in radio and telephone equipment, but as digital switche= s, representing binary 0 and 1. This was the conceptual jump that made elec= tronic digital computing possible: realizing that vacuum tubes could be use= d for discrete binary logic, not just continuous analog signal processing.<= /p>

    The AB= C was never completed to full working order =E2=80=94 Atanasoff left Iowa S= tate in 1942 for war work, Berry died in 1963, and the machine was eventual= ly dismantled. But its principles were documented, and critically, Atanasof= f demonstrated them to John Mauchly during a visit in June 1941 =E2=80=94 a= visit that became central to later patent disputes about who invented the = electronic computer.

    The vacuum tube = =E2=80=94 understanding it deeply

    The va= cuum tube is the physical heart of the first electronic computers. Understa= nding it precisely matters because it is the direct predecessor of the tran= sistor =E2=80=94 which is the direct predecessor of the integrated circuit = =E2=80=94 which is what your CPU is made of today.

    The basic physics

    Inside= a vacuum tube, electrons behave differently than in air. In air, electrons= collide with gas molecules and are scattered =E2=80=94 it is hard to contr= ol their flow precisely. In a vacuum =E2=80=94 where there are essentially = no gas molecules =E2=80=94 electrons travel in straight lines, controllable= by electric and magnetic fields.

    The si= mplest useful vacuum tube is the triode =E2=80=94 three electrodes:

    Cathode =E2=80=94 A metal element heated by a filament. When hot= , it emits electrons =E2=80=94 they boil off the surface. This is thermioni= c emission.

    Anode (plate) =E2=80=94 A metal element at positive voltage rela= tive to the cathode. Electrons, being negatively charged, are attracted to = the positively charged anode. They flow from cathode to anode through the v= acuum.

    Grid =E2=80=94 A wire mesh between cathode and anode. Applying a= negative voltage to the grid repels electrons =E2=80=94 reducing or stoppi= ng the flow from cathode to anode. Applying a less negative (or positive) v= oltage allows more electrons through.

    The re= sult: small changes in grid voltage produce large changes in cathode-anode = current. The tube amplifies =E2=80=94 a weak signal on the grid controls a = strong signal at the anode.

    For di= gital computing, the tube is used as a switch rather than an amplifier:

    • = Grid very negative: no electron flow. Tube is off. Represents 0.
    • = Grid at zero or positive: maximum electron flow. Tube is on. Represents 1.<= /li>

    The tu= be switches between these two states in microseconds =E2=80=94 millionths o= f a second. Compared to mechanical relays, which switch in milliseconds =E2= =80=94 thousandths of a second =E2=80=94 tubes are 1,000 times faster.

    The reliability problem

    Vacuum= tubes had a well-known reputation for unreliability. They contained a heat= ed filament =E2=80=94 like a light bulb =E2=80=94 that eventually burned ou= t. The glass envelope could crack. The vacuum could degrade over time as tr= aces of gas leaked in.

    The av= erage life of a vacuum tube, under normal use, was perhaps a few thousand h= ours. An electronic computer with 18,000 tubes =E2=80=94 like ENIAC =E2=80= =94 had a statistical expectation of a tube failure every few hours of oper= ation.

    A tube= failure meant incorrect computation. Finding which of 18,000 tubes had fai= led was not trivial.

    Eckert= 's key insight for ENIAC was that tube failure rate depended strongly on op= erating voltage. Running tubes at significantly below their rated voltage d= ramatically extended their lives =E2=80=94 to tens of thousands of hours. H= e designed ENIAC to run its tubes at conservative voltages, and the reliabi= lity was far better than anyone had expected.

    Flower= s had made the same discovery independently for Colossus: tubes that were n= ever switched off =E2=80=94 left running continuously at conservative volta= ges =E2=80=94 lasted almost indefinitely. The act of switching them on and = off =E2=80=94 the thermal shock of heating and cooling =E2=80=94 was what k= illed them.

    Colossus =E2=80= =94 the first large-scale electronic computer

    Coloss= us was built at Bletchley Park under conditions of extreme secrecy. Most of= the people who worked there did not know what the machines in the next roo= m were doing.

    The problem it solved

    The Lo= renz cipher =E2=80=94 used for Germany's highest-level communications =E2= =80=94 was implemented by a teleprinter attachment that XORed each characte= r of the plaintext with a pseudo-random character stream generated by a set= of rotating wheels. The wheels had irregular patterns of pins =E2=80=94 so= me pins set to active, others inactive =E2=80=94 that determined the pseudo= -random stream.

    To dec= rypt intercepted messages, the codebreakers needed to determine the wheel s= tarting positions for each message. This required counting specific statist= ical patterns in the intercepted ciphertext =E2=80=94 patterns that were mo= re or less frequent depending on the wheel settings. The right settings pro= duced distinctive statistical signatures.

    The co= unting task: for each possible setting of the wheels (millions of possibili= ties), count how often a specific pattern appeared in the ciphertext. The s= etting with the highest count was likely the correct one.

    This w= as pure counting =E2=80=94 no complex arithmetic, just comparison and tally= ing =E2=80=94 but at enormous scale and speed.

    The machine

    Coloss= us Mark 1 contained approximately 1,600 vacuum tubes. Mark 2, introduced si= x months later, had 2,400 tubes and ran roughly five times faster.

    The in= tercepted ciphertext was punched onto paper tape =E2=80=94 a long loop of p= aper with holes =E2=80=94 that ran through the machine at 5,000 characters = per second.
