    Control transfer =E2=80=94 JMP (unconditional jump =E2=80=94 lo= ad a new value into IP, and possibly CS too for "far" jumps, per the near/f= ar discussion from earlier), the conditional jumps (JE/JNE/JZ<= /code>/JNZ/JG/JL/JC/JO and many more =E2=80=94 each one checks one= specific flag, or combination of flags, from FLAGS, and jumps onl= y if that condition holds =E2=80=94 this is the direct mechanism by wh= ich CM= P followed by a conditional jump implements an if-statement: CMP se= ts flags based on a subtraction's result, the conditional jump reads those = flags and decides whether to redirect IP), CALL/RET (function call/return= =E2=80=94 CALL pushes the return address onto the stack and jumps; RET p= ops that address back into IP and resumes there), LOOP (decrement CX, jump= back to the start of the loop if CX is not yet zero =E2=80=94 per Part 4.1= ), INT= /Processor control =E2=80=94 CLI/STI (clear/set IF =E2=80=94 co= vered in the interrupt section), CLD/STD (clear/set DF =E2=80=94 Part 4.5= ), HLT= (halt =E2=80=94 stop fetching/executing entirely, until the next ha= rdware interrupt arrives), NOP (no operation =E2=80=94 do nothing for one = instruction's worth of time; useful for precise timing or as placeholder sp= ace), = WAIT and LOCK (synchronization-related instructions for coordinatin= g with external hardware =E2=80=94 less relevant for a single-CPU OS just g= etting started, but present in the instruction set).

    What this = document establishes

    Every = register now has a name, a size, a physical location (BIU or EU), and a reason =E2=80=94 a specific job that explains why it exists rather th= an some other configuration. The ALU's operations are grounded in the same = addition/Boolean-logic primitives traced all the way back through Pascal, L= eibniz, and Boole. The FLAGS register's nine bits are each individually mea= ningful, and collectively they're how "if-statements" exist on a chip that = has no built-in concept of "if." The BIU/EU split =E2=80=94 and the prefetc= h queue it motivates =E2=80=94 is the 8086's first, primitive brush with th= e pipelining and branch-penalty concepts that will recur, in far more elabo= rate form, throughout the rest of CPU history. And the worked instruction-d= ecoding example in Part 6.5 shows all of these pieces operating together, o= n three real bytes, producing one real operation.

    With t= his in hand, the next document =E2=80=94 real mode, protected mode, and the= GDT, told as their own history from absolute zero =E2=80=94 has a complete= vocabulary to draw on: when that document says "the segment register," "th= e offset," "the flags," "CS:IP," or "an instruction's encoding," every one = of those phrases now refers to something concrete you've seen built from th= e ground up.

    Ready = to move there when you are.

    =

    You said= : Go ahead!

    Go ahead!

    Jun 15

    Claude responded: Real Mode, Protected Mode, and the GDT =E2=80= =94 From Absolute Zero

    <= div>
    Architec= ted comprehensive CPU modes document from foundational principles
    Archi= tected comprehensive CPU modes document from foundational principles=

    Real Mode, Protected Mode, and the GDT =E2=80=94 = From Absolute Zero

    This d= ocument does for "modes" and the GDT what the previous document did for the= 8086's registers and instructions: nothing is assumed. We start from a CPU= that has no concept of "mode" at all =E2=80=94 because, as you'll see, no such concept existed until a specific problem forced it into exist= ence =E2=80=94 and build up to the complete mechanism, byte by byte, with w= orked examples.

    Part 1 =E2= =80=94 Before "mode" existed

    Go bac= k to the 8086, exactly as described in the previous document. Every registe= r, every instruction, every addressing mode we covered =E2=80=94 all of it = describes the only way the 8086 knows how to operate. There is no = alternative. The chip does not have a "mode" in any meaningful sense, for t= he same reason a hammer doesn't have a "mode" =E2=80=94 it does one thing, = the way it does it, and that's the entire story.

    If you= asked an engineer in 1979, "what mode is your 8086 running in?" =E2=80=94 = the question would not make sense. It would be like asking "what mode is yo= ur calculator in?" when the calculator only ever does arithmetic one way.

    The term "real mode" did not exist in 1978. It is a retroact= ive name =E2=80=94 a label applied later, in 1982, the moment a se= cond way of operating was introduced on a different chip (the 286). On= ce there were two ways, the old way needed a name to distinguish it from th= e new way, and "real mode" is the name that was chosen for "the way the 808= 6 always worked, now considered as one option among several." The new way g= ot the name "protected mode."

    This m= atters because it reframes the entire question. You are not learning about = "two settings a CPU can be in." You are learning about a second sys= tem that was bolted onto the first one, for a specific reason =E2= =80=94 and "mode" is just the name for "which of these two systems is curre= ntly active." Once we understand why a second system was needed at= all, the mechanics of how it works will follow naturally, because every pi= ece of it exists to solve a specific, nameable problem.

    Part 2 =E2= =80=94 The protection problem, from absolute zero

    2.1 =E2=80=94 On= e program, one computer: no problem

    Pictur= e the simplest possible situation: a computer with an 8086-style CPU, some = RAM, and exactly one program running. That program was loa= ded into RAM by whoever is operating the computer, and it has the entir= e machine to itself =E2=80=94 all of RAM, all of the CPU's time, every= thing.

    In thi= s situation, nothing can go wrong from "another program" = =E2=80=94 because there is no other program. If this one program has a bug = that causes it to write to some address it shouldn't, the only thi= ng it can damage is itself, or the operating system underneath it (if there= is one) =E2=80=94 and if it crashes, the user just restarts it. Annoying, = but contained.

    This w= as the situation for most of the 8086's early life. DOS =E2=80=94 the opera= ting system the IBM PC ran =E2=80=94 was built around this assumption: one program runs at a time, has full access to everything, and if it mi= sbehaves, it can only hurt itself.

    2.2 =E2=80=94 No= w put two programs in the same RAM, at the same time

    Now im= agine a different situation. Two programs are both loaded into RAM= simultaneously, and the CPU =E2=80=94 using the timer-interrupt-d= riven preemptive multitasking mechanism from the interrupt section =E2=80= =94 switches back and forth between them, giving each a slice of time.

    Program A is, say, a word processor. It has loaded its own code = into one region of RAM, and is using another region of RAM to hold the docu= ment the user is typing.

    Program B is, say, a calculator program, loaded into a diffe= rent region of RAM, with its own data.

    Both p= rograms are written exactly as they would be for the single-program cas= e =E2=80=94 neither was written with any awareness that the other exis= ts. Each program's instructions contain ordinary MOV instructions, ordinar= y addressing modes ([BX], [BP+4], direct addresses), exactly as covered i= n the 8086 document. Each program simply computes addresses =E2=80=94 using= its registers, its segment registers, whatever values its own logic produc= es =E2=80=94 and reads or writes to those addresses, trusting that= the address it computed is "its own" memory.

    Here is the problem, stated as concretely as possible: Program A= has a bug. Somewhere in its code, due to that bug, a register ends up hold= ing a value that =E2=80=94 when combined with a segment register through th= e segmentation unit (per the foundational addressing document) =E2=80=94 pr= oduces a 20-bit physical address that happens to fall inside the region= of RAM where Program B's data lives. Program A then executes an ordin= ary MO= V [BX], AX =E2=80=94 a completely normal instruction, identical in e= very way to a million other instructions Program A executes correctly =E2= =80=94 and this one instruction overwrites part of Program B's data= with whatever happened to be in AX.

    From the CPU's perspective, nothing unusual happened. The segmen= tation unit did exactly what it always does: combined a segment register an= d an offset register into a 20-bit address, and the BIU did exactly what it= always does: put that address on the address bus and asserted WRITE. The R= AM chip's decoder did exactly what it always does: activated the one byte-g= roup matching that address, and the data bus's value got written into it. <= strong>Every single piece of hardware we've described so far performed flaw= lessly, exactly as designed. And yet, the result is that = one program corrupted another program's data =E2=80=94 silently, with no er= ror, no warning, nothing.

    2.3 =E2=80=94 Wh= y can't software just "be careful"?

    The na= tural first reaction is: "well, just write Program A correctly, so it never= computes a bad address." But this misses the actual shape of the problem, = for two separate reasons.

    Reason one =E2=80=94 bugs are not a choice. Programs have bugs. = This is not a moral failing to be eliminated by trying harder; it is an emp= irical, universal fact about software of any meaningful size, for reasons c= onnected to Turing's halting problem (you genuinely cannot, in general, pro= ve a program will never compute a bad address, for the same family of reaso= ns you can't generally prove a program will halt). Saying "the solution is = for programs to not have bugs" is not a solution =E2=80=94 it's a restateme= nt of the problem as a wish.

    Reason two =E2=80=94 even a perfectly correct Program A cannot p= rotect itself from a malicious Program C. Suppose Program= C is not buggy at all =E2=80=94 it is deliberately written to com= pute addresses landing inside Program A's memory, and deliberately overwrit= e Program A's data, perhaps to steal information or sabotage it. Program A = did nothing wrong. There is no "being more careful" Program A could have do= ne =E2=80=94 the threat isn't coming from A's own mistakes, it's coming fro= m a different program's deliberate actions.

    So the= real question becomes: is there any mechanism =E2=80=94 s= hort of "trust everyone to be both perfect and benevolent" =E2=80=94 that c= an prevent one program's memory accesses from reaching another program's me= mory?

    2.4 =E2=80=94 Wh= y can't the operating system (software) enforce this?

    This i= s the crucial step, and it's where "hardware-enforced" stops being an arbit= rary design choice and becomes the only option that actually works= .

    Suppos= e the OS =E2=80=94 itself just a program, running on the same CPU =E2=80=94= tries to enforce protection in software. What would this even mea= n, mechanically? The OS would need to somehow intercept ev= ery memory access any program makes, check whether the address is "allowed"= for that program, and only then let it through.

    But th= ink about what "intercept every memory access" requires, given everything i= n the 8086 document. A MOV [BX], AX instruction, once fetched by the BIU a= nd decoded by the EU, directly drives the segmentation unit, which= directly drives the address bus, which directly triggers= RAM's decoder. There is no step in this sequence where "ask the OS first" = naturally fits =E2=80=94 the EU doesn't pause mid-instruction to consult so= me other program. The hardware path from "decoded instruction" to "address = bus signal" is a fixed, direct circuit =E2=80=94 the whole point o= f building it as a direct circuit (rather than, say, routing every memory a= ccess through some software dispatcher) was speed, per everything = we've established about why registers/ALU/buses are designed the way they a= re.

    You could, in principle, imagine a scheme where every single instruction= that touches memory is preceded by additional instructions that c= ompute the address, compare it against some "allowed range" stored by the O= S, and only proceed if it's in range. But consider what this would cost: every single memory access in every program would need several ad= ditional instructions just for this check =E2=80=94 multiplyi= ng the instruction count of every program by some significant factor, for <= em>no functional benefit to that program (the checking instructions do= n't help the program do its job; they're pure overhead). And =E2=80=94 fata= lly =E2=80=94 nothing stops a program from simply not including tho= se extra instructions. If "checking" is just more instructions= the program itself executes, a program (buggy, or malicious) that ski= ps those instructions faces no obstacle at all =E2=80=94 the under= lying direct-circuit path from instruction to address bus is still there, u= nchanged, and a program that doesn't bother with the "check first" instruct= ions just... doesn't get checked.

    This is the core insight: software-based protection would requir= e every memory-accessing instruction to be, in effect, volunta= rily preceded by a check =E2=80=94 and "voluntary" is exactly the prop= erty that cannot provide protection against either bugs (which don't "volun= tarily" do anything =E2=80=94 they're just wrong) or malice (which actively= wants to skip the check).

    2.5 =E2=80=94 Wh= at "hardware-enforced" actually means, concretely

    The on= ly way to make a check non-optional =E2=80=94 to make it impossibl= e for any sequence of instructions, however written, to bypass it = =E2=80=94 is for the check to be performed by circuitry that sits p= hysically in the path between "decoded instruction" and "address bus signal= ," through which every memory access must pass, with no a= lternative route.

    This i= s exactly analogous to the decoder we described for RAM (Part of the founda= tional document) =E2=80=94 the decoder isn't "asked nicely" to activate the= right byte-group; it's wired such that the address bus's signal physic= ally, electrically determines which byte-group gets activated, with no= other possible outcome.

    Hardware-enforced protection means: insert a piece of circuitry into the = BIU, between "the segmentation unit has produced a candidate address" and "= this address actually goes out onto the address bus," whose job is to check= =E2=80=94 using information the OS configured once, in advance = =E2=80=94 whether this specific program, right now is allowed to a= ccess this specific address, and if not, to not let the a= ccess proceed at all, but instead to redirect the CPU into an interrupt (sp= ecifically, an exception =E2=80=94 recall the General Protection Fault, vec= tor 13, from the interrupt section) where the OS gets control and can deal = with the violation.

    Crucia= lly: this circuitry is in the only path memory accesses can take. = There is no instruction, no sequence of bytes, no clever trick a program co= uld execute that bypasses this circuitry =E2=80=94 because it's no= t a step the program takes; it's a property of the wire, = the same way the RAM decoder isn't a step a program takes, it's just what t= he wires do when a signal arrives.

    This is the answer to the question that started this entire journey, week= s ago. The "bit that controls the switch between kernel and user m= ode," and the reason memory protection is "hardware-enforced rather than a = software convention," is this: protection must live in the address= -generation circuitry itself =E2=80=94 the same circuitry, in the same phys= ical location, that the segmentation unit already occupies =E2=80=94 becaus= e that is the only point through which every memory access, by any program, must physically pass, with no possible detour. = Software conventions can always be skipped by software that doesn't follow = the convention. A wire that's actually in the only available path cannot be= skipped by anything =E2=80=94 skipping it would require the signa= l to reach the address bus via some other route, and no other rout= e exists.

    Part 3 =E2= =80=94 The 80286: building this circuitry, and the birth of "modes"

    In 198= 2, Intel released the 80286, and this is where the circuitry from Part 2.5 = was actually built =E2=80=94 for the first time, on an x86 chip.

    But he= re's the constraint Intel faced: millions of 8086-based machines al= ready existed, running millions of programs written for the 8086's addressi= ng scheme (segment=C3=9716+offset, no protection circuitry, per the foundat= ional document). Intel could not simply replace the old a= ddressing scheme with the new protection-aware one =E2=80=94 every existing= program would stop working, because every existing program's segment regis= ter values were "raw" values meant to be multiplied by 16, and under a = new scheme where segment register values mean something different<= /em> (as we're about to see), those same bit patterns would compute complet= ely different =E2=80=94 and almost certainly wrong, or protection-violating= =E2=80=94 addresses.

    Intel's solution: build the new circuitry alongside the old circ= uitry, with a single bit determining which one is "live" at any given momen= t. This is the literal origin of "mode" as a concept =E2=80=94 not= a metaphor, not an abstraction, but a precise engineering description: two distinct pieces of address-generation circuitry exist on the same chip= , and exactly one of them is connected to the address bus at any given time= , determined by one bit.

    The ol= d circuitry =E2=80=94 segment=C3=9716+offset, no checks, exactly as the 808= 6 always did it =E2=80=94 retroactively became known as "real mode" (the "r= eal," literal, direct interpretation of a segment register's bits as a numb= er to be multiplied).

    The ne= w circuitry =E2=80=94 which we're about to build in detail =E2=80=94 became= "protected mode" (named directly for what it does: it protects).<= /p>

    Part 4 =E2= =80=94 CR0 and the PE bit: the actual switch, in physical terms

    The bi= t that selects which circuitry is live is bit 0 of a register called CR0 ("Control Register 0"). This register did not exist on the 8= 086 at all =E2=80=94 it is itself part of what the 286 added. CR0 = is 32 bits wide (though on the 286 specifically, only the lower bits are me= aningful; the full 32-bit width became relevant with the 386). Bit 0 is nam= ed PE =E2=80=94 Protection Enable.

    Physically: recall, from the foundational document, that the seg= mentation unit is a piece of circuitry that takes a segment-register value = and an offset-register value as inputs and produces a 20-bit (or, with prot= ected mode, potentially wider) address as output. The 286 version of this c= ircuitry contains, in effect, two separate sub-circuits th= at can each compute "an address" from a segment-register value =E2=80=94 ca= ll them the real-mode address generator and the pr= otected-mode address generator =E2=80=94 plus a final-stage select= or (built from the same kind of AND/OR gate logic as the RAM decoder we bui= lt earlier) that picks which sub-circuit's output actually proceed= s to the address bus, based on the current value of PE.

    • = If PE =3D 0: the selector routes the real-mode address generator's output (= segment=C3=9716+offset, exactly as the 8086 always computed it) to the addr= ess bus. The protected-mode generator's circuitry exists on the chip, physi= cally, but its output is simply not connected to anything at this = moment =E2=80=94 it computes a result, but that result goes nowhere.
    • = If PE =3D 1: the selector routes the protected-mode address generator's out= put to the address bus instead. Now the real-mode generator's outp= ut goes nowhere, and the protected-mode generator's output =E2=80=94 which,= as we're about to see, involves the GDT, descriptors, and privilege checks= =E2=80=94 is what determines every memory access.

    How does PE get set? CR0, like the other CR registers (briefly m= entioned in the previous document), is not a general-purpose register =E2= =80=94 you cannot use ordinary MOV between CR0 and AX the way you'd move b= etween AX and BX. There is a specific instruction form, MOV CR0, <reg= ister>, that exists precisely for loading values into control reg= isters =E2=80=94 and (foreshadowing Part 9) this instruction can only be executed when the CPU is at the highest privilege level. The OS, dur= ing its startup sequence, executes something like:

    asm
    mov eax, cr0 ; read CR0's current value into EAX or eax, 1 ; set bit 0 (PE) to 1, leaving all ot= her bits unchanged mov cr0, eax ; write the modified value back into CR0

    The in= stant that third instruction executes, the chip's internal selector =E2=80= =94 the AND/OR-gate circuitry described above =E2=80=94 switches which addr= ess generator is connected to the address bus. Every memory access = from this point forward uses the protected-mode generator. This is= the literal "mode switch" =E2=80=94 one bit, in one register, controlling = which of two physically-present circuits is electrically connected to the a= ddress bus.

    Part 5 =E2= =80=94 The selector: what a segment register's value means now

    With P= E=3D1, the meaning of the 16-bit value sitting in, say, the DS reg= ister changes completely. In real mode, that 16-bit value was "the segment"= =E2=80=94 directly fed into the =C3=9716 multiplication. In protected mode= , that same 16-bit storage location (the physical register hasn't changed = =E2=80=94 it's the same DS register from the 8086 document) now ho= lds something called a selector, and its 16 bits are s= ubdivided into three separate fields, each with its own meaning. Let's= lay out exactly which bits mean what =E2=80=94 the same byte/bit-field app= roach we used for ModRM in the previous document.

    A sele= ctor's 16 bits, numbered 15 (highest) down to 0 (lowest):

    Bits 15=E2=80=933 (the upper 13 bits) =E2=80=94 Index. This 13-b= it number identifies which entry in a table (the GDT, which we're = about to build) this selector refers to. 13 bits gives 2=C2=B9=C2=B3 =3D 8,= 192 possible index values =E2=80=94 meaning a table can have up to 8,192 en= tries.

    Bit 2 =E2=80=94 TI (Table Indicator). A single bit choosing = which table the index refers to: TI=3D0 means "the GDT" (Global Descri= ptor Table =E2=80=94 global in the sense that it's shared by the whole syst= em, set up once by the OS). TI=3D1 means a different table called the LDT (Local Descriptor Table) =E2=80=94 a per-program t= able, intended to let each program have its own private set of descriptors,= separate from the global ones. The LDT mechanism exists but is rarely cent= ral to OS design in practice (most modern OSes set up one GDT and don't bot= her with per-program LDTs, instead achieving per-program isolation through = paging, which we'll cover in a later document) =E2=80=94 but the bit exists= , and TI=3D0 (GDT) is overwhelmingly the common case, including in everythi= ng your OS project will do.

    Bits 1=E2=80=930 =E2=80=94 RPL (Requested Privilege Level). A 2-= bit number, 0 through 3, which we'll explain fully in Part 9. For now: it r= epresents "what privilege level is being requested by whoever's us= ing this selector."

    Worked example =E2=80=94 decoding the selector value 0x10:

    0x10 i= n binary, as 16 bits: 0000 0000 0001 0000

    • = Bits 1=E2=80=930 (RPL): 00 =E2=86=92 RPL =3D 0
    • = Bit 2 (TI): 0 =E2=86=92 TI =3D 0 =E2=86=92 this refers to the GDT
    • = Bits 15=E2=80=933 (Index): take bits 15 through 3, which is 0000 0000 0001 0 =E2=86=92 as a number, this is 0 0000 0000 0010 =3D 2<= /li>

    So sel= ector 0x10 means: "GDT entry number 2 (the third entry, since inde= xing starts at 0), requested at privilege level 0, via the GDT (not the LDT= )."

    Why does the raw number 0x10 happen to equal "16," and why does 16 =3D 2= =C3=978? Because =E2=80=94 as we'll see in Part 6 =E2=80=94 each G= DT entry occupies exactly 8 bytes in memory. To find where in memory GDT entry number 2 actually lives, the CPU computes: (GDT's base address= in memory) + (index =C3=97 8 bytes per entry) =3D (GDT's base) + (2 =C3=97= 8) =3D (GDT's base) + 16. And =E2=80=94 purely as a numerical coincidence = arising from how the bit-fields are laid out =E2=80=94 when RPL=3D0 and TI= =3D0, the selector's raw 16-bit value, interpreted as an ordinary n= umber, is numerically identical to (index =C3=97 8) =E2= =80=94 because the index occupies bits 15-3, which is exactly "the number, = shifted left by 3 positions" =E2=80=94 and shifting left by 3 is multiplyin= g by 2=C2=B3=3D8 (recall the shift-is-multiplication principle from the bit= -shifting document). So "selector 0x10" and "byte-offset 0x10 within the GD= T" are the same number, for the most common case (RPL=3D0, TI=3D0) =E2=80= =94 which is why you'll often see selector values like 0x08, 0x10,= 0x18, 0x20 in OS code: these are simply "entry 1, entry 2, entry 3, entry = 4," expressed as their byte-offsets.

    Part 6 =E2= =80=94 The GDT: what it is and where it lives

    The GDT (Global Descriptor Table) is, physically, nothing more t= han an array of bytes sitting in ordinary RAM =E2=80=94 th= e same RAM we built up from the decoder/byte-group picture in the foundatio= nal document. There is nothing special about this RAM compared to = any other RAM; it's just a region the OS has set aside and filled with spec= ific data, in a specific format that the protected-mode address generator k= nows how to interpret.

    How does the CPU know where this array is? Via another = new register the 286 introduced: GDTR (GDT Register). GDTR= holds two pieces of information: a base address (where in= RAM the GDT starts) and a limit (how many bytes long the = GDT is, i.e., how far past the base address valid entries extend).

    The OS= loads GDTR using a dedicated instruction, LGDT ("Load GDT= register"), which takes, as its operand, the memory address of a small 6-b= yte structure the OS has prepared =E2=80=94 2 bytes for the limit, 4 bytes = for the base address (this 6-byte structure is sometimes just called "the G= DT descriptor," confusingly similar to "descriptor" meaning "one entry in t= he GDT" =E2=80=94 context disambiguates: "the GDT descriptor" with no furth= er qualification usually means this 6-byte base+limit structure fed to LGDT= , while "a descriptor" or "a segment descriptor" means one 8-byte entry within the GDT).

    asm
    gdt_descriptor: dw gdt_end - gdt_start - 1 ; limit: size of GDT = in bytes, minus 1 dd gdt_start ; base: address where the G= DT begins lgdt [gdt_descriptor] ; load GDTR with this base+= limit

    Once G= DTR is loaded, the protected-mode address generator now has everyth= ing it needs: given a selector (Part 5), it can compute "index =C3= =97 8," add that to GDTR's base address, and arrive at the memory address o= f one specific 8-byte descriptor =E2=80=94 which is what Part 7 de= fines.

    Part 7 =E2= =80=94 The descriptor: 8 bytes, field by field

    Each e= ntry in the GDT =E2=80=94 each descriptor =E2=80=94 is exa= ctly 8 bytes. Let's build up what those 8 bytes mean, the same way we built= up the ModRM byte: field by field, with the purpose of each field= stated alongside its bit position.

    Recall= the descriptor's job, from Part 2.5: it must let the protected-mode addres= s generator answer two questions for any memory access using this descripto= r: (1) where, physically, does this segment actually start (its bas= e address)? (2) how big is it, i.e., what's the largest valid offset within= it (its limit)? and (3) is the program currently allowed to access it at a= ll (privilege check)?

    Here i= s the full 8-byte layout (byte 0 is the lowest-addressed byte of the descri= ptor; this is the format used from the 386 onward =E2=80=94 the 286's origi= nal format was a subset, which we'll note):

    Bytes 0=E2=80=931 =E2=80=94 Limit, bits 0=E2=80=9315. The lower = 16 bits of the segment's size limit.

    Bytes 2=E2=80=933 =E2=80=94 Base, bits 0=E2=80=9315. The lower 1= 6 bits of the segment's starting physical address.

    Byte 4 =E2=80=94 Base, bits 16=E2=80=9323. The next 8 bits of th= e base address.

    Byte 5 =E2=80=94 Access byte. This single byte is itself subdivi= ded:

    • = Bit 7: P (Present). If 0, this descriptor is not currently= valid/in-use =E2=80=94 any attempt to use a selector pointing here causes = a fault (recall: this is the "Present bit" mentioned back in the IDT discus= sion in the interrupt section =E2=80=94 the same concept, applied to GDT en= tries instead of IDT entries).
    • = Bits 6=E2=80=935: DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level). A 2-bi= t value, 0=E2=80=933 =E2=80=94 the privilege level required to acc= ess this segment. Central to Part 9.
    • = Bit 4: S (System/Segment flag). If 1, this is an ordinary = code or data segment descriptor (what we're focused on). If 0, this descrip= tor instead describes a system structure =E2=80=94 for example, a = descriptor in the GDT can actually describe where the IDT is, or d= escribe a special structure called a TSS (Task State Segment) used in some context-switching schemes. For our purposes =E2=80=94 your= OS's basic code/data segments =E2=80=94 S=3D1.
    • = Bits 3=E2=80=930: Type. For S=3D1 descriptors, these bits = further specify: is this a code segment or a data segment= , and =E2=80=94 for code =E2=80=94 is it executable-only or also readable; = for data, is it read-only or read-write; plus a couple of less commonly use= d bits (a "direction" bit for data segments, and an "accessed" bit the CPU = sets automatically the first time the segment is used, which some OSes use = for memory-management bookkeeping).

    Byte 6 =E2=80=94 Flags + Limit, bits 16=E2=80=9319. This byte is= also subdivided:

    • = Bit 7: G (Granularity). This bit changes the units of the Limit field. If G=3D0, the limit (bits 0-19, spread across bytes 0= -1 and the low nibble of byte 6, giving 20 bits total) is measured in bytes =E2=80=94 meaning the maximum representable limit is 2=C2= =B2=E2=81=B0 =3D 1MB (exactly matching the 8086's original 1MB world =E2=80= =94 not a coincidence; this preserves meaningful continuity with real-mode-= sized segments). If G=3D1, the same 20-bit limit field is instead = measured in 4-kilobyte units =E2=80=94 so a limit value of= , say, 0xFFFFF (the maximum 20-bit value) with G=3D1 means "0xFFFFF =C3=97 = 4096 bytes" =E2=89=88 4GB. This is the mechanism by which a 20-bit= field can describe segments up to 4GB =E2=80=94 by changing what = each "unit" of the field represents, exactly the same kind of trick as the = segment=C3=9716 formula changed what a register's units represente= d, just applied to a table field instead of a register.
    • = Bit 6: D/B (Default operation size). If 1, this code/data = segment is treated as 32-bit by default (instructions within it default to = 32-bit registers and addresses unless overridden); if 0, 16-bit default (pr= eserving 286-style behavior). This bit is part of how the 386 lets the = same chip run 16-bit-flavored and 32-bit-flavored protected-mode code,= selected per-segment.
    • = Bit 5: L (Long mode). Relevant only on 64-bit CPUs =E2=80= =94 indicates a 64-bit code segment. Not meaningful in the 32-bit story we'= re building here, but exists in the byte.
    • = Bit 4: AVL (Available). Not used by the CPU hardware at al= l =E2=80=94 reserved for the OS to use for whatever it wants, a si= ngle bit of "scratch space" within the descriptor.
    • = Bits 3=E2=80=930: Limit, bits 16=E2=80=9319. The upper 4 b= its of the 20-bit limit field (combining with bytes 0-1 for the full 20 bit= s).

    Byte 7 =E2=80=94 Base, bits 24=E2=80=9331. The final 8 bits of t= he base address, completing a full 32-bit base.

    Total: Base =3D 32 bits (bytes 2,3,4,7 =E2=80=94 note the non-contiguous = byte order, a direct artifact of byte 7 being a later addition to = a format whose first 6 bytes were fixed by the original 286 design, which o= nly had a 24-bit base =E2=80=94 Intel had to find "spare" space for the ext= ra base bits without disturbing the existing byte layout, and byte 7 was wh= ere that spare space existed). Limit =3D 20 bits (bytes 0,1, plus low nibbl= e of byte 6), with the G bit determining whether this represents up to 1MB = (G=3D0) or up to 4GB (G=3D1).

    Worked example = =E2=80=94 decoding a real descriptor

    Suppos= e a GDT entry, as 8 bytes in memory (shown as hex bytes, in the order they'= d appear in memory =E2=80=94 byte 0 first), reads:

    FF FF 00 00 00 9A CF 00

    Bytes 0-1 (Limit 0-15): FF FF =E2=86=92 as a 16-bit number (rec= all little-endian from the memory bus document =E2=80=94 the lower-addresse= d byte is the low byte of the value): byte 0 =3D 0xFF (low byte), = byte 1 =3D 0xFF (high byte) =E2=86=92 value =3D 0xFFFF =3D 65,535.

    Bytes 2-3 (Base 0-15): 00 00 =E2=86=92 0x0000.

    Byte 4 (Base 16-23): 00.

    Byte 5 (Access byte): 9A =3D binary 1001 1010.

    • = Bit 7 (P) =3D 1 =E2=86=92 Present.
    • = Bits 6-5 (DPL) =3D 00 =E2=86=92 DPL =3D 0 (most privileged =E2=80=94 kerne= l level).
    • = Bit 4 (S) =3D 1 =E2=86=92 ordinary code/data segment.
    • = Bits 3-0 (Type) =3D 1010 =E2=86=92 for S=3D1 descriptors, bit 3 of Type di= stinguishes code (1) vs data (0); here bit 3 =3D 1, so this is a c= ode segment. The remaining type bits (010) further specify it's e= xecutable and readable (the precise sub-encoding of these bits follows Inte= l's defined type table, but the headline is: executable code segmen= t).

    Byte 6 (Flags + Limit 16-19): CF =3D binary 1100 1111.

    • = Bit 7 (G) =3D 1 =E2=86=92 limit is in 4KB units.
    • = Bit 6 (D/B) =3D 1 =E2=86=92 32-bit default operand/address size.
    • = Bit 5 (L) =3D 0.
    • = Bit 4 (AVL) =3D 0.
    • = Bits 3-0 (Limit 16-19) =3D 1111.

    Byte 7 (Base 24-31): 00.

    Putting it together:

    Full B= ase =3D bytes (7)(4)(3)(2) =3D 00 00 00 00 =3D 0x00000000= =E2=80=94 this segment starts at physical address 0.

    Full L= imit =3D (byte 6's low nibble)(bytes 1)(0) =3D 1111 1111 1111 1111 1111 = =3D 11= 11 1111 1111 1111 1111 (20 bits, all 1s) =3D 2=C2=B2=E2=81=B0 =E2=88= =92 1 =3D 1,048,575.

    But G= =3D1, so this limit is in 4KB units: actual byte limit =3D (1,048,575 + 1) = =C3=97 4096 =E2=88=92 1 =3D 1,048,576 =C3=97 4096 =E2=88=92 1 =3D 4,294,967= ,295 =3D 0xFFFFFFFF =E2=80=94 the maximum possible 32-bit = address.

    In plain language, this descriptor says: "This is an executable = code segment, accessible only at privilege level 0, 32-bit by default, star= ting at physical address 0 and extending all the way to the end of the enti= re 4GB address space."

    This is precisely the "flat segment" described in the earlier CPU-evoluti= on document =E2=80=94 a single descriptor whose base=3D0 and limit= =3D4GB means "this segment covers everything," which is exactly ho= w 32-bit flat-model operating systems (Part 11) configure their code and da= ta descriptors: not because segmentation has been removed, but bec= ause this particular descriptor, when used, imposes no meaningful = restriction =E2=80=94 every address a 32-bit offset register could ever pro= duce (0 to 0xFFFFFFFF) falls within base=3D0-to-limit=3D0xFFFFFFFF, so the = limit check (Part 8) never fails, and the base of 0 means "offset =3D physi= cal address" directly, with no translation at all.

    Part 8 =E2= =80=94 The complete lookup, step by step, with a worked example

    Now le= t's trace through, in full, what happens when an instruction performs a mem= ory access in protected mode =E2=80=94 paralleling the "complete worked exa= mple" from the 8086 document (Part 6.5), but now for protected mode.

    Setup: Suppose DS currently holds the selector 0x10 (decoded in= Part 5 as: index=3D2, TI=3D0/GDT, RPL=3D0). Suppose GDTR's base points to = some address in RAM =E2=80=94 call it 0x00001000 =E2=80=94 where the OS bu= ilt its GDT. Suppose the CPU is currently executing at CPL=3D0 (we'll defin= e CPL fully in Part 9; for now, "CPL=3D0" means "currently running at the m= ost-privileged level"). Suppose the instruction is MOV AL, [SI], with SI c= urrently holding the value 0x00002500.

    Step 1 =E2=80=94 The EU decodes the instruction. Per the 8086 do= cument's Part 6, the EU determines: this is a MOV (data transfer), the sou= rce operand is a memory location whose offset is given by SI, and (per the = default-segment conventions from Part 26 of the earlier document) since no = segment-override prefix is present and SI isn't BP, the default se= gment for this access is DS.

    Step 2 =E2=80=94 The protected-mode address generator retrieves DS's "sha= dow" information. Recall, from the foundational addressing documen= t, that segment registers have a hidden/shadow portion the hardware maintai= ns automatically, populated whenever a new value is loaded into the seg= ment register (not re-fetched on every access). At some earlier point,= an instruction mov ds, ax (with AX holding 0x10) was executed, and a= t that moment, the CPU performed the GDT lookup: selector 0x10 =E2= =86=92 index 2 =E2=86=92 physical address 0x00001000 + (2 =C3=97 8) =3D 0x000010= 10 =E2=86=92 read the 8 bytes at 0x00001010 (the descriptor, per Pa= rt 7's byte layout) =E2=86=92 extract Base, Limit, DPL, Type =E2=86=92 stor= e these extracted values in DS's shadow registers.

    So at the moment of this MOV AL, [SI] instruction, no GDT memor= y-read happens at all =E2=80=94 the shadow values from that earlie= r load are simply already sitting there, ready to use. (This is wh= y, as noted in the foundational document, segment loads are the "expensive"= operation =E2=80=94 they trigger the GDT read =E2=80=94 while subsequent o= rdinary accesses using that segment are fast, using the cached shadow.)

    Suppos= e the shadow values, from our Part 7 worked example, are: Base =3D 0x00000000, Limit =3D 0xFFFFFFFF (in bytes, after the G-bit expansion), DPL =3D 0= .

    Step 3 =E2=80=94 The limit check. The address generator computes= : is the offset (0x00002500, from SI) =E2=89=A4 the segment's limit (0xFFFFFFFF<= /code>)? Yes, 0x00002500 is far less than 0xFFFFFFFF. Check passe= s.

    (If th= is check had failed =E2=80=94 offset greater than limit =E2=80=94 = the address generator would not produce an address at all. Instead= , it signals a General Protection Fault (interrupt vector = 13, from the interrupt section's exception table), and the CPU's normal int= errupt mechanism takes over: current state is pushed onto the stack, the ID= T is consulted for vector 13's handler, and the OS's GPF handler runs inste= ad of the MOV instruction ever completing. This is the literal mechanism b= ehind "segmentation fault" =E2=80=94 the segmentation unit's limit check fa= iled.)

    Step 4 =E2=80=94 The privilege check. The address generator comp= ares CPL (currently 0, by our setup) against DPL (0, from the descriptor) = =E2=80=94 using the rule we'll formalize in Part 9, but informally: is the = currently running code privileged enough to use this desc= riptor? CPL=3D0 vs DPL=3D0 =E2=86=92 yes, allowed. Check passes.

    (If CP= L had been, say, 3 =E2=80=94 user mode =E2=80=94 and DPL were 0 =E2=80=94 k= ernel-only =E2=80=94 this check would fail, again producing a Gene= ral Protection Fault, without the limit check or anything else pro= ceeding further. This is the literal mechanism behind "this user program tr= ied to access kernel memory and was stopped.")

    Step 5 =E2=80=94 Address computation. Both checks passed. The ad= dress generator computes: physical address =3D Base + offset =3D 0x00000000 + 0x0= 0002500 =3D 0x00002500.

    Step 6 =E2=80=94 The address goes out on the bus. This 32-bit va= lue is placed on the (32-wire, for a 386-class chip) address bus. RAM's dec= oder =E2=80=94 built from the same AND-gate-tree principle as the foundatio= nal document, just scaled to 32 input wires and however many byte-groups th= is RAM chip has =E2=80=94 activates the one byte-group at address 0x00002500, and (READ being asserted) its contents appear on the data bus, which th= e EU reads into AL.

    Notice what didn't change, compared to the 8086's real-mode stor= y: the RAM chip, the decoder, the data bus =E2=80=94 all of it is = identical in kind to the foundational document's description. Everything new is upstream of the address bus =E2=80=94 inside= the BIU, in the address-generation circuitry =E2=80=94 exactly as Part 2.5= predicted: the protection mechanism had to live in the only path = memory accesses take, and that path is entirely on the CPU side; RAM never = knows or cares whether it's being addressed by a real-mode formula or a pro= tected-mode descriptor-based computation. It just sees a number on the addr= ess bus and activates the matching byte-group, as it always has.

    Part 9 =E2= =80=94 Privilege levels: CPL, DPL, RPL, and the actual rule

    We've = used CPL and DPL informally above; let's now define all three precisely, an= d state the actual rule the hardware applies.

    DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level) =E2=80=94 we've met this: 2 bit= s, stored in byte 5 of a descriptor (Part 7), set by the OS when i= t builds the GDT. It answers: "what privilege level does someone need, to b= e allowed to use this segment?" A kernel code segment's descriptor= would have DPL=3D0. A descriptor describing memory that user programs are = allowed to use would have DPL=3D3.

    CPL (Current Privilege Level) =E2=80=94 this is not sto= red in a descriptor; it's a property of the currently executing code, specifically: CPL is defined as the RPL field (bits 1-0, Part 5= ) of whatever selector is currently loaded in CS. Recall,= CS:IP is what the BIU uses for instruction fetching =E2=80=94 so "the curr= ently executing code" literally means "the code whose address CS:IP current= ly points into," and CPL is just that code segment's selector's RPL bit= s, read live from CS.

    This i= s an elegant unification: there is no separate "privilege register.= " The CPU's current privilege level is encoded inside the very= same selector that's already sitting in CS for instruction-fetching p= urposes. When the OS sets up a code segment descriptor with DPL=3D0 and the= n arranges for CS to be loaded with a selector pointing to that descriptor = with RPL=3D0 (Part 5's bits 1-0), the CPU is, simply by virtue of = executing code from that segment, automatically "at CPL=3D0" =E2=80=94 no s= eparate step required.

    RPL (Requested Privilege Level) =E2=80=94 the bits 1-0 of an= y selector (not just CS's). When a piece of code loads, say, DS with s= ome selector, that selector's RPL bits represent: "I am requesting access <= em>as if I were running at this privilege level" =E2=80=94 which, for = almost all ordinary code, will simply be the same as CPL (a Ring-3= program naturally uses RPL=3D3 selectors for everything, because that's th= e privilege level it is). RPL exists as a separate, settable field mainly to handle a specific edge case: the kernel (CPL=3D0) somet= imes needs to access memory on behalf of a user program (e.g., cop= ying data from a user buffer into a kernel structure during a system call) = =E2=80=94 and the kernel can deliberately use a selector with RPL= =3D3 for that specific access, effectively saying "even though I (= the kernel) am CPL=3D0, perform this access as if requested by a R= ing-3 program" =E2=80=94 preventing the kernel from accidentally u= sing its own elevated privilege to touch memory the user program itself wou= ldn't be allowed to touch (a real security mechanism, not just a formality)= .

    The actual rule, stated precisely: For a memory access to be all= owed, the hardware computes max(CPL, RPL) =E2=80=94 call t= his the "effective privilege level" of the request =E2=80=94 and requires <= strong>effective privilege level =E2=89=A4 DPL (numerically =E2=80= =94 remember, 0 is most privileged, 3 is least, so "=E2= =89=A4" here means "at least as privileged as," which reads backwards from = normal number intuition but is consistent: a smaller number means = more privilege, so requiring the requester's level to be numer= ically =E2=89=A4 the descriptor's required level means "the requester = is at least as privileged as required").

    In our= Step 4 worked example: CPL=3D0, RPL=3D0 (from selector 0x10's bits 1-0, w= hich were 00), so effective level =3D max(0,0) =3D 0. DPL=3D0. Is 0 =E2=89= =A4 0? Yes. Access allowed =E2=80=94 matching what we asserted.

    If ins= tead the running code were at CPL=3D3 (a user program) and tried to load DS= with a selector pointing at this same descriptor (DPL=3D0, kernel= -only): effective level =3D max(3, RPL) =E2=80=94 and RPL can be at bes= t 3 as well (a CPL=3D3 program can't manufacture a selector with a lower RPL than its own CPL for this to help =E2=80=94 actually, more = precisely, even if it tried to construct a selector with RPL=3D0, the l= oading of that selector into a segment register is itself checked agai= nst CPL, and a CPL=3D3 program attempting to load a selector with RPL=3D0 w= ould itself be rejected =E2=80=94 but even setting that subtlety aside) =E2= =80=94 effective level =3D 3 (at best). Is 3 =E2=89=A4 0? No. Acces= s denied =E2=80=94 General Protection Fault.

    This s= ingle inequality =E2=80=94 max(CPL, RPL) =E2=89=A4 DPL =E2= =80=94 is the entire mechanism that makes "kernel space" and "user= space" real, hardware-enforced concepts. The kernel's descriptors have DPL= =3D0. User programs run with CPL=3D3. The inequality fails for any user-pro= gram access to a DPL=3D0 descriptor, unconditionally, automati= cally, on every memory access, with the check happening as a = fixed part of Step 4 in Part 8's pipeline =E2=80=94 not as something any pi= ece of software decided to do, but as something the address-generation circ= uitry always does, for every access, because that circuit= ry is the only path (Part 2.5).

    Part 10 = =E2=80=94 The complete switching sequence, connecting back to the prefetch = queue

    We now= have every piece needed to walk through exactly what an OS does t= o go from real mode to protected mode =E2=80=94 and, crucially, why each step is necessary, with direct callbacks to the 8086 document.

    Step 1 =E2=80=94 Build the GDT in memory. Before switching anyth= ing, the OS (still running in real mode, where it can use ordinary real-mod= e addressing to write to memory anywhere in the 1MB it can reach) construct= s an array of 8-byte descriptors (Part 7) at some chosen location =E2=80=94= typically at least: a "null descriptor" (GDT entry 0 is, by convention, ne= ver used for actual access =E2=80=94 Intel reserves it, and loading selecto= r 0x00 into a segment register is specifically not an error, used = as a deliberate "this segment register is unused" marker), a code descripto= r (e.g., our Part 7 example: base=3D0, limit=3D4GB, DPL=3D0, executable), a= nd a data descriptor (similar, but Type bits indicating data instead of cod= e).

    Step 2 =E2=80=94 LGDT. Load GDTR with the base address and size= of this array (Part 6). The protected-mode address generator now knows= where to look, but PE is still 0 =E2=80=94 it isn't being used yet.

    Step 3 =E2=80=94 Set PE in CR0 (Part 4). The instant this execut= es, the address-generation selector circuit switches from real-mode generat= or to protected-mode generator. But here is the subtlety: = recall the prefetch queue from the 8086 document (Part 3) =E2=80=94 the BIU= may have already fetched several bytes ahead, decoded under the <= em>old (real-mode) assumptions. More subtly: even the instruction = immediately following the mov cr0, eax that set PE might already be s= itting, fetched, in the queue =E2=80=94 fetched before PE was set,= but not yet executed.

    Why this matters: CS =E2=80=94 and its shadow base/limit/etc. = =E2=80=94 was loaded a long time ago, under real-mode rules (where "shadow"= information, if it's tracked at all, simply mirrors the real-mode segment= =C3=9716 base directly). The moment PE flips to 1, CS's select= or value (whatever raw 16-bit number it happens to hold from real mode= ) is now supposed to be interpreted as a protected-mode selector = =E2=80=94 index/TI/RPL fields, pointing into the GDT =E2=80=94 but = CS's shadow information was never populated via a GDT lookup, beca= use no GDT lookup ever happened for CS (CS was last loaded back in real mod= e, when "loading CS" meant something entirely different).

    Step 4 =E2=80=94 An immediate far jump. This is why, universally= , the instruction immediately following the mov cr0, eax that set= s PE is a far jump (JMP with an explicit new CS and new IP) =E2=80=94 e.g., jumping to a label within the very same code, b= ut specifying CS explicitly via a selector this time (say, selecto= r 0x08= , pointing at GDT entry 1, our code descriptor).

    This f= ar jump accomplishes two things simultaneously, both essential:

    (a= ) It flushes the prefetch queue (Part 3 of the 8086 d= ocument) =E2=80=94 any bytes sitting in the queue, fetched under whatever a= ddressing was active before, are discarded, and the BIU fetches fresh, = starting from the new CS:IP, using the protected-mode address generator (since PE is now 1).

    (b= ) It performs a bona fide CS load with a real selecto= r =E2=80=94 0x08 =E2=80=94 which triggers the GDT lookup= (Part 8's Step 2) for CS, populating CS's shadow with the code descriptor'= s base/limit/DPL, and =E2=80=94 per Part 9 =E2=80=94 setting CPL to this se= lector's RPL (typically 0, for the kernel's own code segment).

    Without this far jump, the CPU would continue executing from whe= rever the (now-stale, real-mode-fetched) prefetch queue left off, with CS's= shadow information still reflecting whatever real mode had set up =E2=80= =94 a state that is simply undefined/invalid under the protected-m= ode address generator's rules, and would almost certainly produce a General= Protection Fault on the very next instruction.

    Step 5 =E2=80=94 Reload the other segment registers. DS, SS, ES,= etc. still hold their real-mode raw values too =E2=80=94 these ne= ed to be explicitly reloaded with valid selectors (e.g., 0x10 for= the data descriptor), each such load triggering its own GDT lookup (Step 2= of Part 8) and populating that register's shadow correctly.

    After Step 5, the CPU is genuinely, fully "in protected mode": P= E=3D1, CS/DS/SS/ES all hold valid selectors with correctly-populated shadow= s, CPL is established (typically 0, since the OS is doing all this setup), = and every subsequent memory access follows the full Part 8 pipeline =E2=80= =94 limit checks, privilege checks, descriptor-based base addresses, all of= it.

    Part 11 = =E2=80=94 Closing the loop: the 286's dead end, and the 386's fix

    Now we= can resolve the apparent contradiction from much earlier (the "HIMEM.SYS s= witches back to real mode, but switching back requires a reset" puzzle).

    On the 286, the circuitry described in Part 4 =E2=80=94 the PE-b= it-controlled selector between two address generators =E2=80=94 was, in the= actual silicon, only designed to go one direction reliably. Setting PE from 0=E2=86=921 (Part 10) worked exactly as described. But t= he reverse =E2=80=94 clearing PE back to 0 =E2=80=94 while it = did flip the address-generator-selector back to "real mode," l= eft various other pieces of internal state (much of it related to = how the 286 internally tracked CPL and segment shadows, mechanisms that rea= l mode has no concept of and no way to "clean up") in configuration= s that the real-mode circuitry wasn't designed to encounter =E2=80= =94 essentially, real mode's circuitry assumed it would only ever run start= ing from "freshly powered on" conditions, and the 286's designers didn't bu= ild in a path for "real mode, but with leftover protected-mode-era internal= state lying around."

    The practical fix available on the 286 was: trigger a hardware r= eset =E2=80=94 a signal that forces all internal state, c= hip-wide, back to its power-on defaults (PE=3D0 among everything else) =E2= =80=94 and have the reset circuitry, as part of its very last steps, check = a special location in memory that the OS pre-arranged before trigg= ering the reset, and jump directly back into the OS's code= (rather than going through the full BIOS startup sequence a "real" power-o= n reset would normally trigger) =E2=80=94 a technique sometimes nicknamed (= somewhat informally, in period documentation) "warm reset" or "shutdown res= et," distinguishing it from a true cold power-on. This is what HIMEM.SYS ac= tually did: not "switch back to real mode" in the gentle sense Part 10 migh= t suggest for the forward direction, but "trigger this special res= et-and-immediately-jump-back sequence" =E2=80=94 which, from the outsid= e, looked like "briefly went to protected mode and came back,= " but internally involved this reset-based mechanism, with all the= overhead and fragility that "trigger a reset, but then skip most of what r= eset normally does" implies. This is also the origin of "technically invali= d" (Q41 from much earlier) =E2=80=94 Intel's documentation describes PE goi= ng 1=E2=86=920 as something that requires a reset to recover from = cleanly; relying on the reset-and-jump-back trick to simulate<= /em> a clean return without a true full reset was a documented tec= hnique, but one that depended on details of how the reset circuitr= y happened to be implemented =E2=80=94 details Intel made no promise to pre= serve, hence "invalid" in the sense of "unsupported, works by happenstance,= could break on a different 286-compatible chip."

    On the 386, Intel fixed this directly: the 386's internal state-= management was redesigned such that clearing PE (1=E2=86=920) does= cleanly restore real-mode operation, with no leftover-state problems, and = no reset required. This is a big part of why DOS = extenders (Q43-44) became practical on the 386 in a way they fundamentally = weren't on the 286 =E2=80=94 a DOS extender needs to switch into protected = mode (to access more memory, use 32-bit registers, etc.), do its work, and = then switch back to real mode cheaply and repeatedly (pot= entially many times per second) to make BIOS/DOS calls (which only function= in real mode) =E2=80=94 and "cheaply and repeatedly" was simply incompatib= le with "requires triggering a reset" on the 286, but became entirely feasi= ble on the 386.

    What this = document establishes, and the direct line to your OS project

    Every = term that was floating, unanchored, in the original CPU-evolution timeline = now has a complete mechanism behind it. "Mode" is two physical address-gene= ration circuits and a selector bit (PE in CR0). "Selector," "descriptor," a= nd "GDT entry" are precisely related (Part 5/Part 6/Part 7), down to the by= te. The "kernel/user boundary" is the inequality max(CPL,RPL)=E2=89=A4DPL, = evaluated by hardware on every memory access, in the one place =E2=80=94 th= e address-generation circuitry =E2=80=94 that cannot be bypassed, = which is why it's hardware-enforced rather than a convention (Part= 2's entire argument). And the precise sequence your bootloader executes = =E2=80=94 build descriptors, LGDT, set CR0's bit 0, immediate far jump, re= load segment registers =E2=80=94 now has a reason for every single= step, including the otherwise-mysterious "why does a far jump have to come= right after setting PE" (Part 10, tying directly back to the prefetch queu= e from the 8086 document).

    The ne= xt natural document, when you're ready, is paging =E2=80= =94 the second major addition (after segmentation/GDT) to how addr= esses get translated, introduced on the 386 alongside the wider registers, = and the mechanism that ultimately makes "each process has its own private 4= GB address space" a reality. But that's for next time =E2=80=94 this is a s= ubstantial amount to sit with first.

    =EE=81=96=EE=80=BB=EE=83=81
    =

    You said: The RAM explanation, and especially the decoder part was real= ly helpful in makking myself sure that the RAM itself considers the binary = sequence in the address=E2=80=A6

    <= div class=3D"group/thumbnail" data-testid=3D"file-thumbnail">

    The RAM explanation, and especially the decoder part was reall= y helpful in makking myself sure that the RAM itself considers the binary s= equence in the address bus as a 20-bit binary number only, with no special = bitwise encoding as in this bit means this etc (please confirm though). Now= , about the address being generated, the other end of the address bus. The = segmentation unit's logic you explained. Fine. As I said before, I understo= od how it worked. But after reading theough further and getting clarified a= bout RAM part, and that RAM itself didn't decode any other specific encodii= ng except the usual binary number one. But at the time pf address generatio= n, yo locate a particular RAM cell, the go to a particular address, we woul= d have to load the segment and offset registers with some values, right? My= real question was, now I feel like I might be able to articulate, that how= do wedecide what values to choose for the segment register and what for th= e offset address in order to computer our desired result. And I feel like t= o be able to actually do it, we must be able to make physiical sense of the= terms segment and offset, like what they actually mean in terms of the phy= sical RAM space, a group of RAM of cells?, a particular distance from a RAM= cell?, etc.

    Show more
    Jun 16

    Claude responded: Yes = =E2=80=94 confirmed completely.

    Synthesized segment/offset physical meaning and practical= value selection methodology
    Yes =E2= =80=94 confirmed completely. RAM's decoder does exactly one thing: it treat= s the pattern on the address bus as a single binary number, and activates t= he one byte-group whose sequential position in the array matches that numbe= r. No segment, no offset, no special encoding. A 20-bit pattern 10100011010111110= 000 arrives on the 20 wires; the decoder activates byte-group number= 667,632 (whatever that binary number decodes to in decimal); done. The dec= oder has no knowledge that this number was produced by combining a= segment and an offset =E2=80=94 it simply cannot tell, because the combina= tion already happened before the signal reached the address bus.

    Now to= your real question =E2=80=94 what do segment and offset actually mean<= /em> in terms of physical RAM space, and how do you choose values to reach = a desired location.

    What "segm= ent" means physically

    The RA= M array has 1,048,576 byte-groups, numbered 0 through 1,048,575. You can pi= cture these as a single long row of boxes, labeled 0, 1, 2, 3, ... stretchi= ng out to box number 1,048,575.

    The segment register value, multiplied by 16, gives you the physical position number of one specific box =E2=80=94 the box= where a particular region of RAM begins. That is all a segment re= gister value means: it identifies one specific box in that long row as a starting point.

    Let's = be precise: if the segment register holds the value 0x1000 (which is 4,096 = in decimal), then segment =C3=97 16 =3D 4,096 =C3=97 16 =3D 65,536 =3D 0x10= 000. This means: the region of RAM this segment register is describing = begins at box number 65,536.

    The se= gment register value, multiplied by 16, is sometimes called the seg= ment base =E2=80=94 the physical address of the first box= of the region this segment describes.

    Notice= one consequence of the =C3=9716: the segment base is always a mul= tiple of 16. If the segment register holds 0x1000, the base is 65,536 =E2= =80=94 a multiple of 16. If it holds 0x1001, the base is 65,552 =E2=80=94 a= lso a multiple of 16. You cannot use a segment register to point a= segment's start at box 65,537, or box 65,540, or any address that is n= ot divisible by 16. The lowest-addressable starting position is box 0,= the next is box 16, then box 32, and so on =E2=80=94 always jumping by 16.= These 16-byte-aligned positions are the only places a segment can start. T= his is the "16-byte paragraph boundary" constraint from the earlier section= , now grounded in the physical picture.

    What "offs= et" means physically

    The offset is literally: how many boxes past the segment= base you want to reach.

    If the= segment base is box 65,536 and the offset is 42, you want box 65,536 + 42 = =3D box 65,578. The offset is a distance =E2=80=94 measured in box= es (bytes) =E2=80=94 from the segment's starting box to the specific box yo= u actually want.

    Since = the offset register is 16 bits, its maximum value is 65,535. This means the= furthest box you can reach from any given segment base is base + 65,53= 5 =E2=80=94 which is 65,536 boxes (64KB) of range from any one segment= base. That is where the "64KB segment limit" comes from physically: not a = rule anyone decided to impose, but simply the consequence of "offset is 16 = bits, so the largest distance you can specify is 65,535 boxes."

    So in = the physical picture:

    • = Segment register =E2=86=92 specifies where a region of RAM begins (the segment base), expressed as a multiple-of-16 box numb= er divided by 16
    • = Offset register =E2=86=92 specifies how far into that = region the specific box is

    And th= e formula physical address =3D (segment =C3=97 16) + offset is just: start= -of-region + distance-into-region =3D the specific box.

    How you ch= oose values to reach a desired physical address

    Suppos= e you want to access physical box number 0x2A3F0 (which is 172,016 in decim= al =E2=80=94 a completely arbitrary example).

    You ne= ed segment and offset such that: (segment =C3=97 16) + offset =3D 0x2A3F0<= /p>

    This i= s one equation with two unknowns =E2=80=94 which is exactly why there are <= em>multiple valid choices, as established earlier. Here is how to thin= k through the options:

    The most natural choice: maximize the segment, minimize the offset.

    Take t= he physical address you want (0x2A3F0) and ask: what is the largest segment= base that is =E2=89=A4 this address and is a multiple of 16? The = answer is simply the address itself rounded down to the nearest multipl= e of 16 =E2=80=94 but 0x2A3F0 is already a multiple of 16 (its last he= x digit is 0, and any hex number ending in 0 is a multiple of 16, per the e= arlier shift-by-4 discussion). So the largest valid segment base is exactly= 0x2A3F0.

    Divide= that by 16 to get the segment register value: 0x2A3F0 =C3=B7 16 =3D 0x2A3F= . So if you set segment =3D 0x2A3F, offset =3D 0x0000, you get:

    (0x2A3= F =C3=97 16) + 0 =3D 0x2A3F0 + 0 =3D 0x2A3F0 =E2=9C=93

    A second valid choice: pull the segment base back, push the offset forwar= d.

    Suppos= e you want the segment base to be 0x2A000 instead (maybe because that's a c= onvenient round number, or because your data region started there). Segment= register value =3D 0x2A000 =C3=B7 16 =3D 0x2A00. Then:

    offset= =3D 0x2A3F0 - 0x2A000 =3D 0x03F0

    So seg= ment =3D 0x2A00, offset =3D 0x03F0:

    (0x2A0= 0 =C3=97 16) + 0x03F0 =3D 0x2A000 + 0x03F0 =3D 0x2A3F0 =E2= =9C=93

    The general rule: Choose any segment base that is (a) a multiple= of 16, and (b) =E2=89=A4 your target physical address, and (c) at most 65,= 535 boxes before your target (so the offset doesn't overflow 16 bi= ts). Then the offset is just target_physical_address - segment_base. Any (= segment, offset) pair satisfying these three conditions is valid.

    How progra= mmers actually use this =E2=80=94 the physical picture in practice

    The ab= stract question "how do I choose values" has a concrete, practical answer i= n the way real programs are actually loaded and run.

    When D= OS loads a program from disk into RAM, it picks a starting physical address= =E2=80=94 say, 0x10000 (box number 65,536) =E2=80=94 and copies the progra= m's bytes there. The program's code starts at box 65,536. The program's dat= a starts maybe 512 bytes later, at box 66,048. The stack will grow downward= from box 131,072 (one 64KB region's worth up from the start), or whatever = the loader decides.

    The lo= ader then sets the segment registers accordingly:

    • = CS (Code Segment) =3D 0x1000, because 0x1000 =C3=97 16 =3D 0x10000 =3D box = 65,536 =3D exactly where the code bytes are sitting in RAM. So CS:IP points= the BIU at the program's first instruction.
    • = DS (Data Segment) =3D maybe also 0x1000 for a small-model program where cod= e and data share one 64KB region. The program's own local data (its variabl= es) are at various offsets from 0x10000 =E2=80=94 maybe the first variable = is at offset 0x0200 (box 65,536 + 512 =3D box 66,048), the next at offset 0= x0202, and so on. Since DS =3D 0x1000, MOV AX, [0x0200] gives physical add= ress 0x10000 + 0x0200 =3D 0x10200 =3D box 66,048. Correct.

    The pr= ogram's code itself was compiled not knowing what physical address= it would be loaded at. The program contains instructions like MOV AX, [0x0200]<= /code> =E2=80=94 with the literal value 0x0200 baked into the machine code = bytes. This 0x0200 is an offset relative to wherever DS points. Th= e program trusts that, at runtime, DS will have been set up to point at "wh= erever my data actually started in physical RAM" =E2=80=94 and the loader's= job is to make that true, by computing the right segment register value fr= om the actual load address.

    This i= s the key physical picture: the segment register encodes "where in = physical RAM this program's region starts," and the program's own code only= uses offsets =E2=80=94 distances from that starting point. The se= gmentation unit combines these two pieces of information =E2=80=94 "where t= his program lives in physical RAM" (segment register, set by loader) + "whi= ch byte within this program's region" (offset, baked into the instruction b= y the compiler) =E2=80=94 to produce the actual physical box number for the= RAM decoder.

    What "segm= ent" actually is as a region of RAM

    Pullin= g it all together into the clearest possible picture:

    A segment =E2=80=94 the noun, the thing, the region =E2=80=94 is= a run of consecutive boxes in the RAM array, starting at some multiple-of-= 16 box number, and extending up to 64KB (65,536 boxes) from that starting p= oint. It is not a special kind of RAM, not a physically separate chip, not = demarcated by any hardware divider in the RAM array itself. It is simply: <= strong>a contiguous stretch of boxes, defined by two things =E2=80=94 where= it starts (the segment base, encoded in the segment register), and how far= it extends (always at most 64KB, limited by the 16-bit offset).

    Multip= le segments can overlap in physical RAM =E2=80=94 there is nothing= preventing segment A (base 0x10000) and segment B (base 0x10100) from both= covering box 0x10200 (segment A's offset 0x0200 and segment B's offset 0x0= 100 both reach it). This overlap is exactly the "multiple paths to one addr= ess" phenomenon, now seen in physical terms: two segments overlap = in the physical RAM array, so any box in their overlapping region is reacha= ble via either segment. No hardware prevents this; the RAM array doesn't kn= ow or care that two different segment registers might both point into the s= ame physical boxes.

    The of= fset, then, is just a distance measured in boxes from the segment's startin= g box =E2=80=94 nothing more exotic than that.

    Once t= he physical picture is clear =E2=80=94 "segment base is a physical RAM posi= tion, offset is a distance from there, the segmentation unit adds them to p= roduce a final position number for the decoder" =E2=80=94 every subsequent = concept (near pointers covering one 64KB stretch from one base, far pointer= s carrying both the base and the distance, the 640KB barrier being a conseq= uence of certain physical regions being reserved, the GDT replacing the =C3= =9716 formula with a table-lookup for the base) becomes variations on this = one physical picture. Ready to continue to Q19 onward when you are.

    =EE=83=BB=

    You said: Please list down all the sy= mbols, terminologies, etc.

    Please list down all the symbols, terminologies, etc. used in this resp= onse of yours, an exhaustive list

    Jun 17

    Claude responded: Here is = the exhaustive list, organized by category for clarity, covering everything= in the Real Mode / Protected Mode / GDT document.

    Systematically catalogued technical termin= ology across multiple document sections
    <= div class=3D"row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0">

    Here is the exhaustive list, organized by category for clarity, covering e= verything in the Real Mode / Protected Mode / GDT document.

    CPU Modes = and States

    • = Real mode
    • = Protected mode
    • = PE (Protection Enable)
    • = CPL=3D0
    • = CPL=3D3
    • = Ring 0
    • = Ring 3
    • = Kernel mode
    • = User mode
    • = 32-bit mode
    • = 16-bit mode
    • = Long mode (64-bit, mentioned briefly)

    Named Regi= sters

    • = CR0 (Control Register 0)
    • = CS (Code Segment)
    • = DS (Data Segment)
    • = SS (Stack Segment)
    • = ES (Extra Segment)
    • = FS
    • = GS
    • = IP (Instruction Pointer)
    • = CS:IP
    • = EAX
    • = SI (Source Index)
    • = SP (Stack Pointer)
    • = BP (Base Pointer)
    • = GDTR (GDT Register)

    Hardware U= nits and Components

    • = BIU (Bus Interface Unit)
    • = EU (Execution Unit)
    • = Segmentation unit
    • = Real-mode address generator
    • = Protected-mode address generator
    • = Selector circuit (the PE-controlled switch between the two generators)
    • = RAM decoder
    • = AND gate
    • = OR gate
    • = Prefetch queue
    • = Address bus
    • = Data bus
    • = Control bus
    • = Pins (chip package pins)
    • = Silicon die
    • = Integrated circuit
    • = Transistors
    • = Metal traces (internal wiring)
    • = READ signal (control bus signal)
    • = Byte-group (one addressable unit in RAM)

    Data Struc= tures

    • = GDT (Global Descriptor Table)
    • = LDT (Local Descriptor Table)
    • = Descriptor (=3D GDT entry =E2=80=94 a single 8-byte structure)
    • = Segment descriptor
    • = Null descriptor (GDT entry 0)
    • = Code descriptor
    • = Data descriptor
    • = System descriptor
    • = TSS (Task State Segment)
    • = IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table =E2=80=94 referenced in context of exceptio= ns)
    • = Shadow register / Hidden portion (of a segment register)
    • = GDT descriptor (the 6-byte base+limit structure passed to LGDT =E2=80=94 di= stinct from a segment descriptor)

    Fields Wit= hin the Selector (16-bit value in a segment register)

    • = Index (bits 15=E2=80=933 of a selector)
    • = TI (Table Indicator =E2=80=94 bit 2 of a selector)
    • = RPL (Requested Privilege Level =E2=80=94 bits 1=E2=80=930 of a selector)

    Fields Wit= hin a Descriptor (the 8-byte GDT entry)

    • = Base (32 bits, spread across bytes 2, 3, 4, 7)
    • = Limit (20 bits, spread across bytes 0=E2=80=931 and low nibble of byte 6)
    • = P (Present =E2=80=94 bit 7 of byte 5)
    • = DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level =E2=80=94 bits 6=E2=80=935 of byte 5)
    • = S (System/Segment flag =E2=80=94 bit 4 of byte 5)
    • = Type (bits 3=E2=80=930 of byte 5)
    • = G (Granularity =E2=80=94 bit 7 of byte 6)
    • = D/B (Default operation size =E2=80=94 bit 6 of byte 6)
    • = L (Long mode =E2=80=94 bit 5 of byte 6)
    • = AVL (Available =E2=80=94 bit 4 of byte 6)
    • = Access byte (the whole of byte 5)
    • = Segment base
    • = Segment limit
    • = 4KB units (the unit the Limit is measured in when G=3D1)
    • = Byte offset (of a descriptor within the GDT array)

    Privilege = Level Terminology

    • = CPL (Current Privilege Level)
    • = DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level)
    • = RPL (Requested Privilege Level)
    • = Effective privilege level (=3D max(CPL, RPL))
    • = max(CPL, RPL) =E2=80=94 the actual hardware rule
    • = Privilege check
    • = Limit check

    Instructio= ns and Instruction Forms

    • = MOV CR0, <register> (the privileged control-register load form)
    • = MOV eax, cr0
    • = OR eax, 1
    • = MOV cr0, eax
    • = LGDT (Load GDT Register)
    • = MOV AL, [SI] (example memory-access instruction)
    • = MOV DS, AX (segment register load =E2=80=94 triggers GDT lookup)
    • = JMP (far jump =E2=80=94 the instruction immediately after PE is set)
    • = Far jump (jump specifying both a new CS selector and a new IP)
    • = Queue flush (side effect of a far jump)

    Assembler = Directives (in the LGDT code example)

    • = dw (define word =E2=80=94 2 bytes)
    • = dd (define doubleword =E2=80=94 4 bytes)
    • = gdt_descriptor (label)
    • = gdt_start (label)
    • = gdt_end (label)

    Exceptions= and Interrupts

    • = General Protection Fault (GPF)
    • = Interrupt vector 13
    • = Fault (general term for a CPU-generated exception)
    • = Segmentation fault (the user-visible name for a GPF caused by a segment lim= it/privilege violation)
    • = Hardware reset (a signal that forces all CPU internal state to power-on def= aults)
    • = Warm reset / Shutdown reset (the 286-era technique of triggering a reset bu= t jumping back immediately)
    • = Cold reset / Cold power-on reset (a true full reset from power-off)
    • = Double fault (vector 8 =E2=80=94 briefly referenced in passing)

    Key Concep= ts and Mechanisms

    • = Mode (as a hardware concept =E2=80=94 which of two physical address-generat= ion circuits is currently connected)
    • = Mode switch (the act of changing PE in CR0)
    • = Retroactive naming (why "real mode" got its name after protected mode exist= ed)
    • = Backward compatibility (why real mode was preserved as an option on the 286= )
    • = Hardware-enforced protection (protection implemented in circuitry, not soft= ware convention)
    • = Software-based protection (why this cannot work =E2=80=94 the argument in P= art 2)
    • = Voluntary check (the fatal flaw of software-based protection =E2=80=94 it c= an be skipped)
    • = Segment base (the physical starting address of a segment =3D segment regist= er value =C3=97 16 in real mode, or Base field from descriptor in protected= mode)
    • = Segment limit (the maximum valid offset within a segment)
    • = Flat segment / Flat memory model (a descriptor with base=3D0 and limit=3D4G= B, effectively covering all of RAM with no restriction)
    • = Segment (as a physical concept =E2=80=94 a contiguous run of byte-groups in= RAM, defined by a base and a limit)
    • = Offset (a distance in bytes from a segment base to a specific byte-group)
    • = Segment:offset (the two-part address format of real mode)
    • = Physical address (the final, single number that reaches the address bus and= the RAM decoder)
    • = Virtual address (briefly referenced in PAE context)
    • = GDT lookup (the sequence: selector =E2=86=92 index =C3=97 8 + GDTR.base =E2= =86=92 read 8-byte descriptor)
    • = Descriptor caching / Shadow population (what happens when a segment registe= r is loaded with a new selector)
    • = Paragraph (a 16-byte-aligned starting position =E2=80=94 the only valid sta= rting points for real-mode segments)
    • = Paragraph boundary (=3D a physical address divisible by 16)
    • = Segment overlap (multiple segments covering the same physical byte-groups)<= /li>
    • = Selector value as byte-offset (when RPL=3D0 and TI=3D0, the raw selector nu= mber =3D index =C3=97 8 =3D byte offset within GDT)
    • = Stack frame (briefly referenced in BP context)
    • = Demand paging (briefly referenced as a future topic)
    • = Bank switching (referenced in EMS context)
    • = Context switching (referenced in TSS context)
    • = Per-program isolation (achieved via paging, not segmentation =E2=80=94 forw= ard reference)

    Privilege = and Access Control Concepts

    • = Kernel space
    • = User space
    • = Privilege level
    • = Privilege boundary
    • = Privilege ring
    • = Non-optional check (the key property that makes hardware enforcement meanin= gful)
    • = The only available path (the core argument for why protection must be in th= e address-generation circuitry)
    • = System call (mentioned as the controlled crossing-point of the privilege bo= undary)
    • = Kernel-on-behalf-of-user access (the RPL use-case)

    Historical= Chips Referenced

    • = 8086 (Intel, 1978)
    • = 8088 (Intel =E2=80=94 briefly mentioned)
    • = 80286 / 286 (Intel, 1982)
    • = 80386 / 386 (Intel, 1985)

    Software a= nd OS References

    • = DOS
    • = IBM PC
    • = BIOS
    • = HIMEM.SYS
    • = DOS extender
    • = Windows 95 (mentioned in flat-model context)
    • = Linux (mentioned in flat-model context)
    • = FreeBSD (mentioned in flat-model context)

    Memory Siz= e and Address Values (examples used in worked calculations)

    • = 0x10 (example selector value =E2=80=94 decoded as index=3D2, TI=3D0, RPL=3D= 0)
    • = 0x08 (example selector for GDT entry 1)
    • = 0x00001000 (example GDTR base address)
    • = 0x00001010 (computed address of GDT entry 2 in example)
    • = 0x00002500 (example SI value / offset in the Step 8 walkthrough)
    • = 0xFFFFFFFF (maximum 32-bit address / example limit value)
    • = 0x9A (example access byte in descriptor =E2=80=94 decodes to: P=3D1, DPL=3D= 0, S=3D1, Type=3Dexecutable-readable code)
    • = 0xCF (example flags byte in descriptor =E2=80=94 decodes to: G=3D1, D/B=3D1= , L=3D0, AVL=3D0, Limit[19:16]=3D0xF)
    • = FF FF 00 00 00 9A CF 00 (the complete 8-byte example descriptor in Part 7)<= /li>
    • = 4GB (=3D 2=C2=B3=C2=B2 =E2=80=94 the address space limit of 32-bit protecte= d mode)
    • = 1MB (=3D 2=C2=B2=E2=81=B0 =E2=80=94 the address space limit of real mode / = G=3D0 segment limit maximum)
    • = 64KB (=3D 2=C2=B9=E2=81=B6 =E2=80=94 the maximum offset range, and therefor= e the maximum single-segment size in real mode)
    • = 8,192 (=3D 2=C2=B9=C2=B3 =E2=80=94 the maximum number of GDT entries, from = the 13-bit Index field)
    • = 16 (the multiplier in the real-mode segment=C3=9716 formula, and the byte-s= ize of a paragraph)
    • = 6 bytes (size of the base+limit structure passed to LGDT)
    • = 8 bytes (size of one GDT descriptor/entry)
    • = 20 bits (width of the real-mode physical address)
    • = 24 bits (width of the 286's address bus)
    • = 32 bits (width of the 386's address bus and registers)
    • = 48 bits (width of x86-64's implemented address bus =E2=80=94 briefly refere= nced)
    • = 2=C2=B9=C2=B3 =3D 8,192
    • = 2=C2=B2=E2=81=B0 =3D 1,048,576
    • = 2=C2=B2=E2=81=B4 =3D 16,777,216
    • = 2=C2=B3=C2=B2 =3D 4,294,967,296

    Physical a= nd Electrical Terms

    • = Voltage (high =3D 1, low =3D 0)
    • = Wire
    • = Signal
    • = Bit (as a physical state: high/low voltage on one wire)
    • = Byte-group (the RAM array's fundamental addressable unit)
    • = Silicon
    • = Transistors
    • = Motherboard
    • = Circuit board trace

    Mathematic= al / Logical Operations Referenced

    • = Bitwise AND (AND gate =E2=80=94 used in the decoder and the PE-controlled s= elector)
    • = Bitwise OR (OR gate =E2=80=94 same)
    • = Shift left by 3 (how the selector's Index field maps to byte-offset: index = =C3=97 8 =3D index shifted left by 3)
    • = Shift left by 4 (the real-mode segment=C3=9716 formula =E2=80=94 multiplyin= g by 16 =3D shifting by 4)
