    Forbidden instruction / Privileged instruction =E2=80=94 An inst= ruction that the CPU will refuse to execute when CPL > 0. Examples: HLT= (halt the CPU =E2=80=94 a user program halting the entire machine would be= catastrophic), LGDT (loading a new GDT =E2=80=94 a user program replacing= the GDT would destroy all memory protection), IN/OUT to hardware ports (= when IOPL level requires it =E2=80=94 a user program directly reprogramming= the disk controller or interrupt controller could corrupt the entire syste= m), MO= V CR0, reg (changing control registers =E2=80=94 a user program clea= ring the PE bit would switch the CPU back to real mode).

    Kernel space =E2=80=94 The region of memory (and the CPU mode) t= hat belongs exclusively to the kernel. The kernel's code, the kernel's data= structures, the GDT, the IDT =E2=80=94 all live in kernel space, protected= by DPL=3D0 on their descriptors (in the segmentation scheme) or by page-le= vel protection bits (in the paging scheme, which we'll cover next). User pr= ograms cannot read or write kernel space =E2=80=94 any attempt produces a p= rotection fault.

    User space =E2=80=94 The region of memory and the CPU mode in wh= ich user programs run. Each user program has its own user-space region =E2= =80=94 its code, data, heap, and stack =E2=80=94 protected from other user = programs (also by paging, coming next). A user program cannot access anothe= r user program's memory, and cannot access kernel space.

    System call =E2=80=94 The controlled, hardware-enforced mechanis= m by which a user program (running in Ring 3) asks the kernel (running in R= ing 0) to perform a privileged operation on its behalf. A user program cann= ot directly open a file, write to the network, or allocate memory =E2=80=94= these require hardware access that only Ring 0 permits. Instead, the progr= am executes a special instruction (INT 0x80 on older Linux, SYSCALL on mo= dern x86-64) that deliberately triggers a controlled transition to Ring 0, = the kernel handles the request, and then the CPU returns to Ring 3 to resum= e the user program. This is the only legitimate way to cross from Ring 3 to= Ring 0.

    IOPL =E2=80=94 I/O Privilege Level =E2=80=94 Two bits in the FLA= GS register (introduced with the 286) that specify the minimum privilege le= vel allowed to execute IN and OUT instructions (direct hardware port acce= ss). If the running code's CPL is numerically greater than IOPL, executing = IN/OUT causes a protection fault. Normally IOPL=3D0, meaning only Ring 0 ca= n access hardware ports directly.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    Where we are in = the story

    The in= terrupt document established the mechanism by which the CPU can be temporar= ily redirected =E2=80=94 a device asserts an IRQ, the CPU saves state, jump= s to an ISR, handles the event, and returns. The timer interrupt specifical= ly gives the OS guaranteed CPU access every few milliseconds, enabling pree= mptive multitasking.

    The me= mory bus and segmentation documents established that in real mode, every pr= ogram has unrestricted access to every physical address. The GDT and protec= ted mode document showed how descriptor-based limit and privilege checks ca= n restrict which memory addresses a given piece of code can reach.

    But th= ere is still a missing piece: the protected mode privilege system requires = that some code has privilege level 0 (it can set up the GDT, confi= gure the IDT, manage memory) and other code has privilege level 3 = (it cannot do these things). But we have not yet fully explained:

    1. = What exactly is different about the CPU when CPL=3D0 versus CPL=3D= 3 =E2=80=94 beyond memory access
    2. = How CPL is stored and tracked
    3. = How CPL changes =E2=80=94 who can change it, when, how
    4. = Why a software-only solution to this problem cannot work
    5. = Where this concept historically came from and why

    Let's = build all of this from the ground up.

    The fundamental = problem: multiple programs, one machine, no trust

    In the= single-program world (early batch computers, early personal computers like= the 8086 running DOS), there was no problem to solve. One program ran. It = had full access to everything. If it misbehaved, the worst that happened wa= s that program crashed and you restarted the computer.

    In the= time-sharing world =E2=80=94 multiple programs running simultaneously on o= ne machine, often submitted by different users who do not know or trust eac= h other =E2=80=94 everything changes.

    Consid= er a university computer in the early 1960s. It serves dozens of students s= imultaneously. Student A is running a program that computes chemistry calcu= lations. Student B is running a program that sorts names. Student C =E2=80= =94 perhaps a malicious student, or simply a careless one with a bug =E2=80= =94 is running a program with a loop that writes random values to memory ad= dresses, including addresses that happen to contain Student A's data and th= e operating system's own code and data structures.

    The co= nsequences:

    • = Student A's computation is silently corrupted =E2=80=94 the answers are wro= ng
    • = The operating system's own memory is overwritten =E2=80=94 the entire machi= ne crashes
    • = The only recovery is a full restart, interrupting everyone

    This i= s not a theoretical concern. It happened constantly on early time-sharing s= ystems. The machines were expensive, shared by many people, and frequently = crashed due to one user's program corrupting another's data or the OS's own= structures.

    The question is: what mechanism can prevent this?

    Why "just be car= eful" fails

    The fi= rst attempted answer was: educate programmers to not write code that access= es memory outside their own area. Write your code correctly, and it won't t= ouch other programs' memory.

    This f= ails for three reasons that are not overcome by any amount of education or = effort:

    Reason 1 =E2=80=94 Bugs are universal. Every non-trivial program= has bugs. A bug, by definition, is code that does something the programmer= did not intend. A programmer cannot prevent their code from having bugs by= trying harder =E2=80=94 if bugs could be eliminated by trying, they would = not exist. A program with a bug might compute a wrong address =E2=80=94 not= because the programmer meant to access that address, but because a calcula= tion somewhere went wrong and produced an incorrect value that was then use= d as an address.

    Reason 2 =E2=80=94 Malice exists. Even if all bugs could be elim= inated, a deliberate attacker can write code that intentionally reads anoth= er program's memory (to steal data =E2=80=94 passwords, private documents) = or intentionally writes to the OS's memory (to gain control of the machine)= . "Just be careful" is irrelevant to someone who is deliberately trying to = break the rules.

    Reason 3 =E2=80=94 Verification is impossible. Even if you wante= d to verify that a program "only accesses its own memory," doing so in gene= ral is equivalent to the halting problem =E2=80=94 it is mathematically imp= ossible to write a program that, given any other program as input, correctl= y determines whether that program will ever access memory outside its bound= s. This is not a practical limitation =E2=80=94 it is a fundamental mathema= tical impossibility, proven as a direct consequence of Turing's 1936 work.<= /p>

    Why software enf= orcement fails

    Given = that "trust programmers" fails, the next attempt was: have the operating sy= stem enforce memory boundaries in software.

    The id= ea: before any memory write, the OS somehow checks "is this address within = the allowed range for this program?" and only proceeds if it passes.

    But th= ere is a fatal problem. Think carefully about what "the OS checks before ev= ery write" would require.

    When u= ser code executes MOV [BX], AX =E2=80=94 writing AX's value to the address= computed from DS:BX =E2=80=94 this instruction, once fetched from the inst= ruction queue and decoded by the EU, goes directly to the segmentation unit= , which computes a physical address, which goes directly onto the address b= us. The entire path from "instruction decoded" to "address on bus" is a dir= ect circuit. There is no natural pause point where software can be consulte= d.

    To ins= ert a software check, you would need to somehow interrupt this circuit =E2= =80=94 to redirect the instruction's execution through a "checking routine"= before allowing it to proceed. But this checking routine is itself code = =E2=80=94 it would also need to be checked, or it could be circumvented. An= d the code that checks the checker would need to be checked. The recursion = is infinite.

    More p= ractically: the ONLY way to have "check before execute" in software is to h= ave the PROGRAM ITSELF execute the checking code. Something like:

    asm
    ; Before every memory write, the program must do: cmp bx, allowed_lower_bound jl fault ; jump to error handler if below lower bou= nd cmp bx, allowed_upper_bound jg fault ; jump to error handler if above upper bou= nd mov [bx], ax ; only then proceed with the write<= /code>

    But no= tice: this checking code is inside the program itself. The= program can simply not include it. A buggy program has already produced a = wrong BX value =E2=80=94 it certainly did not correctly include safety chec= ks either. A malicious program deliberately omits the checks. The check is = only present if the programmer chose to include it, and absent if they did = not =E2=80=94 which is exactly the condition under which protection is need= ed.

    Softwa= re enforcement of memory protection is logically impossible because the enf= orcement code must be executed by the same entity being restricted, and tha= t entity can always find a way around code it executes.

    Why hardware enf= orcement is the only solution

    The on= ly mechanism that cannot be bypassed by the program being restricted is one= that operates at a level the program cannot reach =E2=80=94 specifically, = circuitry that sits in the mandatory path between "instruction decoded" and= "action performed," where no instruction can create an alternative route a= round it.

    This i= s the same argument made in the GDT/protected mode document for memory prot= ection via descriptor privilege checks. Now we extend it to a broader class= of restrictions: not just "can this program access this memory address" bu= t "can this program execute this type of instruction at all."

    The so= lution: build into the CPU's instruction decoder a permanent check =E2=80= =94 for every instruction, before executing it, check whether the current p= rivilege level allows this instruction. If not, do not execute it. Generate= a protection fault instead. This check is not code =E2=80=94 it is circuit= ry. The program being restricted cannot reach it, cannot modify it, cannot = bypass it by any sequence of instructions.

    This i= s hardware-enforced privilege levels =E2=80=94 the fundame= ntal mechanism that makes modern computing secure and stable.

    The historical o= rigin: the Atlas Supercomputer (1962)

    The co= ncept of hardware-enforced privilege levels did not appear with the x86. It= was invented over a decade earlier.

    The Atlas Computer =E2=80=94 designed at the University of Manch= ester, UK, and completed in 1962 =E2=80=94 was one of the first computers t= o implement the concept of a "supervisor mode" and "user mode" at the hardw= are level. The designers recognized exactly the problems described above an= d built a solution directly into the hardware.

    Atlas = had two operating modes:

    Supervisor mode =E2=80=94 equivalent to Ring 0. All instructions= permitted. The operating system (called the Supervisor on Atlas) ran here.=

    User mode =E2=80=94 equivalent to Ring 3. Certain instructions f= orbidden. User programs ran here.

    The me= chanism for switching between modes was triggered by specific events =E2=80= =94 a "supervisor call" instruction (equivalent to a system call) to enter = supervisor mode from user mode, and a return instruction to go back.

    The At= las concept was immediately influential. IBM incorporated similar concepts = into the System/360 (1964) =E2=80=94 which became one of the most important= and widely used computer architectures in history. The System/360 had "pri= vileged instructions" that could only be executed by the OS, and a "problem= state" versus "supervisor state" distinction.

    By the= time Intel designed the 286 in the early 1980s, hardware privilege levels = were a well-established concept in larger computers (mainframes and minicom= puters). The 286 was the first time these concepts appeared in a mass-marke= t personal computer processor.

    The four rings = =E2=80=94 why 4, and why only 2 are used

    The 28= 6 (and all subsequent x86 processors) implemented four privilege levels =E2= =80=94 Rings 0, 1, 2, and 3 =E2=80=94 stored in a 2-bit field (2 bits =3D 4= possible values, as established above).

    Intel's original design intent for all four rings:

    • = Ring 0: The OS kernel =E2=80=94 most trusted, unrestricted=
    • = Ring 1: OS services =E2=80=94 trusted but slightly restric= ted. The designers imagined certain OS components (device drivers, for exam= ple) that need more privilege than user programs but should not have unrest= ricted kernel-level access
    • = Ring 2: OS extensions =E2=80=94 similar idea, a further le= vel of trust
    • = Ring 3: User applications =E2=80=94 least trusted, most re= stricted

    This w= as a thoughtful design. In theory, you could have a layered OS where the co= re kernel is Ring 0, drivers are Ring 1, less trusted OS services are Ring = 2, and user programs are Ring 3. A Ring 1 driver could access certain hardw= are but could not modify the GDT. A Ring 2 service could call Ring 1 driver= s but couldn't directly access hardware.

    Why only Rings 0 and 3 are used in practice:

    The in= termediate rings (1 and 2) turned out to be difficult to use correctly in p= ractice. The rules for crossing between rings =E2=80=94 what can call what,= how parameters are passed across ring boundaries, how segment descriptors = with different DPL values interact =E2=80=94 are complex. Operating system = designers found it simpler and more practical to use only the two extremes:=

    • = Ring 0 for the kernel (trusted, unrestricted)
    • = Ring 3 for everything else (untrusted, restricted)

    The bo= undary between these two is clean: a Ring 3 program can only enter Ring 0 t= hrough the explicit, controlled system call mechanism. There is no ambiguou= s middle ground.

    Modern= x86 operating systems =E2=80=94 Linux, Windows, macOS =E2=80=94 all use on= ly Ring 0 and Ring 3. Rings 1 and 2 exist in the hardware specification but= are unused by all major operating systems.

    One exception: virtualization. Modern virtualization (running a = virtual machine inside a physical machine) sometimes uses Ring 1 to run the= guest OS's kernel, reserving Ring 0 for the hypervisor (the virtualization= software). But this is a special use case. Most virtualization today uses = dedicated hardware virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) that add n= ew privilege modes specifically for virtualization, rather than using Rings= 1 and 2.

    Where CPL is phy= sically stored

    In pro= tected mode on x86, the Current Privilege Level is stored in the lo= west 2 bits of the CS selector =E2=80=94 bits 1 and 0 of whatever = value is currently loaded in the CS register.

    Recall= from the GDT document that a selector's 16 bits are divided into three fie= lds:

    • = Bits 15=E2=80=933 (13 bits): Index into the GDT
    • = Bit 2: TI (Table Indicator =E2=80=94 GDT or LDT)
    • = Bits 1=E2=80=930: RPL (Requested Privilege Level)

    When C= S is loaded with a selector, the CPU's hardware reads bits 1=E2=80=930 of t= hat selector and stores them internally as CPL =E2=80=94 the current privil= ege level. This CPL is then used for every subsequent privilege check until= CS is loaded with a new value.

    So CPL= is not a separate register you can read or write independently. It is an i= mplicit property of the CS selector's lowest 2 bits. The privilege level is= determined by which code segment you are currently executing in = =E2=80=94 and the code segment is described by a GDT descriptor that the OS= set up, specifying the DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level) for that segment, = and the selector that the OS chose to load into CS, whose RPL bits determin= e CPL.

    A user= program's CS selector has bits 1=E2=80=930 =3D 11 (binary) =3D 3. So CPL= =3D3 whenever a user program is running.

    The ke= rnel's CS selector has bits 1=E2=80=930 =3D 00 (binary) =3D 0. So CPL=3D0 = whenever the kernel is running.

    The mo= ment a context switch happens =E2=80=94 the OS restoring a user program's s= aved register state =E2=80=94 CS gets loaded with the user program's select= or (RPL=3D3), and from that instant, CPL=3D3. The hardware knows, from that= CS load, that privilege has dropped to Ring 3.

    The complete set= of restrictions at Ring 3

    Here i= s the exhaustive list of what Ring 3 code cannot do, with the reason for ea= ch restriction:

    1 =E2=80=94 Cannot execute privileged instructions

    The CP= U's instruction decoder, before executing any instruction, checks: does thi= s instruction require CPL=3D0? If yes and CPL=E2=89=A00, the decoder does n= ot execute the instruction. Instead it generates a General Protection Fault= (interrupt vector 13).

    The pr= ivileged instructions on x86 include:

    • = HLT =E2=80=94 Halt the CPU. A user program halting the CPU would freeze th= e entire machine for all other users.
    • = LGDT / LLDT =E2=80=94 Load GDT, IDT, or LDT register. A user prog= ram replacing the GDT would destroy all memory protection immediately.
    • = MOV CR= 0, reg / MOV CR3, reg / MOV CR4, reg =E2=80=94 Write to control re= gisters. CR0 contains the PE bit (mode switch) and PG bit (paging enable). = CR3 contains the page table address. A user program modifying these would c= ollapse the entire memory management infrastructure.
    • = LMSW =E2=80=94 Load Machine Status Word (on 286 =E2=80=94 sets PE bit)
    • = INVLPG= =E2=80=94 Invalidate a TLB entry. (We'll cover TLB in the paging do= cument =E2=80=94 briefly, it's a cache of recent address translations. A us= er program invalidating TLB entries could force the system to re-read page = tables, potentially enabling certain attacks.)
    • = WRMSR<= /code> =E2=80=94 Write to Model-Specific Registers. These registers control= CPU features like frequency scaling, cache configuration, and debug facili= ties.
    • = CLTS =E2=80=94 Clear Task Switched flag in CR0. Related to floating-point = context switching.

    2 =E2=80=94 Cannot access memory outside its allowed regions

    This i= s enforced by the descriptor privilege checks (from the GDT document): if t= he running code has CPL=3D3 and tries to use a segment descriptor with DPL= =3D0, the privilege check fails (max(CPL, RPL) =3D 3 > DPL =3D 0) and a = General Protection Fault is generated.

    More c= oncretely in paging (which we'll cover next): the OS marks kernel memory pa= ges as "supervisor only." Attempting to access a supervisor-only page from = user mode (CPL=3D3) causes a Page Fault, which the OS handles by terminatin= g the offending process.

    3 =E2=80=94 Cannot directly access hardware ports (under default IOPL)

    IN an= d OUT<= /code> instructions access hardware device ports. Under the default configu= ration (IOPL=3D0), these instructions can only be executed when CPL =E2=89= =A4 IOPL =3D 0, meaning only Ring 0 can execute them. A Ring 3 program atte= mpting IN or OUT gets a General Protection Fault.

    This p= revents user programs from directly reprogramming the disk controller (pote= ntially corrupting storage), the interrupt controller (disrupting all inter= rupt handling), the keyboard controller (intercepting keystrokes from other= programs), or any other hardware device.

    4 =E2=80=94 Cannot change its own privilege level

    There = is no instruction like SET_CPL 0 =E2=80=94 no instruction that directly se= ts CPL to a lower number. CPL can only change through controlled, OS-superv= ised mechanisms:

    • = A system call instruction (SYSCALL, INT 0x80, SYSENTER) =E2=80=94 which = always jumps to a fixed OS entry point, never to arbitrary code
    • = An interrupt or exception =E2=80=94 which always jumps to the handler regis= tered in the IDT by the OS
    • = An IRET instruction returning to a higher privilege level (when the OS retu= rns to a user program after handling a system call or interrupt)

    In all= these cases, the transition is either:

    • = From Ring 3 =E2=86=92 Ring 0: only via a fixed, OS-controlled entry point (= the system call handler or interrupt handler). The user program cannot choo= se where Ring 0 execution starts =E2=80=94 the OS pre-registered the destin= ations in the IDT.
    • = From Ring 0 =E2=86=92 Ring 3: only the OS can do this, by executing IRET or= SYSRET with the appropriate saved CS value that has RPL=3D3.

    A user= program at Ring 3 cannot grant itself Ring 0 access. The mechanism simply = does not exist for code running at CPL=3D3.

    Privileged instr= uctions: the hardware check

    Let's = make this concrete. In the CPU's instruction decoder =E2=80=94 the circuitr= y that takes bytes from the instruction queue and determines what to do wit= h them =E2=80=94 there is logic equivalent to this for each instruction typ= e:

    For HLT (opcode 0xF4):

    When opcode 0xF4 is decoded:
  IF CPL =3D=3D 0:
      Halt the CPU. Stop fetching instructions. Wait for inter=
rupt.
  ELSE (CPL is 1, 2, or 3):
      Do not halt.
      Generate General Protection Fault (interrupt 13).
      Push error code, CS, IP, FLAGS onto stack.
      Look up vector 13 in IDT.
      Jump to the General Protection Fault handler.

    This c= heck is implemented in hardware =E2=80=94 in the transistor-level logic of = the instruction decoder, not in any piece of software. No instruction a pro= gram can execute can reach or modify this check. It is as fixed as the arit= hmetic of the ALU.

    For LGDT (opcodes 0x0F 0x01 /2):

    When LGDT is decoded:
  IF CPL =3D=3D 0:
      Read the 6-byte GDT base+limit structure from the memory=
 operand.
      Load that base and limit into the GDTR register.
  ELSE:
      Generate General Protection Fault.

    The GD= T cannot be replaced by unprivileged code. Period. Not by any sequence of i= nstructions. Not by any trick. The hardware will not allow it.

    The transition f= rom Ring 3 to Ring 0: system calls

    When a= user program needs the kernel to do something =E2=80=94 open a file, read = keyboard input, allocate memory, send network data =E2=80=94 it executes a = system call.

    On x86= Linux (32-bit), this is done via INT 0x80. Let's trace exactly what happe= ns, step by step:

    Before the system call =E2=80=94 setup

    The us= er program loads the system call number into EAX (each system call has a un= ique number =E2=80=94 e.g., 1 =3D sys_exit, 3 =3D sys_read, 4 =3D sys_write). Parameters are loaded into EBX, ECX, EDX, etc. Then the program execu= tes IN= T 0x80.

    Step 1 =E2=80=94 The CPU detects INT 0x80

    INT 0x80 is an explicit software interrupt =E2=80=94 deliberately triggering vec= tor 0x80. This is not a fault; it is intentional. The CPU processes it like= any interrupt:

    Step 2 =E2=80=94 Privilege level check for the INT instruction

    The CP= U checks the IDT entry for vector 0x80. Recall from the interrupt document = that IDT entries have a DPL field. The OS set up the vector 0x80 IDT entry = with DPL=3D3 =E2=80=94 specifically so that Ring 3 programs can trigger it.= (Vectors with DPL=3D0 in the IDT can be triggered by software INT only fro= m Ring 0 =E2=80=94 they're for kernel-internal use. Setting DPL=3D3 for a s= ystem call vector allows user programs to call it deliberately.)

    Step 3 =E2=80=94 Ring transition

    This i= s the critical step. The CPU sees that:

    • = The current CPL is 3 (we're in user code)
    • = The target code segment (the kernel's system call handler) has DPL=3D0

    A priv= ilege level change is happening: 3 =E2=86=92 0. The CPU performs a ring tra= nsition:

    1. = The CPU automatically switches to the kernel's stack (not = the user program's stack =E2=80=94 this is essential for security; the kern= el cannot trust the user's stack). The kernel's stack address is stored in = a special data structure called the TSS (Task State Segment), specifically in the SS0 and ESP0 fields (the stack for Ring 0).
    2. = The CPU pushes onto the kernel's stack (in this order):
      • = SS (the user's stack segment selector)
      • = ESP (the user's stack pointer =E2=80=94 so we can find our way back)
      • = EFLAGS (the user's flags register)
      • = CS (the user's code segment selector =E2=80=94 contains CPL=3D3 in its low = bits)
      • = EIP (the user's instruction pointer =E2=80=94 the return address)
    3. = CS is loaded with the kernel code segment selector from the IDT entry (DPL= =3D0, CPL becomes 0)
    4. = EIP is loaded with the offset from the IDT entry (the address of the system= call handler)

    From this moment, CPL=3D0. The kernel is now running.

    Step 4 =E2=80=94 Kernel executes the system call

    The sy= stem call handler reads EAX to find the system call number, looks up the ap= propriate function in the system call table, calls it with the parameters f= rom EBX/ECX/EDX, and performs the requested operation (file I/O, memory all= ocation, etc.) with full Ring 0 access.

    Step 5 =E2=80=94 Return to user mode

    The ke= rnel executes IRET (Interrupt Return). This pops from the kernel's stack:<= /p>

    • = EIP =E2=86=92 restores the user program's instruction pointer
    • = CS =E2=86=92 restores the user's code segment selector (with RPL=3D3 in its= low bits)
    • = EFLAGS =E2=86=92 restores the user's flags
    • = ESP =E2=86=92 restores the user's stack pointer
    • = SS =E2=86=92 restores the user's stack segment

    The mo= ment CS is restored, CPL becomes 3 again. The CPU is back in Ring 3. The us= er program resumes from exactly where it left off =E2=80=94 the instruction= immediately after INT 0x80 =E2=80=94 with the system call's result in EAX= .

    The us= er program was in Ring 3, briefly entered Ring 0 (only via the controlled g= ateway the OS registered in the IDT), the kernel did its work, and returned= control to Ring 3. At no point did the user program execute any Ring 0 cod= e of its choosing. It could only trigger the one specific entry point the O= S registered.

    This i= s the controlled crossing point of the privilege boundary. Every file read,= every network packet, every memory allocation in your programs =E2=80=94 e= very one of these ultimately works via this mechanism or the equivalent SYSCALL instruction on modern 64-bit systems.

    The TSS =E2=80= =94 Task State Segment

    The sy= stem call transition above mentioned the TSS. This needs explanation since = it appears without definition in that context.

    What the TSS is:

    The TS= S (Task State Segment) is a special data structure in memory =E2=80=94 a bl= ock of bytes at a fixed location, described by a descriptor in the GDT (jus= t like code and data segments have descriptors in the GDT). The CPU knows w= here the TSS is because a special register =E2=80=94 TR (Task Register) =E2= =80=94 holds a GDT selector that points to the TSS descriptor.

    What the TSS contains:

    The TS= S holds the CPU state for task switching =E2=80=94 the saved register value= s of a "task" (an OS-level concept for a unit of execution). It also holds,= critically for the system call mechanism:

    • = SS0: the stack segment selector for Ring 0 (kernel stack s= egment)
    • = ESP0: the stack pointer for Ring 0 (where the kernel's sta= ck is)

    When a= ring transition from Ring 3 to Ring 0 occurs (via interrupt, exception, or= system call), the CPU automatically reads SS0 and ESP0 from the TSS and sw= itches to that stack. This is how the kernel always has its own private sta= ck, separate from the user program's stack, ready to use immediately when a= ring transition occurs.

    The OS= sets up the TSS during initialization and keeps SS0/ESP0 updated whenever = it switches between processes (since each process has its own kernel stack)= .

    The General Prot= ection Fault =E2=80=94 what happens when protection fires

    Every = protection violation =E2=80=94 privileged instruction at Ring 3, memory acc= ess outside permitted range, accessing a not-present segment, and many othe= rs =E2=80=94 causes a General Protection Fault (GPF), which is x86 exceptio= n vector 13.

    The CP= U's response to a GPF (which we can now fully trace, with everything from t= he interrupt document combined with the privilege level knowledge):

    1. = Finish the current instruction's fetch. Do not execute the instruction that= caused the fault.
    2. = Push onto the current privilege level's stack (if we're cr= ossing to Ring 0 for the handler, push onto the kernel stack after switchin= g):
      • = Error code (a value describing why the fault occurred =E2=80=94 which segme= nt was involved, whether it was a protection violation or a not-present fau= lt, etc.)
      • = EIP (address of the faulting instruction =E2=80=94 so the OS knows where th= e fault happened)
      • = CS (code segment at time of fault)
      • = EFLAGS (flags at time of fault)
      • = If a ring transition occurred: SS and ESP (old stack information)
    3. = Look up vector 13 in the IDT
    4. = Load CS:EIP from the IDT entry (jumping to the OS's GPF handler)
    5. = CPL becomes 0 (the handler runs in kernel mode)

    The OS's GPF handler typically:

    1. = Reads the error code to understand what happened
    2. = Determines which process caused the fault (from the saved CS:EIP)
    3. = Logs or prints a diagnostic (the "Segmentation fault" message in Linux, or = a crash dump in Windows)
    4. = Terminates the offending process (frees its memory, removes it from the sch= eduler)
    5. = Returns to the scheduler to run the next waiting process

    The re= st of the system is completely unaffected. Other processes continue running= . The OS itself is unharmed. This is the fundamental value of hardware-enfo= rced privilege: a misbehaving program cannot escape its sandbox; the OS cat= ches the violation, terminates the offender, and moves on.

    Software impact = =E2=80=94 what this adds to the story of the OS

    The kernel is not just "code that runs first."

    Before= understanding privilege levels, you might think of the kernel as "the prog= ram that runs first and sets things up." This is not wrong, but it misses w= hat makes the kernel the kernel.

    The ke= rnel is defined by its privilege level. It is code that runs at CPL=3D0. Th= is privilege level is not self-declared =E2=80=94 it is granted by the hard= ware. When the OS sets up a GDT entry with DPL=3D0 for the kernel's code se= gment, and loads CS with a selector pointing at that entry, the hardware fr= om that moment enforces that this code is Ring 0. No user program can claim= the same privilege just by loading a different selector =E2=80=94 a user p= rogram at CPL=3D3 cannot load a selector with DPL=3D0 into CS (the privileg= e check blocks it).

    Every OS design choice is shaped by the Ring 0/Ring 3 boundary.<= /p>

    Device= drivers are in the kernel (Ring 0) because they need direct hardware acces= s (IN<= /code>/OUT instructions, writing to memory-mapped device registers) that i= s only available at Ring 0.

    The me= mory allocator is in the kernel (Ring 0) because allocating memory means mo= difying page tables =E2=80=94 a privileged operation involving CR3 and page= -level data structures.

    The fi= le system is in the kernel (Ring 0) because reading from disk means talking= to the disk controller via hardware ports or memory-mapped I/O =E2=80=94 p= rivileged.

    Every = system call exists because some operation requires Ring 0 access. The set o= f system calls a kernel provides defines the boundary between "what user pr= ograms can do" and "what only the kernel can do." This boundary is enforced= by hardware.

    Your OS project:

    When y= our bootloader sets up the GDT and switches to protected mode, the PE bit e= nables the protection mechanism. But at that moment, you're still in Ring 0= (because the GDT you set up for your kernel code has DPL=3D0). Everything = you write in the early OS kernel runs at Ring 0.

    When y= our OS eventually creates and runs its first user process, it must:

    1. = Set up GDT descriptors with DPL=3D3 for user code and data
    2. = Set up page table entries with the user/supervisor bit set to "user" for us= er memory
    3. = Set up the TSS with the kernel's stack address in SS0/ESP0
    4. = Load user-mode segment selectors into CS, DS, SS, ES (with RPL=3D3)
    5. = Execute IRET (or SYSRET on 64-bit) to drop to Ring 3 and begin executing us= er code

    From t= hat moment, the hardware enforces that this user process cannot touch the k= ernel. It cannot HLT the machine. It cannot read the GDT. It cannot access = other processes' memory. If it tries, vector 13 fires, your kernel's GPF ha= ndler runs, and you terminate the process.

    This i= s the kernel/user boundary. Not a convention. Not a promise. A property of = the physics of transistors in the address generation and instruction decode= r circuits.

    What this = adds to the CPU story

    The ba= re minimum CPU gave us fetch-decode-execute.

    The me= mory bus gave us the connection to RAM and address width constraint= s.

    The in= terrupt gave us the event notification mechanism and the timer-driv= en OS control.

    Privil= ege levels give us:

    The hardware enforcement of code authority. Not all code is equa= l. Some code (the kernel) has earned complete trust by being set up with DP= L=3D0 descriptors by the boot process. Other code (user programs) has earne= d limited trust by virtue of running at CPL=3D3. The hardware ensures that = limited trust stays limited =E2=80=94 no sequence of instructions available= to CPL=3D3 code can escalate to CPL=3D0.

    The kernel/user boundary as a physical property. The transition = is not a software convention. It is an implication of which value is in CS'= s low 2 bits, checked by hardware on every instruction decode and every mem= ory access.

    System calls as the only controlled crossing point. User program= s can request kernel services, but they can only enter kernel code at entry= points the kernel pre-registered in the IDT. They cannot execute arbitrary= kernel code. The gateway is narrow, controlled, and one-way unless the ker= nel explicitly chooses to return.

    The foundation for everything in modern OS design. Process isola= tion, device driver architecture, memory protection, virtual address spaces= , the entire software stack built on the OS =E2=80=94 all of it rests on th= is hardware enforcement of privilege levels.

    Next i= s paging and virtual memory =E2=80=94 the 386's contribution =E2=80=94 whic= h takes the protection story further: where privilege levels protect wh= at instructions code can execute, paging protects which physical m= emory addresses code can reach, at a finer granularity than segmentati= on, and with the added ability to give every process its own private view o= f the entire address space.

    =EE=84= =9D

    You said: Go ahead!

    =

    Go ahead!

    Jun 23=
    =
    <= /div>

    Claude responded: Paging and Virtual Memory =E2=80=94 The Intel 386, 1985<= /h2>

    Paging = and Virtual Memory =E2=80=94 The Intel 386, 1985

    The sectio= n text

    "T= he CPU remaps every memory address. Process A's address 0x1000 and Process = B's address 0x1000 are different physical locations. Isolation becomes tota= l."

    And th= e expanded detail:

    "T= he Difference Engine is a special-purpose calculator: brilliant, but it can= only do one type of computation. Segmentation gave us memory protection bu= t remained unwieldy =E2=80=94 variable-sized segments, requiring careful ma= nagement. Running many programs simultaneously while keeping them completel= y isolated from each other required something more elegant. The 386 introdu= ced paging =E2=80=94 a second layer of memory translation, below segmentati= on. With paging enabled, every memory address a program uses is a virtual a= ddress. Before accessing physical RAM, the CPU's Memory Management Unit (MM= U) translates the virtual address to a physical address using a data struct= ure called the page table. Each process has its own page table. Process A's= virtual address 0x00001000 maps to one physical location. Process B's virt= ual address 0x00001000 maps to a completely different physical location. Bo= th processes think they start at address 0 =E2=80=94 but they are in entire= ly different parts of physical RAM. Neither can see or affect the other's m= emory. Total isolation. The TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) is a small c= ache inside the CPU that stores recent virtual-to-physical translations. Wi= thout it, every memory access would require two memory accesses =E2=80=94 o= ne to read the page table entry, one for the actual data. The TLB makes pag= ing fast by caching recent translations. Paging also enables demand paging = =E2=80=94 pages that are not currently in RAM can be stored on disk and loa= ded on demand when accessed, triggering a page fault."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    Remap =E2=80=94 To change the mapping between two things. In eve= ryday language, if a road is closed and traffic is redirected to a differen= t road, that is remapping. In computing, remapping memory means changing wh= ich physical location a given address label refers to. The label (the addre= ss number the program uses) stays the same; the physical location it points= to changes.

    Virtual address =E2=80=94 An address number that a program uses = to refer to memory, which does not necessarily correspond directly to a phy= sical location in RAM. "Virtual" means it exists as an abstraction =E2=80= =94 the program believes it is accessing memory at a certain address, but t= he hardware silently translates that address to a different, real physical = location. The program never sees the translation happening.

    Physical address =E2=80=94 The actual, real address that goes on= to the address bus and reaches the RAM decoder. This is the real location i= n physical RAM where data is actually stored. The RAM chip itself only ever= sees physical addresses =E2=80=94 it has no knowledge of virtual addresses= .

    Translation =E2=80=94 The act of converting a virtual address in= to a physical address. This happens transparently, automatically, in hardwa= re, on every single memory access. The program issues a virtual address; th= e hardware translates it to a physical address before the signal ever reach= es the address bus.

    Page =E2=80=94 A fixed-size block of memory. Both the virtual ad= dress space and physical RAM are divided into pages of equal size. On x86, = the standard page size is 4,096 bytes (4KB). The word "page" comes from the= analogy to pages in a book =E2=80=94 a book is divided into pages of equal= size; memory is divided into pages of equal size. Every virtual page maps = to exactly one physical page.

    Page size =E2=80=94 The fixed size of each page. On x86 protecte= d mode (32-bit), the standard page size is 4,096 bytes =3D 4KB =3D 2=C2=B9= =C2=B2 bytes. This number is not arbitrary =E2=80=94 as we will see, it div= ides a 32-bit address neatly into a 20-bit page number and a 12-bit offset = within the page.

    Page table =E2=80=94 A data structure in RAM =E2=80=94 an array = of entries =E2=80=94 that records the mapping from virtual page numbers to = physical page numbers. Each entry in the page table corresponds to one virt= ual page and contains: the physical page number this virtual page currently= maps to (or a flag saying "not currently in RAM"), plus permission bits (r= eadable? writable? user-accessible?).

    Page table entry (PTE) =E2=80=94 One element of the page table a= rray. A single 4-byte (32-bit) value on x86 that encodes: the physical page= number (20 bits), and a set of flag bits (12 bits) recording present/absen= t, read/write permissions, user/supervisor access level, whether the page h= as been accessed recently, whether it has been modified (dirty), etc.

    MMU =E2=80=94 Memory Management Unit =E2=80=94 The hardware comp= onent inside the CPU (on the 386 and later, this is integrated directly int= o the CPU chip) responsible for performing address translation on every mem= ory access. The MMU takes a virtual address, consults the page table, and p= roduces a physical address. If the virtual address has no valid mapping, th= e MMU raises a Page Fault.

    Page fault =E2=80=94 An exception (hardware-generated interrupt)= that fires when the CPU tries to access a virtual address that has no vali= d mapping in the page table =E2=80=94 either because the page is not curren= tly in RAM (it's on disk, or was never allocated), or because the access vi= olates the page's permission bits (e.g., writing to a read-only page). The = page fault handler in the OS decides what to do: load the missing page from= disk, terminate the program for an illegal access, etc.

    Page directory =E2=80=94 On x86 32-bit paging, the page table is= actually a two-level structure. The page directory is the top level =E2=80= =94 an array of 1,024 entries, each pointing to a second-level structure ca= lled a page table. The page table at each second level is also an array of = 1,024 entries, each pointing to one physical page. This two-level structure= allows the mapping information to itself be stored efficiently (only the p= age tables for virtual address regions actually used need to be present in = RAM).

    CR3 =E2=80=94 Control Register 3 =E2=80=94 A special CPU registe= r (one of the CR0=E2=80=93CR4 family introduced with the 386) that holds th= e physical address of the current process's page directory. When the CPU pe= rforms address translation, the first step is always: read CR3 to find the = page directory. CR3 is a privileged register =E2=80=94 only Ring 0 code can= write to it. When the OS switches from one process to another (context swi= tch), it changes CR3 to point at the new process's page directory =E2=80=94= instantly changing the entire virtual-to-physical mapping.

    TLB =E2=80=94 Translation Lookaside Buffer =E2=80=94 A small, ex= tremely fast cache built into the CPU that stores recently used virtual-to-= physical address translations. Without the TLB, every memory access would r= equire reading the page table from RAM (itself a memory access), then acces= sing the actual data (another memory access) =E2=80=94 doubling the number = of memory accesses for every single operation. The TLB caches the results o= f recent translations so that most memory accesses find their translation a= lready cached, requiring only one memory access (the actual data read/write= ) rather than two.

    TLB miss =E2=80=94 When the CPU needs a translation that is not = currently cached in the TLB. The CPU must then "walk" the page table in RAM= (reading the page directory entry, then the page table entry) to find the = translation, load it into the TLB, and then proceed with the original memor= y access. This is slower =E2=80=94 requires RAM accesses for the table walk= .

    TLB flush =E2=80=94 Clearing (invalidating) all or some entries = in the TLB. When the OS changes CR3 (switching to a different process's pag= e directory), the entire TLB is automatically flushed =E2=80=94 because all= the cached translations belonged to the old process's virtual space and ar= e no longer valid. This is one cost of context switching.

    Demand paging =E2=80=94 A memory management strategy where pages= are not loaded into physical RAM until the first time the program actually= tries to access them, rather than all at once when the program starts. A p= age that has never been accessed simply has a "not present" entry in the pa= ge table. The first access triggers a page fault. The OS's page fault handl= er loads the page from disk into a free physical page, updates the page tab= le entry to point to it (and marks it present), and returns =E2=80=94 the f= aulting instruction is retried and succeeds. From the program's perspective= , it accessed memory and got a valid result; the brief pause while the page= was loaded was invisible (or nearly so).

    Swap space / Paging file =E2=80=94 A region on disk (a dedicated= partition on Linux, a file called pagefile.sys on Windows) used to store p= ages that have been evicted from physical RAM. When physical RAM fills up, = the OS can write some pages' contents to swap space and mark those pages as= "not present" in the page table, freeing physical RAM for other uses. If t= he program later accesses an evicted page, a page fault fires, the OS reads= the page back from swap into physical RAM, updates the page table, and con= tinues. This allows programs to collectively use more memory than physicall= y exists.

    Present bit =E2=80=94 Bit 0 of a page table entry. If present=3D= 1, this page is currently in physical RAM and the physical page number in t= he entry is valid. If present=3D0, this page is not currently in RAM (eithe= r never allocated, or evicted to disk) =E2=80=94 accessing it causes a page= fault.

    Dirty bit =E2=80=94 Bit 6 of a page table entry. The CPU sets th= is automatically when a page is written to. The OS uses this bit when decid= ing which pages to evict: a page that has not been written (dirty=3D0) can = be evicted without writing it to disk (the disk still has the original copy= ); a dirty page must be written to disk before eviction (otherwise the modi= fied contents would be lost).

    Accessed bit =E2=80=94 Bit 5 of a page table entry. The CPU sets= this automatically when a page is read from or written to. Used by the OS = to implement page replacement algorithms: pages that have not been recently= accessed (accessed=3D0) are good candidates for eviction.

    User/Supervisor bit =E2=80=94 Bit 2 of a page table entry. If se= t to 0 (supervisor), this page can only be accessed when CPL=3D0 (kernel mo= de). If set to 1 (user), the page can be accessed from any privilege level = including CPL=3D3 (user programs). This is how paging enforces the kernel/u= ser memory separation at the page level, working alongside the segment desc= riptor privilege checks.

    Read/Write bit =E2=80=94 Bit 1 of a page table entry. If 0, the = page is read-only =E2=80=94 writes trigger a Page Fault. If 1, the page is = writable. The OS uses this for copy-on-write optimization (described below)= and for protecting code pages (marking them read-only prevents programs fr= om accidentally overwriting their own instructions).

    Copy-on-write (COW) =E2=80=94 An optimization where two processe= s initially share the same physical pages, marked read-only. When either pr= ocess tries to write to a shared page, a page fault fires. The OS's page fa= ult handler then: allocates a new physical page, copies the shared page's c= ontents into it, updates the writing process's page table entry to point to= the new private copy (marked writable), and returns. The write proceeds to= the private copy. The other process still sees the original. This is how m= odern OSes implement process creation (fork()) efficiently =E2=80=94 instea= d of copying the entire parent's memory to the child immediately, both star= t sharing pages via copy-on-write, only making private copies of pages that= are actually modified.

    CR0 PG bit =E2=80=94 Bit 31 of CR0 (Control Register 0 =E2=80=94= the same register whose bit 0 is PE, which enables protected mode). Bit 31= is named PG (Paging). When PG=3D1, paging is enabled =E2= =80=94 all memory accesses go through the MMU's address translation. When P= G=3D0, paging is disabled =E2=80=94 virtual addresses equal physical addres= ses (no translation). On the 386, paging can only be enabled while in prote= cted mode (PE must be 1 before PG can be set to 1).

    Linear address =E2=80=94 On x86, there is actually a three-stage= address translation: segment:offset =E2=86=92 linear address =E2=86=92 phy= sical address. The segmentation unit (GDT lookup + base + limit check) conv= erts segment:offset to a "linear address." Then if paging is enabled, the M= MU converts the linear address to a physical address via the page table. If= paging is disabled, linear address =3D physical address. In flat-mode 32-b= it OSes (like Linux), all segment bases are 0, so linear address =3D offset= , which means the programmer just sees "32-bit addresses" without any visib= le segment component.

    Flat address space =E2=80=94 When segments are configured with b= ase=3D0 and limit=3D4GB (as we described in the GDT document =E2=80=94 the = "flat model"), the segmentation step produces a linear address equal to the= offset register's value. With paging enabled, this linear address is then = translated to a physical address by the page table. The programmer sees a f= lat 32-bit address space =E2=80=94 a single range of addresses from 0x00000= 000 to 0xFFFFFFFF =E2=80=94 without any segment:offset complexity.

    Process isolation =E2=80=94 The property that one process cannot= read or write another process's memory. Implemented by giving each process= its own page table (its own CR3 value), with each process's page table map= ping the same virtual addresses to different physical pages. Even if both p= rocesses try to access virtual address 0x1000, they get different physical = pages.

    Virtual address space =E2=80=94 The complete range of virtual ad= dresses available to a process. On 32-bit x86, each process has a 4GB virtu= al address space (addresses 0x00000000 through 0xFFFFFFFF), regardless of h= ow much physical RAM the machine has. The OS divides this: typically the lo= wer 3GB (0x00000000=E2=80=930xBFFFFFFF) is for user space (the program's co= de, data, heap, stack), and the upper 1GB (0xC0000000=E2=80=930xFFFFFFFF) i= s for kernel space (mapped to the kernel's code and data in every process's= page table, but only accessible at Ring 0).

    Kernel mapped into every process =E2=80=94 Every process's page = table includes mappings for the kernel's virtual address range (typically 0= xC0000000=E2=80=930xFFFFFFFF on Linux). This means the kernel is always "pr= esent" in every process's virtual address space =E2=80=94 but marked superv= isor-only (user/supervisor bit =3D 0), so Ring 3 code cannot access it. Thi= s design means that when a system call or interrupt switches from Ring 3 to= Ring 0, the kernel code is already mapped and immediately accessible =E2= =80=94 no page table switch needed. (On modern systems, Meltdown and its mi= tigations changed this, but the principle holds for understanding the basic= design.)

    Page replacement algorithm =E2=80=94 The rule the OS uses to dec= ide which page to evict from physical RAM when RAM is full and a new page n= eeds to be brought in. Common algorithms: LRU (Least Recently Used =E2=80= =94 evict the page not accessed for the longest time, using the accessed bi= t), Clock (an approximation of LRU using a circular buffer), and others. Th= e choice of page replacement algorithm significantly affects performance un= der memory pressure.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    Where we are in = the story

    Privil= ege levels gave us protection of what instructions code can execute =E2=80=94 Ring 3 code cannot execute privileged instructions, cannot chan= ge CR0, cannot modify the GDT.

    Protec= ted mode with segmentation gave us memory bounds =E2=80=94 a progr= am can only reach addresses within its segments' limits, and can only acces= s segments whose DPL =E2=89=A5 its CPL.

    But th= ere is a problem with segmentation alone that we have not yet fully address= ed.

    The problem with= segmentation alone

    Recall= how segmentation works in 32-bit protected mode: you set up GDT entries wi= th a base address (where the segment starts in physical RAM) and a limit (h= ow large the segment is). The physical address for any access is: segment's= base address + offset.

    This m= eans: the physical addresses that a program uses are directly visib= le and directly constrained by GDT entries. If the OS gives Progra= m A a data segment with base=3D0x00100000 and limit=3D0x0FFFFFFF, Program A= 's physical addresses all fall in the range 0x00100000=E2=80=930x10FFFFFF. = The OS can see this range. Other programs can be given non-overlapping rang= es. This provides protection.

    But it= has significant practical problems:

    Problem 1 =E2=80=94 External fragmentation. Suppose physical RAM= looks like this after several programs have been loaded and some have exit= ed:

    Physical RAM:
[0x00000000 =E2=80=93 0x000FFFFF]: Kernel (1MB)
[0x00100000 =E2=80=93 0x001FFFFF]: Free (1MB)
[0x00200000 =E2=80=93 0x003FFFFF]: Program A's memory (2MB)
[0x00400000 =E2=80=93 0x005FFFFF]: Free (2MB)
[0x00600000 =E2=80=93 0x007FFFFF]: Program B's memory (2MB)
[0x00800000 =E2=80=93 0x009FFFFF]: Free (2MB)
[0x00A00000 =E2=80=93 0x00BFFFFF]: Program C's memory (2MB)

    Total = free RAM: 1MB + 2MB + 2MB =3D 5MB. Now a new program needs 4MB of contiguou= s RAM. Can we give it to the program? No =E2=80=94 even th= ough 5MB is free, no single contiguous block of 4MB exists. The free space = is fragmented into non-contiguous pieces. With segmentation, a segment must= be contiguous in physical RAM. There is no way to use these three scattere= d free regions as one 4MB segment.

    This i= s called external fragmentation =E2=80=94 wasted free memo= ry that cannot be used because it is not contiguous, even though the total = amount is sufficient.

    Problem 2 =E2=80=94 Fixed physical addresses are visible. With s= egmentation, the segment base in the GDT is a physical address. The program= , by knowing its own segment selector and the GDT entry it points to, knows= exactly where in physical RAM it lives. Two programs with adjacent physica= l addresses have adjacent physical address ranges =E2=80=94 and the protect= ion comes entirely from the limit checks ensuring each program stays within= its own range.

    But th= is means: if a limit check fails (a bug, or a security flaw in the limit-ch= ecking mechanism), the program can immediately reach the physical address s= pace of adjacent programs. The physical memory layout is directly exposed.<= /p>

    Problem 3 =E2=80=94 Programs must be loaded at specific physical addresse= s. With segmentation, Program A's code has been compiled with addr= esses that assume the code starts at a certain base. If physical RAM at tha= t base is not available (another program is there), either the program cann= ot be loaded, or an expensive relocation process must rewrite all the addre= sses in the program's code.

    Problem 4 =E2=80=94 No easy way to exceed physical RAM. If the m= achine has 4MB of physical RAM and three programs together need 6MB, there = is no clean way to handle this with segmentation. You would need to manuall= y swap entire segments to disk and back =E2=80=94 but since segments are va= riable-size and must be contiguous, finding space for a returning segment i= n a fragmented physical RAM is exactly the fragmentation problem again.

    The core limitation: Segmentation works with physical addres= ses. All programs share one physical RAM, and their physical ranges mu= st not overlap. Managing non-overlapping, contiguous, variable-size physica= l regions for many simultaneously-running programs is inherently complex an= d fragile.

    The key insight = that paging solves all of this

    The fu= ndamental insight of paging: what if we completely decouple the add= resses programs use from the physical locations where their data actually l= ives?

    If a p= rogram can use whatever address it wants =E2=80=94 say, always starting at = address 0x00000000, always seeing a 4GB contiguous address space =E2=80=94 = completely independent of where its data physically lives in RAM, then:

    • = The physical layout doesn't matter to the program
    • = Programs don't interfere with each other because they each have their own a= ddress space
    • = Physical RAM can be divided into fixed-size pieces (pages) and distributed = freely =E2=80=94 no fragmentation
    • = Pages can be swapped to disk and back without the program knowing

    This d= ecoupling is virtual memory: the program uses virtual addr= esses (its own private view of a 4GB space), and the hardware silently tran= slates these to physical addresses (wherever in real RAM the data actually = is).

    How paging physi= cally works =E2=80=94 from first principles, step by step

    Step 1 =E2=80=94= Divide everything into pages

    Physic= al RAM is divided into equal-sized physical pages (also ca= lled page frames or frames). Each frame i= s 4,096 bytes (4KB). If the machine has, say, 32MB of physical RAM, there a= re 32MB =C3=B7 4KB =3D 8,192 physical frames, numbered 0 through 8,191.

    The vi= rtual address space of each process is also divided into equal-sized virtual pages, also 4,096 bytes each. A 32-bit virtual address s= pace has 4GB =C3=B7 4KB =3D 1,048,576 virtual pages (about 1 million), numb= ered 0 through 1,048,575.

    Both v= irtual and physical pages are the same size. A virtual page maps to exactly= one physical frame. A physical frame can be mapped to by any virtual page = in any process (or no virtual page at all =E2=80=94 unmapped frames are fre= e RAM).

    Step 2 =E2=80=94= Split a virtual address into two parts

    A 32-b= it virtual address is split into two fields:

    Bits 31=E2=80=9312: Virtual Page Number (VPN) =E2=80=94 20 b=
its
Bits 11=E2=80=930:  Offset within the page =E2=80=94 12=
 bits

    Why th= is split? Because 2=C2=B9=C2=B2 =3D 4,096 =E2=80=94 the page size. An offse= t of 12 bits can address any byte within a 4KB page (0 through 4,095). The = virtual page number identifies which page, and the offset identifi= es which byte within that page.

    Example: Virtual address 0x00001A3F

    In bin= ary (32 bits):

    0000 0000 0000 0000 0001 1010 0011 1111=

    Split:=

    • = Bits 31=E2=80=9312 (upper 20 bits): 0000 0000 0000 0000 0001 =3D 0x00001 = =3D virtual page number 1
    • = Bits 11=E2=80=930 (lower 12 bits): 1010 0011 1111 =3D 0xA3F =3D offset 2,6= 23

    So vir= tual address 0x00001A3F means: byte 2,623 within virtual page number 1.

    Step 3 =E2=80=94= The page table maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers

    For ea= ch process, the OS maintains a page table =E2=80=94 an arr= ay of entries, one per virtual page. Entry N in the page table corresponds = to virtual page number N.

    Each e= ntry (on x86) is 4 bytes, containing:

    • = Bits 31=E2=80=9312: Physical frame number (20 bits =E2=80= =94 the frame in physical RAM that this virtual page maps to)
    • = Bit 0: Present (1 =3D this page is in physical RAM and the= frame number is valid; 0 =3D page not in RAM)
    • = Bit 1: Read/Write (0 =3D read-only; 1 =3D writable)
    • = Bit 2: User/Supervisor (0 =3D only Ring 0 can access; 1 = =3D Ring 3 can also access)
    • = Bit 5: Accessed (CPU sets this to 1 when the page is read = or written)
    • = Bit 6: Dirty (CPU sets this to 1 when the page is written)=
    • = Other bits: various flags for caching behavior, global pages, etc.

    Example (continuing from Step 2):

    Suppos= e virtual page 1 maps to physical frame 42. The page table entry for virtua= l page 1 would have:

    • = Bits 31=E2=80=9312: 42 (the physical frame number)
    • = Bit 0: 1 (present =E2=80=94 the frame is in RAM)
    • = Bit 1: 1 (writable)
    • = Bit 2: 1 (user-accessible)

    In bin= ary (32 bits), the entry looks like:

    0000 0000 0000 0010 1010 0000 0000 0111=

    Upper = 20 bits =3D 42 (frame number), lower 12 bits =3D 0b000000000111 (flags: pre= sent, writable, user).

    Step 4 =E2=80=94= Address translation in hardware

    When t= he CPU (via the MMU) needs to translate virtual address 0x00001A3F:

    1. = Split the address: VPN =3D 1, offset =3D 0xA3F
    2. = Look up page table entry 1: find physical frame number =3D 42
    3. = Compute physical address: (42 =C3=97 4096) + 0xA3F =3D 172,032 + 2,623 =3D = 174,655 =3D 0x0002AA3F
    4. = This physical address (0x0002AA3F) goes onto the address bus
    5. = RAM's decoder activates byte-group 174,655
    6. = Data is returned on the data bus

    The pr= ogram used virtual address 0x00001A3F. The physical address accessed was 0x= 0002AA3F. The program has no knowledge of this translation. It simply used = the address 0x00001A3F and received (or wrote) data.

    Step 5 =E2=80=94= The two-level page table structure on x86

    There = is a problem with a simple flat page table: a 32-bit virtual address space = has 2=C2=B2=E2=81=B0 =3D 1,048,576 virtual pages. If each page table entry = is 4 bytes, the page table itself would be 4MB per process. With 100 proces= ses, that is 400MB of RAM used just for page tables =E2=80=94 before any ac= tual program data.

    x86 so= lves this with a two-level page table structure:

    Level 1 =E2=80=94 Page Directory: A single table with 1,024 entr= ies (4KB total =E2=80=94 one page). Each entry is 4 bytes and points to a L= evel 2 table (or is marked not-present if no virtual pages in that range ha= ve been mapped). CR3 holds the physical address of the page directory.

    Level 2 =E2=80=94 Page Tables: Up to 1,024 separate tables, each= with 1,024 entries (4KB each). Each entry is a page table entry as describ= ed above.

    A 32-b= it virtual address is now split into three fields:

    Bits 31=E2=80=9322: Page Directory Index (10 bits) =E2=80=94=
 which Level 1 entry
Bits 21=E2=80=9312: Page Table Index (10 bits) =E2=80=
=94 which Level 2 entry
Bits 11=E2=80=930:  Offset (12 bits) =E2=80=94 which byte with=
in the page

    10 + 1= 0 + 12 =3D 32 bits. Perfect.

    Why this saves memory:

    If a p= rocess only uses a small fraction of its 4GB virtual address space (which m= ost programs do =E2=80=94 they use perhaps 100MB out of 4GB), most page dir= ectory entries will be "not present." Those Level 2 page tables don't need = to exist at all. Instead of always using 4MB for the page table, a process = with a modest memory footprint might use only a few KB of page tables (one = page directory =3D 4KB, plus a few Level 2 tables of 4KB each).

    The translation walk (two levels):

    For vi= rtual address with bits 31=E2=80=9322 =3D directory index D, bits 21=E2=80= =9312 =3D table index T, bits 11=E2=80=930 =3D offset O:

    1. = CR3 =E2=86=92 physical address of page directory
    2. = Read page directory entry D =E2=86=92 physical address of Level 2 page tabl= e (or fault if not present)
    3. = Read Level 2 page table entry T =E2=86=92 physical frame number F (or fault= if not present)
    4. = Physical address =3D (F =C3=97 4096) + O
    5. = Access RAM at that physical address

    Withou= t the TLB, every memory access requires three RAM accesses= : read the page directory, read the page table, then access the actual data= . The TLB eliminates this overhead for cached translations.

    The TLB in detai= l

    The TL= B (Translation Lookaside Buffer) is a small, extremely fast associative cac= he built into the CPU chip, containing a small number of recently used virt= ual-to-physical translations (typically 32 to 1,024 entries in modern CPUs,= organized by address range).

    How it works:

    When t= he MMU needs to translate a virtual address:

    1. = First, check the TLB: is there a cached entry for this virtual page number?
      • = TLB hit: Found. Use the cached physical frame number direc= tly. No page table walk needed. Fast =E2=80=94 essentially zero additional = time.
      • = TLB miss: Not found. Perform the full page table walk (2 R= AM accesses for two-level paging). Load the result into the TLB (possibly e= victing an old entry). Then use the result.

    Why TLB misses are rare in practice:

    Progra= ms exhibit temporal locality (they access the same address= es repeatedly over a short period) and spatial locality (t= hey access addresses near addresses they recently accessed). This means:

    • = The same pages are accessed over and over in a short time (temporal) =E2=86= =92 TLB hit because the translation is still cached
    • = Sequential code execution accesses consecutive addresses on the same page = =E2=86=92 one TLB load covers many instruction fetches (spatial)

    In pra= ctice, TLB hit rates are extremely high =E2=80=94 often 99%+ for typical wo= rkloads. The TLB is one of the most impactful performance optimizations in = CPU design.

    TLB flush and context switches:

    When C= R3 changes (because the OS switches to a different process), all TLB entrie= s from the old process become invalid. The CPU automatically flushes the TL= B when CR3 is written. This is one reason context switches have a cost: aft= er a context switch, the TLB is empty, and the first many memory accesses o= f the new process all cause TLB misses, requiring page table walks. This "T= LB warming" cost is measurable in benchmarks.

    Modern= CPUs mitigate this with PCID (Process-Context Identifiers) =E2=80=94 a mechanism that tags TLB entries with a process ID, allowing e= ntries from different processes to coexist in the TLB without interfering. = When a context switch occurs, the TLB is not entirely flushed; entries tagg= ed with the old process's PCID are simply not matched against the new proce= ss's lookups. When the old process runs again, its TLB entries are still th= ere.

    Process isolatio= n via separate page tables

    This i= s the payoff =E2=80=94 the complete explanation of why Process A's address = 0x1000 and Process B's address 0x1000 are different physical locations.

    Each p= rocess has its own page directory (stored somewhere in phy= sical RAM). The OS maintains a separate page directory for each process. CR= 3 holds the physical address of the currently running proc= ess's page directory.

    Setup (simplified):

    The OS= allocates physical frames for Process A's code and data. Suppose:

    • = Process A's code is in physical frames 50, 51, 52, 53
    • = Process A's data is in physical frames 100, 101, 102

    The OS= builds Process A's page table:

    • = Virtual page 0 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 50 (code, read-only, user)
    • = Virtual page 1 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 51 (code, read-only, user)
    • = Virtual page 2 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 52 (code, read-only, user)
    • = Virtual page 3 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 53 (code, read-only, user)
    • = Virtual page 256 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 100 (data, writable, user)
    • = Virtual page 257 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 101 (data, writable, user)
    • = Virtual page 258 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 102 (data, writable, user)
    • = All other virtual pages: not present

    The OS= allocates physical frames for Process B's code and data. Suppose:

    • = Process B's code is in physical frames 200, 201
    • = Process B's data is in physical frames 300, 301, 302, 303

    The OS= builds Process B's page table:

    • = Virtual page 0 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 200 (code, read-only, user)
    • = Virtual page 1 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 201 (code, read-only, user)
    • = Virtual page 256 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 300 (data, writable, user)
    • = Virtual page 257 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 301 (data, writable, user)
    • = Virtual page 258 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 302 (data, writable, user)
    • = Virtual page 259 =E2=86=92 Physical frame 303 (data, writable, user)
    • = All other virtual pages: not present

    While Process A is running:
    CR3 points to Process A's page directory.

    Proces= s A accesses virtual address 0x00000000 (virtual page 0, offset 0):
    =E2=86=92 Page table says: frame 50
    =E2=86=92 Physical address: 50 =C3=97 4096 =3D 0x00032000
    =E2=86=92 RAM delivers data from frame 50

    Context switch to Process B:
    OS saves all of Process A's register values. Sets CR3 to Process B's page d= irectory address. Loads all of Process B's register values. IRET returns to= Process B.

    While Process B is running:
    CR3 points to Process B's page directory.

    Proces= s B accesses virtual address 0x00000000 (virtual page 0, offset 0):
    =E2=86=92 Page table says: frame 200
    =E2=86=92 Physical address: 200 =C3=97 4096 =3D 0x000C8000
    =E2=86=92 RAM delivers data from frame 200

    Same virtual address. Completely different physical location. Pr= ocess A and Process B both use virtual address 0x00000000 to refer to their= respective code's first byte. But they are in different physical frames. N= either can reach the other's frame, because the other process's frames are = simply not mapped anywhere in the current process's page table.

    Can Process A access Process B's physical frames?

    Proces= s A can only generate addresses that go through Process A's page table. Pro= cess A's page table has entries for frames 50, 51, 52, 53, 100, 101, 102 = =E2=80=94 and no others. Frames 200, 201, 300, 301, 302, 303 (Process B's f= rames) are not mapped in Process A's page table at all.

    If Pro= cess A tries to access a virtual address that would land in those frames, t= he MMU would try to look up that virtual page in Process A's page table, fi= nd "not present," and generate a Page Fault. The OS's page fault handler wo= uld terminate Process A (or deliver a segmentation fault signal if it's a U= nix-like OS).

    There = is no virtual address in Process A's address space that maps to Process B's= physical frames. The isolation is total, unconditional, and enforced by ha= rdware on every single memory access.

    Demand paging = =E2=80=94 pages don't need to be in RAM

    With p= aging established, we can now explain one of its most powerful applications= : demand paging =E2=80=94 the ability to run programs whos= e total memory requirements exceed physical RAM.

    The mechanism:

    When a= page is not currently in physical RAM (it's on disk, or was never loaded),= its page table entry has the Present bit =3D 0. The physical frame number = field may contain a "disk address" (where on disk the page's data is), inte= rpreted by the OS's page fault handler.

    When t= he program accesses a virtual address in this not-present page:

    1. = The MMU reads the page table entry and sees Present=3D0
    2. = The MMU raises a Page Fault (exception vector 14)
    3. = The CPU saves state (CS, EIP, EFLAGS, and the faulting address =E2=80=94 wh= ich the CPU stores in CR2 for the fault handler to read)
    4. = The OS's page fault handler runs (Ring 0)
    5. = The handler reads CR2 to find the faulting virtual address
    6. = The handler looks up where this page's data is on disk (using information i= t stored somewhere =E2=80=94 not in the PTE's physical frame field, since t= hat field is only meaningful when Present=3D1; in practice, the OS uses its= own internal data structures)
    7. = The handler finds a free physical frame (or evicts a page to get one =E2=80= =94 see below)
    8. = The handler reads the page's data from disk into the free physical frame
    9. = The handler updates the page table entry: sets the physical frame number to= the newly allocated frame, sets Present=3D1
    10. = The handler executes IRET, which retries the faulting instruction
    11. = This time, the MMU finds Present=3D1, translates successfully, the access p= roceeds

    The pr= ogram never knows this happened. It tried to read a memory address. There w= as a brief pause (milliseconds while the disk read occurred). Then the read= returned a value. From the program's perspective, it just accessed memory = normally.

    When physical RAM fills up =E2=80=94 page eviction:

    If all= physical frames are in use and a page fault occurs, the OS must evict some= existing page to free a frame for the incoming page.

    The pa= ge replacement algorithm selects a victim page (some page currently in RAM = that the OS decides to remove). If the victim page's dirty bit is 1 (it has= been modified since being loaded), the OS writes its contents to disk (to = the swap space) before reusing the frame. If dirty=3D0, the frame can be re= used directly (the disk still has the original contents).

    After = eviction, the victim page's table entry is updated: Present=3D0, and the OS= records the disk location in its own data structures. Now the frame is fre= e for the incoming page.

    The illusion of infinite memory:

    From e= ach program's perspective, it has access to its entire virtual address spac= e (up to 3GB for user space in a typical 32-bit Linux layout). The fact tha= t only a small fraction of those pages are actually in physical RAM at any = moment is invisible. The OS's page fault handler transparently moves pages = between RAM and disk on demand.

    This i= s why you can run programs that collectively need more RAM than your machin= e physically has =E2=80=94 though when RAM is full and paging to disk becom= es frequent, the system slows dramatically (called thrashing =E2=80=94 spending more time moving pages between RAM and disk than doin= g actual computation).

    The complete x86= address translation pipeline

    Now we= can draw the complete picture of how a memory access works on a 386-class = CPU in protected mode with paging enabled:

    PROGRAM ISSUES: virtual address V
         =E2=86=93
[SEGMENTATION UNIT]
  Read segment register (e.g., DS)
  Look up selector in GDT (using GDTR)
  Read descriptor: base B, limit L, DPL D
  Check: offset =E2=89=A4 L? (limit check)
  Check: max(CPL, RPL) =E2=89=A4 D? (privilege check)
  If either check fails =E2=86=92 General Protection Fault (ve=
ctor 13)
  If both pass =E2=86=92 Linear address =3D B + offset
         =E2=86=93
   linear address (32 bits)
         =E2=86=93
[MMU / PAGING UNIT] (only if CR0.PG =3D 1)
  Check TLB: is this linear address cached?
    TLB HIT =E2=86=92 use cached physical frame number
    TLB MISS:
      Read CR3 =E2=86=92 physical address of page directory
      Extract bits 31=E2=80=9322 =E2=86=92 page directory inde=
x D
      Read page directory entry D
        Check Present bit =E2=86=92 if 0: Page Fault (vector 1=
4)
      Extract bits 21=E2=80=9312 =E2=86=92 page table index T
      Read page table entry T
        Check Present bit =E2=86=92 if 0: Page Fault (vector 1=
4)
        Check Read/Write =E2=86=92 if writing to read-only pag=
e: Page Fault
        Check User/Supervisor =E2=86=92 if CPL=3D3 and supervi=
sor-only: Page Fault
      Get physical frame number F
      Load (linear page number =E2=86=92 F) into TLB
  Physical address =3D (F =C3=97 4096) + bits 11=E2=80=930 of =
linear address
         =E2=86=93
   physical address (32 bits)
         =E2=86=93
[ADDRESS BUS] (32 wires on the 386)
         =E2=86=93
[RAM DECODER]
  Activates physical byte-group at that address
         =E2=86=93
[DATA BUS]
  Returns or stores data
         =E2=86=93
PROGRAM RECEIVES: the data at that address
    =

    Every = memory access =E2=80=94 every instruction fetch, every data read, every dat= a write =E2=80=94 goes through this complete pipeline. On a modern CPU runn= ing at 3GHz with a TLB hit rate of 99%, this entire pipeline executes effec= tively in one clock cycle for the common case.

    What the 386 spe= cifically added to make this possible

    The 38= 6 (Intel 80386, 1985) introduced, for the first time in the x86 family, all= the hardware needed for paging:

    New registers:

    • = CR0 (extended from 286's MSW): added bit 31 =3D PG (enable= paging)
    • = CR2: holds the virtual address that caused the most recent= Page Fault (so the page fault handler knows what address was being accesse= d)
    • = CR3: holds the physical address of the page directory (wri= tten when switching processes, triggering TLB flush)
    • = CR4: (added in later 386 revisions and Pentium) controls v= arious extensions

    32-bit address bus: The 386 has a 32-bit address bus (232 =3D 4G= B of physical addressing), matching the 32-bit registers. The page table st= ructure =E2=80=94 page directory entries, page table entries =E2=80=94 is d= esigned for this 32-bit physical address space.

    32-bit registers: With 32-bit general-purpose registers (EAX, EB= X, ECX, EDX, ESP, EBP, ESI, EDI =E2=80=94 the "E" prefix means "Extended" t= o 32 bits), the complete virtual address space is 232 =3D 4GB. The 10+10+12= bit split of a 32-bit address into directory index + table index + offset = maps perfectly onto this register size.

    32-bit protected mode (flat model): The 386 in protected mode wi= th 32-bit segments (the D/B bit in GDT descriptors, per the GDT document) a= llows segment offsets to be 32-bit values =E2=80=94 meaning the offset alon= e can address any byte in the 4GB space. Combined with flat-model segments = (base=3D0, limit=3D4GB), the segmentation step becomes a pass-through (line= ar address =3D offset =3D the 32-bit register value), and paging provides a= ll the actual memory management.

    Why paging super= seded segmentation for memory management

    Segmen= tation and paging both provide memory protection. On the 386, both are acti= ve simultaneously (segmentation first, then paging). But in practice, all m= ajor 32-bit and 64-bit operating systems use paging for actual memory manag= ement and treat segmentation as a pass-through.

    Why?

    1 =E2=80=94 Fixed-size pages eliminate fragmentation. Physical R= AM divided into 4KB frames can be allocated and freed in any order with no = fragmentation =E2=80=94 a freed frame can be used for any page of any proce= ss. Variable-size segments cannot be rearranged without moving data.

    2 =E2=80=94 Page tables support sparse usage. A process that use= s 100MB of its 4GB virtual space needs page table entries only for the 100M= B it uses =E2=80=94 via the two-level structure, only the relevant page dir= ectory entries and page tables need to exist. The remaining virtual space h= as "not present" entries that cost nothing.

    3 =E2=80=94 Paging naturally supports demand paging. The present= bit in a page table entry elegantly represents "page is in RAM" vs "page i= s not in RAM," enabling the page fault mechanism. Implementing the equivale= nt with segmentation requires complex segment management and does not map a= s cleanly onto the mechanism.

    4 =E2=80=94 Per-page permissions are fine-grained. Each 4KB page= can have independent read/write/execute/user-supervisor permissions. With = segmentation, permissions apply to entire segments.

    5 =E2=80=94 Process isolation via CR3 is clean and fast. Switchi= ng between processes' entire address spaces requires only writing one value= to CR3. The hardware automatically flushes the TLB and uses the new page t= ables for every subsequent access.
