    PWR_OK =E2=80=94 Power Good signal =E2=80=94 A signal line from = the PSU back to the motherboard. When all the PSU's output voltages have re= ached their correct stable levels and remained stable for a minimum time (t= ypically 100ms), the PSU asserts PWR_OK =E2=80=94 drives this line to a def= ined high voltage. This is the PSU's declaration: "my outputs are valid, it= is safe to start the electronics." The chipset monitors PWR_OK and does no= t release the CPU's reset signal until PWR_OK has been asserted.

    Reset state =E2=80=94 A special condition the CPU is in from the= moment it receives power until the reset signal is released. In reset stat= e, the CPU is powered (transistors have voltage) but its internal state mac= hine is held in a fixed, defined configuration by the RESET# signal being a= sserted (held low). The CPU executes no instructions. When RESET# is releas= ed (goes high), the CPU's internal state machine initializes all registers = to their documented reset values and begins executing =E2=80=94 we covered = these values in the CPU architecture documents.

    RESET# pin =E2=80=94 A physical pin on the CPU chip package conn= ected to a corresponding signal from the chipset. While RESET# is held at l= ow voltage by the chipset, the CPU is in reset =E2=80=94 all its internal s= tate is frozen at initial values. When the chipset releases RESET# (allows = it to go high), the CPU begins operation. The chipset keeps RESET# asserted= until PWR_OK arrives, ensuring the CPU never tries to execute with unstabl= e power.

    5VSB =E2=80=94 5 Volt Standby =E2=80=94 A special always-on outp= ut rail from the PSU that provides +5V even when the computer is "turned of= f" (PS_ON# deasserted, main outputs off). This rail powers:

    • = The chipset's power management circuitry (so it can monitor the power butto= n)
    • = The RTC (Real-Time Clock) =E2=80=94 the clock that keeps time when the comp= uter is off
    • = Wake-on-LAN and Wake-on-USB circuitry
    • = USB ports marked as "always on" for charging

    This i= s why your phone charges from a USB port even when the computer is off, and= why unplugging the computer from the wall causes it to completely lose pow= er (including the ability to respond to the power button).

    Capacitor =E2=80=94 An electronic component that stores electric= al charge. A capacitor consists of two conductive plates separated by an in= sulating material. When voltage is applied, charge accumulates on the plate= s. The capacitor can then release this stored charge as current. In PSUs, l= arge capacitors smooth out voltage fluctuations =E2=80=94 when the AC input= briefly dips, the capacitors release stored charge to maintain stable DC o= utput. The size of the capacitors determines how long the PSU can maintain = stable output through brief power interruptions.

    Voltage regulator =E2=80=94 A circuit that maintains a stable ou= tput voltage despite variations in input voltage or output current (load). = Inside the PSU, separate voltage regulators maintain each output rail (+12V= , +5V, +3.3V) at their specified values. On modern motherboards, there are = additional voltage regulator modules (VRMs) near the CPU socket that furthe= r step down the PSU's +12V to the much lower voltages the CPU actually need= s (modern CPUs run at 0.7V=E2=80=931.4V depending on workload and clock spe= ed).

    Transformer =E2=80=94 An electromagnetic device that converts AC= voltage from one level to another. A transformer has two wire coils wound = around an iron core. AC current in the primary coil creates a changing magn= etic field in the core, which induces AC current in the secondary coil. The= ratio of the number of wire turns in each coil determines the voltage rati= o. A transformer with 10=C3=97 fewer turns in the secondary coil outputs 1/= 10th the voltage of the input. This is how 220V AC from the wall becomes lo= wer-voltage AC inside the PSU before conversion to DC.

    Rectifier =E2=80=94 A circuit that converts AC (which alternates= between positive and negative) into DC (which flows in one direction). A r= ectifier uses diodes =E2=80=94 components that allow current to flow in onl= y one direction. By arranging four diodes in a "bridge" configuration, both= the positive and negative halves of the AC sine wave are converted to posi= tive DC. The result is not perfectly smooth DC =E2=80=94 it has a ripple (p= ulses at twice the AC frequency) that the capacitors then smooth out.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    Why power-up seq= uencing exists at all

    A naiv= e view of "turning on a computer" is: connect power, everything starts simu= ltaneously. But this cannot work.

    Consid= er: the CPU needs to execute instructions from memory. But memory (RAM) req= uires initialization before it contains valid data. The CPU needs to know a= bout the memory controller settings. The memory controller is part of the c= hipset. The chipset needs stable voltage before it can operate. Stable volt= age requires the PSU to have completed its power-up sequence. The PSU needs= the PS_ON# signal from the chipset. But the chipset needs stable 5VSB to e= ven monitor the power button.

    These = dependencies form a chain. Each component depends on others being ready fir= st. Power-up sequencing solves this by ensuring things happen in the correc= t order, with each step gating the next.

    The physical seq= uence, traced through silicon and copper

    T =3D 0ms: The button press

    You pr= ess the power button. The button is a momentary normally-open switch =E2=80= =94 when not pressed, no contact is made; when pressed, two electrical cont= acts touch.

    Inside= the computer case, two thin wires run from the power button to two pins on= the motherboard =E2=80=94 a header connector labeled "PWR_BTN." When the b= utton is pressed, these two pins are connected together. One pin is connect= ed (inside the chipset) to a pull-up resistor that holds it at 3.3V under n= ormal conditions. The other pin is connected to ground.

    When t= he button connects them: the first pin is now connected to ground through t= he button, and the pull-up resistor limits the current flow. The voltage on= PWR_BTN# drops from 3.3V to near 0V =E2=80=94 the signal is asserted.

    Inside the chipset =E2=80=94 detecting the button press

    The ch= ipset contains logic that monitors PWR_BTN# continuously, even when the mai= n computer is off, because the chipset's power management circuits are powe= red by 5VSB. This logic detects the falling edge (transition from high to l= ow voltage) on PWR_BTN#. To prevent accidental triggers from button contact= bounce (the mechanical contacts briefly bounce when they touch, creating m= ultiple rapid transitions), the chipset implements debouncing =E2=80=94 req= uiring the signal to remain low for a minimum time (typically 4ms) before t= reating it as a valid press.

    After = the debounce period confirms a real button press, the chipset's power manag= ement state machine transitions from "S5" (soft off) to "S0" (fully on). Pa= rt of this transition: the chipset asserts PS_ON# =E2=80=94 pulls that line= low.

    The PSU responds to PS_ON#

    The PS= U's control circuitry continuously monitors PS_ON# on its motherboard conne= ctor. When PS_ON# goes low, the PSU's control chip begins the power-up sequ= ence:

    1. = It enables the main switching circuits =E2=80=94 the transistors that chop = the rectified AC into pulses that the transformer can convert.
    2. = The +12V rail begins rising. The control chip monitors it via a feedback lo= op.
    3. = The +5V rail begins rising (derived from the +12V in most modern designs).<= /li>
    4. = The +3.3V rail begins rising.
    5. = The control chip monitors all rails continuously, checking that they are wi= thin specification (+/- 5% for most rails).
    6. = Once all rails have been within spec for a minimum dwell time (typically 10= 0ms), the control chip asserts PWR_OK =E2=80=94 drives that line to +5V.

    The 10= 0=E2=80=93500ms delay we mentioned: the lower end (100ms) is the minimum dw= ell time before PWR_OK. The upper end (500ms) accounts for PSU designs that= are more conservative, caps that take longer to charge, or complex sequenc= es with more checking steps.

    Why stable voltage matters so much

    A CPU = register is a collection of flip-flops =E2=80=94 circuits that hold a bit a= s a voltage level. If the supply voltage is slightly below spec, a flip-flo= p that should read as "1" might be ambiguous =E2=80=94 it might read as "0"= on the next clock cycle. The result is silent data corruption: the CPU com= putes wrong answers without knowing it, producing results that look like so= ftware bugs but are actually hardware failures caused by marginal voltage.<= /p>

    This i= s why PSU quality matters enormously, and why cheap PSUs that cannot mainta= in stable voltages under load cause mysterious crashes and corruption.

    PWR_OK arrives at the motherboard

    The ch= ipset monitors PWR_OK. When it transitions from low to high, the chipset's = power-on state machine proceeds: it releases RESET# =E2=80=94 allows the CP= U's reset pin to go high.

    The CP= U's internal RESET# monitoring circuitry detects this rising edge. On the n= ext positive edge of the clock signal, the CPU's internal state machine ini= tializes. Every register is forced to its documented reset value. The CPU b= egins its fetch-decode-execute cycle from the reset vector.

    The voltage rail= s and why each exists

    The PS= U provides multiple voltage rails because different components have differe= nt requirements:

    +12V is the highest voltage and carries the most power (power = =3D voltage =C3=97 current; for a given power delivery, higher voltage mean= s lower current, which means thinner wires can be used). The +12V rail powe= rs:

    • = The CPU's local voltage regulators (which step it down to ~1V)
    • = PCIe graphics card power (which also has its own local regulators)
    • = Hard drive motors and read/write actuators (which need 12V directly)
    • = Cooling fans

    +5V historically powered most logic circuits. Early CPUs ran at = 5V (the 8086, 8088, 286, 386 all used 5V logic). As transistors shrank, the= safe operating voltage dropped =E2=80=94 3.3V, then 2.5V, then 1.8V, then = 1.2V, then below 1V. Modern CPUs no longer use 5V directly, but +5V is stil= l used for USB ports and some legacy components.

    +3.3V is the current standard logic voltage for most chips that = don't need dedicated VRMs. Modern DDR5 RAM uses 1.1V (with its own regulato= rs), but older DDR4 used 1.2V (regulated from 3.3V). Many motherboard chips= and slots operate at 3.3V.

    +5VSB is the standby rail =E2=80=94 always on, providing just en= ough power to keep the power management circuitry alive. It is connected di= rectly to mains power through the PSU's standby supply, bypassing the main = switching circuits that PS_ON# controls.

    The significance= of the 100=E2=80=93500ms delay

    You mi= ght wonder: if modern CPUs run at 3=E2=80=935 GHz, executing billions of in= structions per second, why does power-up take hundreds of milliseconds?

    The an= swer is that the delay is entirely in the analog electrical domain =E2=80= =94 the charging of capacitors, the stabilization of switching regulators, = the thermal equilibration of transformer cores. These are governed by physi= cs (RC time constants, electromagnetic induction) that do not benefit from = digital circuit speedups. Making the capacitors charge faster would require= either larger current capacity (more expensive PSU) or smaller capacitors = (less filtering, more ripple, less stability). The 100=E2=80=93500ms is a d= esign choice balancing cost, stability, and speed.

    Once P= WR_OK arrives and the CPU begins, the digital domain takes over =E2=80=94 a= nd things happen at clock speed. The next phase (POST) takes longer not bec= ause of analog delays but because of the sheer amount of hardware that must= be tested and initialized.

    CPU Reset = State =E2=80=94 All Registers to Known Values =E2=80=94 T+~100=E2=80=93500m= s

    The sectio= n text

    "P= WR_OK arrives. The chipset releases the CPU's reset pin. The CPU's internal= state snaps to fixed, defined values. It is ready to fetch its first instr= uction."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    RESET# pin =E2=80=94 Already defined above. The physical pin on = the CPU package through which the chipset holds the CPU in reset (by keepin= g this pin at low voltage) and releases it (by allowing it to return to hig= h voltage).

    Internal state machine =E2=80=94 The collection of circuits insi= de the CPU that determines its current operating condition =E2=80=94 what m= ode it is in, what phase of the fetch-decode-execute cycle it is in, what v= alues its registers hold. A state machine is a system that can be in one of= a finite number of states and transitions between them based on inputs and= the current state. The CPU's internal state machine, when RESET# goes high= , forces the CPU into a specific initial state regardless of whatever rando= m state the transistors might have been in.

    0x0000FFF0 =E2=80=94 The value in EIP after reset =E2=80=94 EIP = is the instruction pointer (called IP in 16-bit mode, EIP in 32-bit mode). = After reset, it holds 0x0000FFF0. This is a 32-bit hexadecimal number. In d= ecimal: 65,520. This is an offset, not a physical address =E2=80=94 the phy= sical address comes from adding the CS base to this offset.

    0xFFFF0000 =E2=80=94 The CS base value after reset =E2=80=94 The= Code Segment register has a hidden portion (the "shadow" portion, covered = in our 8086 and GDT documents). After reset, even though the CS selector va= lue is 0xF000, the CS base is pre-initialized to 0xFFFF0000. This is a spec= ial initial condition =E2=80=94 in normal real-mode operation, loading CS w= ith selector 0xF000 would give a base of 0xF000 =C3=97 16 =3D 0xF0000. But = at reset, Intel pre-loads the CS shadow with a base of 0xFFFF0000 rather th= an the value the real-mode formula would give. This is implemented in hardw= are specifically to place the reset vector at the top of the 32-bit address= space.

    0xFFFFFFF0 =E2=80=94 The reset vector (physical address) =E2=80= =94 The physical address where the CPU fetches its first instruction after = reset. Computed as: CS.base (0xFFFF0000) + EIP (0x0000FFF0) =3D 0xFFFFFFF0.= In decimal: 4,294,967,280. This is 16 bytes below the top of the 4GB addre= ss space (the top being 0xFFFFFFFF =3D 4,294,967,295).

    Real mode =E2=80=94 We covered this completely in the protected = mode document. The CPU's initial operating mode after reset, with segment= =C3=9716+offset addressing, no memory protection, and direct physical addre= ss generation. All x86 CPUs boot in real mode.

    PE bit =E2=80=94 The Protection Enable bit =E2=80=94 bit 0 of CR= 0. After reset, CR0 =3D 0x60000010. Bit 0 is 0, so PE =3D 0, meaning real m= ode is active. The CPU will remain in real mode until the bootloader explic= itly sets this bit.

    PG bit =E2=80=94 The Paging bit =E2=80=94 bit 31 of CR0. After r= eset, this is 0, meaning paging is disabled. Physical addresses equal linea= r addresses. Paging will not be enabled until the OS kernel explicitly enab= les it.

    EFLAGS =3D 0x00000002 =E2=80=94 The FLAGS register's initial val= ue. Only bit 1 is set. Bit 1 is a reserved bit that Intel documentation say= s must always be 1 =E2=80=94 it has no functional meaning but is always 1 b= y specification. All meaningful flag bits (CF, ZF, SF, OF, IF, DF, TF) star= t at 0. Notably, IF =3D 0 means interrupts are disabled =E2=80=94 the CPU w= ill not respond to hardware interrupts until the bootloader or OS explicitl= y executes the STI instruction.

    CMOS =E2=80=94 Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor =E2=80=94= a type of chip manufacturing process that combines two types of transistor= s (n-type and p-type) to create logic that consumes very little power when = not switching. CMOS circuits maintain their state with almost no current fl= ow, which is why a small lithium battery on the motherboard (the CMOS batte= ry) can keep BIOS settings and the real-time clock running for years. When = people say "clear the CMOS," they mean removing this battery (or shorting i= ts contacts) to let the small capacitors discharge, losing the stored BIOS = settings.

    RTC =E2=80=94 Real-Time Clock =E2=80=94 A dedicated low-power ci= rcuit on the motherboard (integrated into the chipset on modern boards) tha= t keeps track of the current date and time even when the computer is off, p= owered by the CMOS battery. The RTC has its own oscillator (a 32.768 kHz cr= ystal =E2=80=94 this specific frequency is chosen because 32,768 =3D 2=C2= =B9=E2=81=B5, making it easy to divide down to exactly 1Hz for a seconds co= unter using simple binary dividers). The firmware reads the RTC at boot to = know the current date and time.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    The moment RESET= # goes high

    The tr= ansition of RESET# from low (0V) to high (3.3V) is detected by circuits ins= ide the CPU that monitor this pin. On the next rising edge of the CPU's clo= ck signal, these circuits trigger a chip-wide initialization sequence.

    This i= nitialization is not software =E2=80=94 it is purely hardware. A dedicated = reset circuit, implemented in transistors, forces every register, every int= ernal state bit, every pipeline stage register to specific values simultane= ously. The entire process completes within a single clock cycle (or a small= fixed number of cycles, depending on the CPU's internal complexity).

    From o= utside the chip, the observable result is: the CPU begins driving the addre= ss bus with the reset vector address (0xFFFFFFF0) and asserts its READ cont= rol signal. This happens automatically, mandated by hardware.

    Why the reset ve= ctor is at 0xFFFFFFF0 specifically

    The pl= acement of the reset vector at 0xFFFFFFF0 is the result of a specific engin= eering requirement: the firmware ROM must be accessible at the reset vector= address, but it must also be accessible via the standard real-mode address= 0xF0000 (for BIOS service calls during normal operation).

    This i= s solved by the motherboard's address decoder doing something clever: it ma= ps the firmware ROM to respond to addresses in TWO ranges:

    • = 0x000F0000 =E2=80=93 0x000FFFFF (the real-mode-accessible range, where BIOS= services live)
    • = 0xFFFE0000 =E2=80=93 0xFFFFFFFF (the top of the 32-bit address space, where= the reset vector lives)

    Both a= ddress ranges decode to the same physical ROM chip =E2=80=94 they are alias= es. When the CPU issues address 0xFFFFFFF0, the ROM responds. When real-mod= e code issues address 0xF0000 + offset (to call BIOS services), the same RO= M chip responds with the same data.

    This a= liasing is implemented in the chipset's address decoding logic =E2=80=94 a = combinational circuit that examines the address bus and decides which compo= nent should respond. For the firmware ROM, the chipset's decoder is wired t= o activate the ROM's chip-select for both address ranges.

    What CR0's reset= value tells us

    CR0 = =3D 0x60000010 after reset. Let us decode the bits that matter:

    Bit 0 = (PE) =3D 0: Real mode. The segmentation unit uses segment=C3=9716+offset, n= ot GDT-based protection.

    Bit 4 = =3D 1: ET (Extension Type) =E2=80=94 a historical flag from the 386 era ind= icating that the math coprocessor (FPU) uses the "387" protocol rather than= "287." Always 1 on modern CPUs. Not functionally relevant for OS developme= nt.

    Bit 29= =3D 1: NW (Not Write-through) =E2=80=94 cache write-through disabled.

    Bit 30= =3D 1: CD (Cache Disable) =E2=80=94 the CPU's caches are disabled at reset= . This is important: the caches start disabled because the cache initializa= tion process itself requires running instructions, which requires a known-g= ood CPU state. The firmware enables caches as part of its early initializat= ion sequence.

    Bit 31= (PG) =3D 0: Paging disabled. Virtual addresses equal physical addresses.

    The fa= ct that caches are disabled at reset (CD=3D1) means the CPU's very first in= structions =E2=80=94 including the jump from the reset vector to the firmwa= re body =E2=80=94 execute directly from the ROM chip without caching. This = ensures the first instructions work correctly regardless of cache state. Th= e firmware later initializes the cache (writing zeros to all cache lines vi= a a special instruction sequence called "cache as RAM" or CAR initializatio= n on some systems) before enabling it.

    Why interrupts s= tart disabled

    EFLAGS= .IF =3D 0 means hardware interrupts are disabled. This is essential.

    The ID= T (Interrupt Descriptor Table) has not been set up yet. No ISRs have been i= nstalled. If a hardware interrupt fired at this point =E2=80=94 say, the ti= mer chip asserting IRQ0 =E2=80=94 the CPU would look up vector 8 (the mappe= d vector for IRQ0 in the IBM PC's standard PIC configuration, before remapp= ing) in the IDT. But the IDT does not exist yet. The IDTR points to address= 0 (its reset value), and whatever bytes happen to be at address 0 in ROM o= r memory are not valid IDT entries. The result would be a triple fault (CPU= attempts to handle a double fault, fails, and resets).

    By sta= rting with interrupts disabled, the firmware and bootloader have safe time = to set up their interrupt infrastructure before hardware events start firin= g.

    The special natu= re of the CS shadow after reset

    We mus= t be precise about something subtle here, because it is directly relevant t= o understanding real mode.

    After = reset, CS selector =3D 0xF000, CS base =3D 0xFFFF0000 (a value that does NO= T follow the real-mode formula of CS =C3=97 16). This is a special hardware= initialization =E2=80=94 the CS base is hardwired to 0xFFFF0000 at reset, = not computed by the real-mode formula.

    After = the firmware executes its first far jump (from the reset vector to somewher= e in the 0xF0000 region), CS is loaded with a new value. From that point, C= S obeys the normal real-mode rule: base =3D CS =C3=97 16. Loading CS with 0= xF000 gives base =3D 0xF0000 =C3=97 1 =3D 0xF0000 =C3=97 16... wait =E2=80= =94 in real mode, CS holds a segment value, and the base =3D CS =C3=97 16. = So CS =3D 0xF000 gives base =3D 0xF000 =C3=97 16 =3D 0xF0000.

    So aft= er the first far jump: CS =3D 0xF000, base =3D 0x000F0000. The firmware now= executes from the normal real-mode BIOS region at 0xF0000 and above.

    This i= s why the BIOS shows up at both 0xF0000 (in the real-mode-accessible first = 1MB) and at 0xFFFFFFF0 (at the top of the 4GB space, aliased to the same RO= M) =E2=80=94 two different addresses, one physical chip, accessed at differ= ent moments in the boot sequence.

    POST =E2= =80=94 Power-On Self-Test =E2=80=94 T+~500=E2=80=93600ms

    The sectio= n text

    "T= he firmware tests the CPU, counts and tests RAM, identifies installed hardw= are, and initializes everything that must work before the OS loads. If some= thing fails, POST stops and reports an error."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    POST =E2=80=94 Power-On Self-Test =E2=80=94 The hardware verific= ation and initialization sequence the firmware performs on every boot befor= e searching for a bootable device. The word "self-test" reflects that the c= omputer is testing itself =E2=80=94 not a program the user ran, but a built= -in diagnostic routine that runs automatically.

    Firmware =E2=80=94 Software that is permanently stored in a non-= volatile memory chip (ROM or flash memory) on a hardware device, providing = the lowest-level control software for that hardware. The BIOS and UEFI are = firmware =E2=80=94 they live on a chip on the motherboard, not on the hard = disk. Unlike the OS, which is loaded from disk, the firmware is always pres= ent and always accessible from address 0xF0000 (in legacy BIOS) or wherever= the ROM is mapped.

    BIOS =E2=80=94 Basic Input/Output System =E2=80=94 The tradition= al name for x86 PC firmware, dating from the original IBM PC (1981). The BI= OS was IBM's firmware implementation =E2=80=94 it provided a standard inter= face (via software interrupts like INT 0x10 for video, INT 0x13 for disk) t= hat programs could use to interact with hardware without knowing the specif= ic hardware details. The BIOS runs in real mode, provides real-mode interru= pt handlers, and has no concept of protected mode or virtual memory.

    UEFI =E2=80=94 Unified Extensible Firmware Interface =E2=80=94 T= he modern replacement for BIOS, introduced in the 2000s (originally as EFI = by Intel for Itanium systems). UEFI provides: a 32-bit or 64-bit execution = environment (not limited to real mode), a graphical setup interface, secure= boot (cryptographic verification of bootloader signatures), support for la= rge disks (GPT partition table vs. MBR's limitations), and a standardized A= PI. Modern x86-64 computers use UEFI, though most still support a "Legacy B= IOS" or "CSM" (Compatibility Support Module) mode for backward compatibilit= y.

    CMOS =E2=80=94 We defined this above. In the context of POST, CM= OS refers specifically to the small battery-backed RAM (sometimes called NV= RAM =E2=80=94 Non-Volatile RAM) that stores BIOS/UEFI settings: boot order,= system clock, RAM speed settings, CPU overclocking settings, and hardware = configuration flags. POST reads these settings to know how to configure har= dware.

    Memory controller =E2=80=94 The component responsible for managi= ng all communication between the CPU and RAM chips. On older Intel systems = (before 2008), the memory controller lived in a separate chip called the "n= orth bridge" on the motherboard. On modern Intel and AMD systems, the memor= y controller is integrated directly into the CPU package =E2=80=94 eliminat= ing the latency of the external chip and significantly improving memory per= formance.

    SPD =E2=80=94 Serial Presence Detect =E2=80=94 A small EEPROM (E= lectrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory) chip on each RAM module= that stores the module's specifications: capacity, data width, operating v= oltages, and most importantly, timing parameters. The firmware reads SPD da= ta via a two-wire protocol called I=C2=B2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) or SM= Bus. Using SPD data, the firmware programs the memory controller with the c= orrect timing parameters for the installed RAM.

    RAM timing parameters =E2=80=94 Specific delay values that the m= emory controller uses when communicating with RAM chips. RAM chips are not = instant =E2=80=94 they require specific times between operations. Key param= eters:

    • = CAS Latency (CL) =E2=80=94 Clock cycles from sending a col= umn address to receiving data
    • = tRCD =E2=80=94 Row to Column Delay =E2=80=94 cycles betwee= n opening a row and accessing a column
    • = tRP =E2=80=94 Row Precharge time =E2=80=94 cycles to close= a row before opening another
    • = tRAS =E2=80=94 Row Active Strobe =E2=80=94 minimum cycles = a row must remain active

    If the= memory controller sends commands too fast (violating these timings), the R= AM produces unreliable data. Too slow, and performance suffers unnecessaril= y. SPD data lets the firmware automatically set optimal timings.

    Memory training =E2=80=94 A modern RAM initialization process wh= ere the firmware runs test patterns to calibrate the physical electrical ch= aracteristics of the memory bus =E2=80=94 signal timing, impedance matching= , clock phase alignment. Modern DDR4 and DDR5 systems must train their memo= ry connections on every boot (or every time RAM configuration changes), bec= ause the exact delay values depend on the specific physical characteristics= of the PCB traces between the CPU and RAM slots.

    I=C2=B2C =E2=80=94 Inter-Integrated Circuit =E2=80=94 A simple t= wo-wire communication protocol for low-speed communication between chips on= the same circuit board. Uses two lines: SCL (clock) and SDA (data). The fi= rmware drives the SCL clock, and devices respond on SDA. SPD EEPROMs on RAM= modules are accessed via I=C2=B2C/SMBus.

    PCI =E2=80=94 Peripheral Component Interconnect =E2=80=94 A para= llel bus standard for connecting peripheral devices to the motherboard. Ori= ginal PCI (from the early 1990s) used a shared 32-bit bus at 33 MHz. Multip= le PCI devices shared the same bus wires, communicating in turn.

    PCIe =E2=80=94 PCI Express =E2=80=94 The modern replacement for = PCI (and AGP), introduced in 2003. Unlike parallel PCI where all devices sh= are wires, PCIe uses point-to-point serial links =E2=80=94 each device has = its own dedicated lanes directly connected to the CPU's PCIe controller. PC= Ie x1 is one lane (one pair of wires each direction), x4 is four lanes, x8 = is eight, x16 is sixteen. Graphics cards use x16 slots for maximum bandwidt= h. Each PCIe lane on PCIe 5.0 provides about 4 GB/s in each direction.

    Configuration space =E2=80=94 A standardized 256-byte (PCI) or 4= KB (PCIe) region of data that every PCIe device exposes, containing: Vendor= ID (who made this device), Device ID (what specific device this is), Class= Code (what type of device =E2=80=94 display, storage, network, etc.), and = Base Address Registers (BARs) that describe what memory ranges or I/O ports= the device needs. The firmware reads these to identify and configure all i= nstalled devices.

    Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) =E2=80=94 In real mode, a 1KB regio= n at physical addresses 0x000=E2=80=930x3FF that maps interrupt numbers to = handler addresses. Each entry is 4 bytes: 2 bytes of IP (offset) followed b= y 2 bytes of CS (segment). Entry N at address 4=C3=97N. When the CPU execut= es INT N in real mode, it reads the 4-byte entry at address 4=C3=97N, loads= CS and IP from those bytes, and jumps to CS:IP. The firmware fills this ta= ble with pointers to its own interrupt handlers during POST.

    I/O port 0x80 =E2=80=94 A specific port address that, on the IBM= PC architecture, is connected to nothing (or to a diagnostic LED header on= server motherboards). Writing to port 0x80 causes a brief delay (due to th= e bus cycle time) and has no side effects =E2=80=94 the written value is no= t stored anywhere useful. But the value can be read by an external diagnost= ic device called a POST card (a small board plugged into a PCIe or PCI slot= that reads and displays port 0x80 values). The firmware writes a unique co= de to 0x80 before each POST step =E2=80=94 if the machine freezes during PO= ST, you can read the last code to identify which step failed.

    Beep codes =E2=80=94 Audio patterns produced by the system speak= er during POST failures that occur before video is initialized. The origina= l IBM PC used a single speaker driven by the 8254 timer chip (connected via= the PPI =E2=80=94 Programmable Peripheral Interface). Different patterns o= f short and long beeps (defined by the BIOS vendor =E2=80=94 AMI, Award, Ph= oenix each had different codes) indicated specific failures: one long beep = + three short =3D video error, for example. On modern systems, some motherb= oards have moved to LED-based POST indicators instead of beep codes.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    Why POST is nece= ssary before any OS code can run

    The op= erating system =E2=80=94 when it eventually gets control =E2=80=94 will ass= ume a working computer. It assumes the CPU executes instructions correctly.= It assumes RAM holds values reliably. It assumes devices are configured wi= th non-conflicting memory ranges. It assumes the interrupt infrastructure i= s in place.

    None o= f these things are true immediately after power is applied. The CPU has had= no self-verification (besides the hardware state machine that initialized = registers =E2=80=94 but this does not verify that the ALU computes correctl= y). RAM chips are uninitialized =E2=80=94 they contain random values, becau= se their internal state is undefined until the memory controller sends init= ialization commands. Devices have been reset to their power-on default stat= es, which often includes undefined or overlapping resource assignments.

    POST c= reates the required environment, step by step.

    The sequence of = POST, in physical detail

    Step 1 =E2=80=94 CPU self-test (BIST =E2=80=94 Built-In Self-Test)

    Modern= x86 CPUs support a Built-In Self-Test mode. The firmware can trigger this = by placing a specific value in a register and executing a BIST instruction.= The CPU runs its own internal test patterns, verifying that its caches, TL= Bs, and execution units function correctly. This takes 1=E2=80=932ms on a m= odern CPU. If BIST fails, EAX contains a non-zero error code. The firmware = checks this and halts if a CPU failure is detected.

    Step 2 =E2=80=94 Initialize the chipset

    The CP= U can read and write the chipset's configuration registers via the PCI conf= iguration mechanism (even before full PCI initialization). The firmware pro= grams essential chipset settings: memory controller base configuration, PCI= e controller enabling, clock routing, power management settings. This step = uses hard-coded values in the BIOS ROM for the specific chipset models the = motherboard supports.

    Step 3 =E2=80=94 Memory controller initialization and SPD reading

    The fi= rmware programs the memory controller with initial (conservative) timing se= ttings. It then reads SPD data from each installed RAM module via I=C2=B2C.= Using the SPD data, it programs the memory controller with the optimal tim= ings for the installed RAM.

    Step 4 =E2=80=94 Memory training

    On mod= ern DDR4/DDR5 systems, the firmware runs an extensive training sequence: it= sends test patterns to the RAM and measures the delay windows within which= the signals are reliably received. This calibrates parameters like receive= r training, write leveling, read timing. This step takes several seconds on= consumer systems (which is one reason modern computers take longer to boot= from a cold start than from sleep/hibernate =E2=80=94 POST must retrain me= mory on every cold boot on many systems).

    Step 5 =E2=80=94 Memory test

    After = training, the firmware performs a basic memory test. The scope varies by fi= rmware =E2=80=94 a minimal test might write 0xAA then 0x55 to each location= and read back; a thorough test runs multiple patterns at every address. A = minimal test that just checks for obviously faulty cells takes about 1 seco= nd per GB.

    After = testing, the firmware knows how much RAM is installed and that it is functi= onal. It stores the memory map (which address ranges are available and whic= h are reserved for hardware) for later retrieval by the OS.

    Step 6 =E2=80=94 PCI/PCIe device enumeration

    The fi= rmware walks the PCIe/PCI bus. PCI devices are addressable via a special me= chanism: the firmware writes a 32-bit "config address" to I/O port 0xCF8 (s= electing a specific device's configuration space register) and reads/writes= port 0xCFC (the "config data" register). This mechanism (called "Config Me= chanism 1") is standardized across all x86 PCI systems.

    The fi= rmware reads the Vendor ID from each potential device location. Vendor ID 0= xFFFF means "no device present" (the bus returns all 1s when nothing is the= re). Any other value means a device is present.

    For ea= ch found device, the firmware:

    • = Reads its Class Code to understand what type it is (0x0300 =3D VGA display,= 0x0101 =3D IDE storage, 0x0200 =3D ethernet, etc.)
    • = Reads its Base Address Registers (BARs) to find what resources it needs
    • = Programs each BAR with a system-wide unique address range (no two devices c= an have overlapping ranges)
    • = Assigns IRQ numbers (via the PIC or APIC routing table) to devices that nee= d interrupts

    Step 7 =E2=80=94 Initialize the video subsystem

    The di= splay adapter (GPU) contains its own firmware =E2=80=94 called the Video BI= OS or VBIOS =E2=80=94 stored in a ROM on the GPU card itself. The firmware = reads the VBIOS from the GPU's ROM and executes it (this is called "Option = ROM execution"). The VBIOS initializes the GPU, sets up a video mode (typic= ally 80=C3=9725 text mode for legacy BIOS), and installs INT 0x10 (video in= terrupt) handlers.

    After = this step, the firmware can display text on screen =E2=80=94 which is why y= ou see the BIOS splash screen or POST output. Everything before this step h= as been silent.

    Step 8 =E2=80=94 Install IVT handlers

    The fi= rmware writes the Interrupt Vector Table =E2=80=94 filling addresses 0x000= =E2=80=930x3FF with CS:IP pointers to real-mode interrupt service routines.= These handlers implement:

    • = INT 0x10: Video services (print character, set video mode, read cursor posi= tion)
    • = INT 0x13: Disk services (read sectors, write sectors, check drive presence)=
    • = INT 0x16: Keyboard services (read keypress, check for pending keypress)
    • = INT 0x15: System services (memory map query, A20 enable/disable)
    • = INT 0x1A: Time/date services (read/write RTC)
    • = And many others

    These = handlers are located in the BIOS ROM at 0xF0000. The IVT entries are just 4= -byte CS:IP pointers into this region.

    Step 9 =E2=80=94 BIOS Data Area

    The fi= rmware fills the BIOS Data Area (BDA) at 0x400=E2=80=930x4FF with hardware = configuration information: number of serial ports and their I/O port addres= ses, number of parallel ports, number of floppy drives, equipment flags, ke= yboard state (shift key status, caps lock status), current video mode. This= data is used by the IVT handlers and may be read by the bootloader.

    Step 10 =E2=80=94 POST complete signal

    The fi= rmware writes 0x00 to port 0x80 (a conventional POST code meaning "all POST= tests passed") and sounds one short beep on the system speaker (the univer= sal "all OK" signal from AMI/Award BIOSes). On modern UEFI systems, there i= s typically no beep by default.

    What happens if = POST finds a failure

    Before video is initialized (very early failures):

    No dis= play is possible. The firmware can only communicate via beep codes and port= 0x80 codes. It stops here =E2=80=94 it cannot proceed because the fundamen= tal hardware needed for any further operation is broken.

    After video is initialized but before boot:

    The fi= rmware displays an error message. It may offer to enter the BIOS setup to c= hange settings (for example, if RAM was recently changed and is not trainin= g properly, you might be prompted to reset to default timings). It may halt= entirely, waiting for the user to power off and fix the hardware.

    Soft errors:

    Some P= OST failures are non-fatal. A missing optional device (like a second optica= l drive) is logged but does not halt POST. The firmware continues and boots= normally, possibly with that device absent from subsequent configuration.<= /p>

    Boot Devic= e Selection =E2=80=94 T+~600=E2=80=931500ms

    The sectio= n text

    "P= OST complete. The firmware now finds a bootable device. It checks the boot = order =E2=80=94 each device in sequence =E2=80=94 looking for a valid boot = sector signature."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    Boot order =E2=80=94 The prioritized list of devices the firmwar= e checks for a bootable operating system. Stored in CMOS/NVRAM, configurabl= e in the BIOS/UEFI setup menu. Typical examples: USB first (so you can boot= from a USB drive for installation or recovery), then internal SSD/HDD, the= n optical drive. The firmware checks each device in order, stopping at the = first bootable one found.

    Sector =E2=80=94 The smallest addressable unit of storage on a d= isk. Historically 512 bytes per sector (established by IBM and maintained a= s the standard for decades). Modern drives use 4,096-byte (4KB) "Advanced F= ormat" sectors internally but typically emulate 512-byte sectors to softwar= e for compatibility (called "512e" =E2=80=94 512 emulation). An NVMe SSD us= es "blocks" rather than sectors but the concept is identical.

    LBA =E2=80=94 Logical Block Addressing =E2=80=94 A way of addres= sing disk sectors by sequential number, starting from 0. LBA 0 is the first= sector, LBA 1 is the second, and so on through all sectors on the disk. LB= A replaced the older CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector) addressing, which required = knowing the physical geometry of the disk platters. All modern firmware use= s LBA.

    MBR =E2=80=94 Master Boot Record =E2=80=94 The first sector (LBA= 0) of a disk, traditionally exactly 512 bytes. Contains: up to 446 bytes o= f bootloader code, four 16-byte partition entries describing the disk's par= tition layout, and the 2-byte boot signature 0x55 0xAA at the very end. The= MBR is the structure the BIOS reads to determine if a disk is bootable and= to begin the boot process.

    0x55 0xAA =E2=80=94 Boot signature (magic bytes) =E2=80=94 The t= wo bytes at offsets 510 and 511 (0-indexed) of the MBR that the firmware ch= ecks to determine if this is a valid boot sector. In memory order: byte at = offset 510 =3D 0x55, byte at offset 511 =3D 0xAA. Note the apparent reversa= l: when stored on disk in little-endian order and read as a 16-bit value, 0= xAA55 is at offset 510 (low byte 0x55 then high byte 0xAA). This is why in = x86 assembly you write dw 0xAA55 =E2=80=94 you are writing a 16-bit little= -endian value whose bytes appear as 0x55 followed by 0xAA in memory order.<= /p>

    Partition =E2=80=94 A defined region of a disk =E2=80=94 a conti= guous range of sectors designated for a specific purpose. A disk can be div= ided into multiple partitions, each formatted with its own file system. Par= tition information is stored in the MBR's partition table (for MBR-style di= sks) or in the GPT (for modern disks).

    Partition table =E2=80=94 A data structure on the disk that desc= ribes how the disk is divided into partitions. The MBR format supports up t= o 4 partitions, each described by a 16-byte entry. Each entry contains: a "= bootable" flag byte, CHS start and end addresses, LBA start address, and se= ctor count. The firmware reads the partition table to understand the disk's= layout.

    GPT =E2=80=94 GUID Partition Table =E2=80=94 The modern replacem= ent for the MBR partition table, required for disks larger than 2TB (the MB= R format's maximum with 32-bit LBA) and used with UEFI. GPT stores partitio= n information in multiple redundant locations on the disk (beginning and en= d) and supports up to 128 partitions with 64-bit LBA addresses (supporting = disks up to 9.4 ZB =E2=80=94 zettabytes, incomprehensibly large). GPT uses = GUIDs (Globally Unique Identifiers =E2=80=94 128-bit random numbers) to ide= ntify partition types and individual partitions.

    GUID =E2=80=94 Globally Unique Identifier =E2=80=94 A 128-bit nu= mber used as a unique identifier. Generated using algorithms that combine t= imestamps, random numbers, and hardware identifiers to make collision (two = different things getting the same GUID) essentially impossible. Written in = a standard hyphenated hex format: xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx.

    EFI System Partition (ESP) =E2=80=94 On UEFI systems, a special = partition formatted with FAT32 that stores UEFI bootloader files. The UEFI = firmware can read FAT32 directly (UEFI includes a FAT32 driver). Boot files= for each OS are stored in subdirectories: Windows stores its bootloader at= \EFI\Microsoft\Boot\bootmgfw.efi, Linux distributions typically use \EFI&l= t;distro>\grub.efi or similar.

    FAT32 =E2=80=94 A file system (File Allocation Table, 32-bit ver= sion) originally developed by Microsoft for Windows 95. Simple, widely supp= orted, and chosen for the ESP because virtually all firmware can implement = its reader. FAT32 uses a File Allocation Table to track which clusters belo= ng to which files (the same concept as the FAT file system described in the= program lifecycle section).

    CHS =E2=80=94 Cylinder-Head-Sector =E2=80=94 The legacy disk add= ressing scheme based on physical disk geometry. A hard disk has multiple pl= atters (cylinders), each platter has two surfaces (one per head), and each = track is divided into sectors. CHS addressing specifies location as (cylind= er number, head number, sector number). Limited to disks of ~504MB (with cl= assic BIOS limits) or ~8GB (with extended BIOS), which is why LBA replaced = it. Your BIOS disk read calls still use CHS in some parameters, but modern = firmware translates internally to LBA.

    CMOS battery =E2=80=94 A small lithium coin cell battery (typica= lly CR2032, 3V) on the motherboard that powers the CMOS RAM and RTC when th= e computer is off. Lasts 5=E2=80=9310 years typically. When it dies, the co= mputer loses time synchronization (clock resets to a default date) and BIOS= settings (returns to factory defaults). "CMOS battery" is a slight misnome= r =E2=80=94 the battery doesn't power the CMOS logic (which is powered by 5= VSB when plugged in) but only the CMOS RAM and RTC when unplugged.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    How the firmware= reads the disk

    When t= he firmware needs to read a disk sector, it uses the disk controller =E2=80= =94 a chip (or integrated circuit in the CPU/chipset) that manages the phys= ical communication with the storage device.

    For a = SATA hard drive or SSD, the firmware communicates with the AHCI (Advanced H= ost Controller Interface) controller via memory-mapped registers that were = assigned during PCI enumeration. The firmware writes a command to the contr= oller's command port: "read N sectors starting at LBA address X and place t= he data at memory address Y." The AHCI controller manages the actual SATA p= rotocol communication with the drive and uses DMA (Direct Memory Access) to= transfer the data directly into RAM without the CPU needing to be involved= byte-by-byte.

    For a = USB drive, the firmware uses the XHCI (eXtensible Host Controller Interface= ) controller and the USB Mass Storage protocol. The USB protocol stack is m= ore complex =E2=80=94 multiple layers of command wrapping =E2=80=94 but the= end result is the same: sectors from the USB device appear in RAM.

    For an= NVMe SSD (connected directly via PCIe), the firmware uses the NVMe protoco= l =E2=80=94 a much simpler and faster command set designed specifically for= flash-based storage.

    In all= cases, after the transfer completes, a 512-byte (or more) buffer in RAM co= ntains the data from LBA 0 of the device. The firmware checks bytes 510=E2= =80=93511 of this buffer. If they are 0x55 and 0xAA respectively, the devic= e is bootable.

    The magic bytes = =E2=80=94 why 0x55AA

    The ch= oice of 0x55AA (stored as 0x55 then 0xAA in byte order) is partly historica= l convention from the original IBM PC BIOS, but it has a useful property: 0= x55 =3D 01010101 in binary and 0xAA =3D 10101010 in binary. Together these = two bytes use all 16 bits in an alternating pattern (0101010110101010). Thi= s alternating pattern is useful for testing data paths =E2=80=94 if you rec= eive 0x55AA correctly, you know that the data bus is transmitting all 16 bi= ts correctly (no stuck-high or stuck-low bits). It was also unlikely to occ= ur by accident at the end of an arbitrary, non-boot sector.

    The 0x7C00 addre= ss =E2=80=94 why specifically

    This i= s a specific historical decision from the IBM PC BIOS design. The value 0x7= C00 was chosen for two reasons:

    First,= the IBM PC had 32KB of RAM guaranteed (the minimum configuration). 32KB = =3D 0x8000. The BIOS wanted to load the boot sector near the top of this gu= aranteed-present RAM while keeping the IVT (at 0x0000=E2=80=930x03FF) and B= DA (at 0x0400=E2=80=930x04FF) intact.

    Second= , the boot sector itself is 512 bytes (0x200), and the BIOS wanted a stack = below it. 0x7C00 - 0x200 =3D 0x7A00 (leave room below for stack). 0x7C00 + = 0x200 =3D 0x7E00 (end of MBR code, beginning of free space above).

    So the= memory map during boot looks like:

    0x0000=E2=80=930x03FF : IVT
0x0400=E2=80=930x04FF : BDA
0x0500=E2=80=930x7BFF : Free (stack grows down from 0x7C00)
0x7C00=E2=80=930x7DFF : Boot sector (512 bytes loaded here)
0x7E00=E2=80=930x7FFF : Free space above boot sector
...

    The st= ack grows downward from 0x7C00 into the free space, and additional bootload= er stages can be loaded at 0x7E00 or anywhere else in free RAM.

    BIOS Loads= and Jumps to the MBR =E2=80=94 T+~1500ms

    The sectio= n text

    "T= he firmware copies the 512-byte MBR to address 0x7C00 in RAM and jumps to i= t. Control leaves the firmware permanently. Your code runs."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    Far jump =E2=80=94 An x86 jump instruction that changes both CS = (Code Segment) and IP (Instruction Pointer) simultaneously, as opposed to a= near jump which only changes IP (staying within the same code segment). A = far jump to 0x0000:0x7C00 means: set CS to 0x0000 and IP to 0x7C00. The phy= sical address of the next instruction will be (0x0000 =C3=97 16) + 0x7C00 = =3D 0x7C00. This is how the BIOS hands control to the MBR.

    Drive number =E2=80=94 A convention used by BIOS disk services t= o identify which drive to access. Values: 0x00 =3D first floppy drive (A:),= 0x01 =3D second floppy (B:), 0x80 =3D first hard disk (C: in DOS), 0x81 = =3D second hard disk, and so on. The high bit (0x80) distinguishes hard dis= ks from floppy drives. USB drives typically appear as 0x80 (if set up as th= e primary boot device, the BIOS maps them there). NVMe SSDs on UEFI systems= use a completely different identification mechanism.

    DL register =E2=80=94 The low byte of the DX register (per the 8= 086 architecture document =E2=80=94 DH is high byte, DL is low byte). The B= IOS sets DL to the drive number before jumping to the MBR, providing the bo= otloader with information about which drive it was loaded from. This is nec= essary because the bootloader needs to read additional sectors from the sam= e drive, and INT 0x13 needs the drive number.

    ORG directive =E2=80=94 An assembler directive (instruction to t= he assembler, not an actual CPU instruction) that tells the assembler: "ass= ume that this code will be located at this address when it executes." The a= ssembler uses this to compute the correct values for labels and memory refe= rences. message: after some instructions, the assembler co= mputes its address as 0x7C00 plus the offset of that label from the beginni= ng of the code.

    NASM =E2=80=94 Netwide Assembler =E2=80=94 a popular, free x86 a= ssembler. It takes assembly language source code (human-readable mnemonics = like m= ov ax, 0x1234) and produces binary machine code. NASM is commonly us= ed for OS development bootloaders because it produces flat binary output (j= ust the raw instruction bytes, no ELF or PE headers) which is exactly what = the boot sector format requires. The command nasm -f bin bootloader.asm -o bootlo= ader.bin produces a raw binary file.

    Segment register loading =E2=80=94 The process of loading a new = value into a segment register (CS, DS, SS, ES). As covered in the 8086 arch= itecture document, in real mode this is straightforward =E2=80=94 the value= loaded into DS becomes the segment base =C3=B7 16. Loading DS with 0x0000 = means all data accesses have segment base 0 (DS:offset =3D 0=C3=9716 + offs= et =3D offset). Most bootloaders begin with explicit DS setup because the B= IOS may have left segment registers at arbitrary values.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    The final BIOS o= perations before handoff

    After = finding a bootable device (checking 0x55AA at bytes 510=E2=80=93511 of LBA = 0), the BIOS:

    1 =E2=80=94 Copies the MBR to 0x7C00:

    The BI= OS has already read LBA 0 into a temporary buffer (somewhere in the BIOS's = own working memory, typically in the area 0x600=E2=80=930x7BFF). It copies = those 512 bytes to 0x7C00 using a simple memory copy loop. After this, the = machine code bytes of your bootloader are sitting in RAM at addresses 0x7C0= 0=E2=80=930x7DFF.

    2 =E2=80=94 Validates (again) and sets DL:

    Some B= IOSes validate the signature again after copying (to confirm the copy succe= eded). The BIOS sets DL =3D the drive number (0x80 for the first hard disk,= the typical case). Other registers may be set to implementation-specific v= alues =E2=80=94 the BIOS specification does not guarantee the state of most= registers, only DL.

    3 =E2=80=94 The far jump:

    The BI= OS executes something equivalent to JMP FAR 0x0000:0x7C00. This loads CS = =3D 0x0000 and IP =3D 0x7C00. The CPU's next fetch-decode-execute cycle fet= ches from CS.base + IP =3D 0 + 0x7C00 =3D 0x7C00. The first byte at 0x7C00 = is decoded as the first instruction of your bootloader.

    From t= his moment: the BIOS has no more active role. Its code continues to sit in = ROM at 0xF0000, and its IVT handlers remain at the addresses installed duri= ng POST. But the CPU is no longer executing BIOS code =E2=80=94 it is execu= ting your code.

    What your bootlo= ader's first instruction should do

    The bo= otloader begins with no guarantees about most register values. It must esta= blish its own environment before doing anything useful. The canonical first= instructions of a boot sector:

    asm
    [BITS 16] ; Tell NASM we're generating 16-bit c= ode [ORG 0x7C00] ; Tell NASM code is loaded at 0x7C0= 0 start: ; Disable interrupts while setting up segment registers cli ; Set up segment registers explicitly ; We cannot trust whatever values the BIOS left xor ax, ax ; AX =3D 0 mov ds, ax ; DS =3D 0 (data segment base =3D 0) mov es, ax ; ES =3D 0 (extra segment base =3D 0) mov ss, ax ; SS =3D 0 (stack segment base =3D 0) mov sp, 0x7C00 ; Stack grows downward from 0x7C00 ; (into free space below our code) ; Re-enable interrupts now that segments are set up sti ; Save the drive number (BIOS set DL before jumping here) mov [boot_drive], dl ; ... proceed with loading additional sectors ...

    The cli (Clear Interrupt Flag) before setting up segment registers is important:= if an interrupt fired while SS was 0 but SP had not yet been set to a vali= d value, the interrupt handler would try to push registers onto the stack a= t SS:SP =3D 0:0 =E2=80=94 address 0 =E2=80=94 which would corrupt the IVT. = The brief cli/sti window around segment setup prevents this.

    Stage 1 Bo= otloader =E2=80=94 The 510-Byte Problem

    The sectio= n text

    "T= he entire first-stage bootloader must fit in 510 bytes (512 minus the 2-byt= e signature). Its only job: load a larger second-stage bootloader from disk= ."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    INT 0x13 =E2=80=94 BIOS Disk Interrupt =E2=80=94 One of the soft= ware interrupt vectors installed by the BIOS during POST. Calling INT 0x13 = with specific register values invokes different disk service functions. AH = selects the function: AH=3D0x00 resets the disk, AH=3D0x02 reads sectors, A= H=3D0x03 writes sectors, AH=3D0x42 reads via LBA extension (modern), etc.

    AH register =E2=80=94 The high byte of AX. When calling BIOS ser= vices, AH typically specifies the function number. This is a convention fro= m the original IBM BIOS: AH selects what to do, other registers provide par= ameters.

    Cylinder =E2=80=94 In a hard disk drive, a cylinder is the set o= f all tracks at the same radial position across all platters. If a drive ha= s 3 platters (6 surfaces) and each surface has track 42, then cylinder 42 c= onsists of all 6 of those tracks. The term comes from the shape: if you cou= ld see all the tracks at position 42 on all platters simultaneously, they w= ould form a cylinder.

    Head =E2=80=94 A read/write head is the component that reads fro= m or writes to the disk surface. Each disk surface has one read/write head.= A 3-platter drive has 6 heads (two per platter =E2=80=94 one for each surf= ace). CHS head number selects which surface (and therefore which track with= in the cylinder).

    Sector (CHS context) =E2=80=94 In CHS addressing, sectors are nu= mbered within each track, starting from 1 (not 0 =E2=80=94 a historical qui= rk). Each track is divided into an equal number of sectors.

    INT 0x13 extensions =E2=80=94 LBA mode =E2=80=94 An extension to= the original INT 0x13 that adds LBA-addressed disk functions. Function AH= =3D0x41 checks if extensions are present. Function AH=3D0x42 reads sectors = by LBA number using a "Disk Address Packet" =E2=80=94 a small structure in = memory that specifies the LBA start, sector count, and destination address.= Modern bootloaders use LBA mode because CHS addressing breaks for disks la= rger than ~8GB.

    Disk Address Packet (DAP) =E2=80=94 A small structure in memory = used with INT 0x13 extension function 0x42:

    Offset 0: Size of packet (0x10 =3D 16 bytes)
Offset 1: Reserved (0)
Offset 2: Number of sectors to transfer (16-bit)
Offset 3: Reserved (0)
Offset 4: Destination offset (16-bit)
Offset 6: Destination segment (16-bit)
Offset 8: LBA start address (64-bit)
    =

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    The 510-byte con= straint and what it forces

    512 by= tes total in the MBR. 2 bytes are the boot signature (0x55, 0xAA at offsets= 510=E2=80=93511). The MBR partition table occupies 64 bytes at offsets 446= =E2=80=93509 (four 16-byte entries). This leaves 446 bytes for bootstrap co= de in a standard MBR layout.

    446 by= tes. For a real OS bootloader, this is almost nothing. GRUB's Stage 1 (whic= h lives in the MBR) uses essentially all 446 bytes just to locate and load = its Stage 1.5.

    For a = simple OS project (like Nick Blundell's tutorial), the 512-byte MBR can con= tain the entire bootloader =E2=80=94 a small amount of code that switches t= o protected mode and loads a tiny kernel. This is possible because:

    • = The kernel being loaded is very small (a few KB)
    • = The bootloader is minimal (no filesystem parsing, just raw sector reads)
    • = The protected mode switch is compact in binary (the actual instructions are= only ~50 bytes)

    A real= istic real-world bootloader (for a full OS with filesystem support) needs m= ultiple stages:

    Stage 1 (MBR, 446 bytes): Find the active (bootable) partition, = locate Stage 1.5 or Stage 2 on that partition, load it into RAM, jump to it= .

    Stage 1.5 (~32KB, lives in the sectors between MBR and first partition):<= /strong> Contains filesystem drivers (so it can read Stage 2 from the files= ystem). GRUB uses this layer.

    Stage 2 (hundreds of KB): The full bootloader with menu, graphic= s, compression support, multiple OS support, module loading.

    The actual disk = read in assembly

    A mini= mal bootloader reading additional sectors using INT 0x13 function 0x02 (CHS= mode, for simplicity):

    asm
    ; Read sectors using BIOS INT 0x13 function 0x02 ; Assumptions: disk uses CHS, reading from the sector i= mmediately after MBR load_kernel: mov ah, 0x02 ; Function: read sectors mov al, 0x01 ; Number of sectors to read: 1 mov ch, 0x00 ; Cylinder: 0 mov cl, 0x02 ; Sector: 2 (sector 1 is the MBR; nex= t is sector 2) mov dh, 0x00 ; Head: 0 ; DL =3D drive number, already set from BIOS (saved earlie= r) mov dl, [boot_drive] ; ES:BX =3D destination address mov bx, 0x0000 mov es, bx ; ES =3D 0 mov bx, 0x7E00 ; BX =3D 0x7E00 (right after MBR in R= AM) int 0x13 ; Call BIOS disk service jc disk_error ; If carry flag set, an error occurre= d ; The kernel is now loaded at 0x7E00 jmp 0x7E00 ; Jump to it

    The ca= rry flag (CF) is set by INT 0x13 if an error occurred =E2=80=94 a BIOS conv= ention for returning error status. AH contains an error code if CF is set.<= /p>

    Stage 2 Bo= otloader =E2=80=94 Setting Up for the OS

    The sectio= n text

    "T= he Stage 2 bootloader enables A20, builds a minimal GDT, switches to protec= ted mode, and jumps to the kernel."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    A20 line =E2=80=94 The 21st address line of the CPU (counting fr= om A0), named A20 because it is the 21st bit (bit position 20) of the addre= ss bus, representing a value of 2=C2=B2=E2=81=B0 =3D 1,048,576 =E2=80=94 ex= actly 1MB. The A20 line controls access to physical addresses at and above = 1MB. The full story:

    The or= iginal 8086 had only A0=E2=80=93A19 (20 address lines). Any address calcula= tion that overflowed 20 bits (wrapped around past 1MB) automatically wrappe= d to 0 =E2=80=94 because there was no A20 to carry the overflow. Some progr= ams deliberately relied on this wrap-around behavior.

    When t= he 286 added A20 (plus A21 and A22 and A23 for its 24-bit address bus), the= wrap-around behavior disappeared =E2=80=94 addresses above 1MB worked corr= ectly, and the "overflow wraps to 0" trick broke programs that relied on it= .

    IBM's = solution in the IBM PC AT (which used the 286): add a gate circuit that phy= sically disconnects the A20 line, forcing it to always output 0. This resto= red the wrap-around behavior. The gate was controlled by a bit in the keybo= ard controller (the Intel 8042 chip, which IBM happened to have in their ke= yboard controller circuit and which had a spare output pin).

    The 80= 42 keyboard controller's output port bit 1 became the "A20 gate" control. W= hen bit 1 =3D 0: A20 is gated off (stuck at 0 =E2=80=94 addresses above 1MB= wrap to 0). When bit 1 =3D 1: A20 is enabled (addresses above 1MB work cor= rectly).

    Enabling A20 =E2=80=94 Multiple methods exist because different = systems implement A20 control differently:

    Method 1 =E2=80=94 BIOS INT 0x15, AX=3D0x2401: Ask the BIOS to e= nable A20. Simplest. Not all BIOSes support this.

    Method 2 =E2=80=94 Keyboard controller (8042): Send a "write out= put port" command to the 8042 and set bit 1 of the output port. Slow (the 8= 042 is slow to respond =E2=80=94 need to wait for "ready" status). The clas= sic method from the 286 era.

    Method 3 =E2=80=94 Fast A20 (port 0x92): A later addition where = many chipsets provide a bit in I/O port 0x92 that directly controls A20 (bi= t 1 =3D A20 enable). Faster than the keyboard controller method. Not univer= sally supported.

    Method 4 =E2=80=94 A20 is already enabled: Many modern systems (= virtualized and physical) enable A20 automatically during POST and never re= quire the bootloader to enable it. QEMU, for example, often has A20 already= enabled when your bootloader starts.

    Robust bootloaders try multiple methods: Enable via BIOS first, = then check if A20 is actually enabled (by testing whether writing to an add= ress above 1MB is visible =E2=80=94 a specific test pattern), then try port= 0x92, then try the keyboard controller.

    DMA =E2=80=94 Direct Memory Access =E2=80=94 A mechanism allowin= g hardware devices to transfer data directly to and from RAM without the CP= U being involved in each byte transfer. The disk controller reads from disk= and writes directly to a specified RAM address =E2=80=94 the CPU merely in= itiates the transfer and receives an interrupt when complete. This frees th= e CPU to do other work while the (slow) disk transfer happens.

    A20 test =E2=80=94 To verify A20 is enabled, bootloaders often u= se a specific test: write a distinctive value to an address just above 1MB = (e.g., 0x100500). Read the address that would be aliased to it if A20 were = disabled (0x000500 =E2=80=94 same address with bit 20 cleared). If the valu= es match (the write at 0x100500 is visible at 0x000500), A20 is still disab= led =E2=80=94 the addresses are the same due to bit 20 being gated off. If = the values differ, A20 is enabled =E2=80=94 they are genuinely different ph= ysical locations.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    The complete Sta= ge 2 sequence in detail

    Enabling A20:

    asm
    ; Method: Fast A20 via port 0x92 in al, 0x92 ; Read current value of port 0x92 or al, 2 ; Set bit 1 (A20 enable) and al, 0xFE ; Clear bit 0 (reset bit =E2=80=94 don't acci= dentally reset!) out 0x92, al ; Write back

    Loading the kernel from disk (while still in real mode, using BI= OS services):

    The ke= rnel binary was produced by compiling C kernel code. The bootloader knows (= by convention =E2=80=94 hardcoded address or configuration) which sectors o= n the disk contain the kernel. It reads those sectors into memory at 0x1000= 00 (1MB) =E2=80=94 this requires A20 to be enabled, because 0x100000 is the= first address that requires the A20 line.

    Building the GDT:

    In mem= ory (a data area declared in the bootloader's assembly source), define the = GDT:

    asm
    gdt_start: ; Entry 0: Null descriptor (required) dd 0x00000000 dd 0x00000000 ; Entry 1: Code descriptor (selector =3D 0x08) ; Base=3D0, Limit=3D4GB, DPL=3D0, 32-bit, executable, read= able dw 0xFFFF ; Limit bits 0-15 dw 0x0000 ; Base bits 0-15 db 0x00 ; Base bits 16-23 db 10011010b ; Access byte: Present, DPL=3D0, Code, Exe= cutable, Readable db 11001111b ; Flags + Limit bits 16-19: G=3D1(4KB), D= =3D1(32-bit), L=3D0, Limit=3D0xF db 0x00 ; Base bits 24-31 ; Entry 2: Data descriptor (selector =3D 0x10) ; Base=3D0, Limit=3D4GB, DPL=3D0, 32-bit, writable dw 0xFFFF ; Limit bits 0-15 dw 0x0000 ; Base bits 0-15 db 0x00 ; Base bits 16-23 db 10010010b ; Access byte: Present, DPL=3D0, Data, Wri= table db 11001111b ; Flags + Limit bits 16-19: G=3D1, D=3D1, = L=3D0, Limit=3D0xF db 0x00 ; Base bits 24-31 gdt_end: gdt_descriptor: dw gdt_end - gdt_start - 1 ; GDT size - 1 (the limit) dd gdt_start ; Physical address of GDT

    Loading GDTR and switching:

    asm
    ; Load the GDT lgdt [gdt_descriptor] ; Disable interrupts (must be off during mode switch) cli ; Set PE bit in CR0 mov eax, cr0 or eax, 1 ; Set bit 0 (PE =3D Protection Enable) mov cr0, eax ; Immediately far jump to flush prefetch queue and load ; CS with the code descriptor selector (0x08) jmp 0x08:protected_mode_start [BITS 32] protected_mode_start: ; Now in 32-bit protected mode ; Load data segment registers with data descriptor (0x10) mov ax, 0x10 mov ds, ax mov es, ax mov fs, ax mov gs, ax mov ss, ax mov esp, 0x090000 ; Set up a temporary kernel stack ; Jump to kernel entry point (loaded at 0x100000) call 0x100000 ; If kernel returns (it shouldn't), halt hlt

    The si= ngle most important instruction in this sequence is jmp 0x08:protected_mode_start= =E2=80=94 the far jump that simultaneously:

    1. = Flushes the instruction prefetch queue (which may contain bytes fetched und= er 16-bit real-mode assumptions)
    2. = Loads CS with selector 0x08 (pointing to our 32-bit code descriptor in the = GDT)
    3. = Sets CPL =3D RPL bits of 0x08 =3D 00 =3D Ring 0

    From t= he [BI= TS 32] label onward, the assembler generates 32-bit instructions (de= fault operand and address sizes of 32 bits, not 16). The CPU decodes them a= s 32-bit instructions because the D/B bit in the code descriptor (entry 0x0= 8) is set to 1.

    Kernel Ent= ry =E2=80=94 First Kernel Instruction =E2=80=94 T+~1800ms

    The sectio= n text

    "T= he kernel begins. The CPU is in 32-bit protected mode. The kernel sets up i= ts own GDT, IDT, stack, and prepares the full OS environment."

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    Kernel entry point =E2=80=94 The specific address (and therefore= the specific function) that is the first code the kernel executes. In a C = kernel, this is typically a function called kernel_main or kmain =E2=80= =94 the kernel's equivalent of main() in a regular C program. A small asse= mbly stub typically calls this function after setting up the environment (s= tack, segment registers).

    C runtime / crt0 =E2=80=94 The C runtime is the small amount of = code that runs before main() in a C program, setting up the environment th= at C code assumes: initializing the stack pointer, zeroing BSS (uninitializ= ed data), copying initialized data from ROM to RAM (on embedded systems), a= nd then calling main. In a kernel, a minimal version of this is implemente= d in assembly =E2=80=94 the bootloader has set up a temporary stack, and th= e assembly entry point sets up a proper kernel stack and clears BSS before = calling PIC remapping =E2=80=94 We covered this in the interrupt section= . The 8259 PIC (Programmable Interrupt Controller) is configured by the BIO= S to use interrupt vectors 0x08=E2=80=930x0F for IRQ0=E2=80=93IRQ7. But x86= exception vectors 0x08 (Double Fault), 0x0D (General Protection Fault), an= d 0x0E (Page Fault) overlap these. In protected mode, a timer interrupt (IR= Q0 =E2=86=92 vector 0x08) would be indistinguishable from a double fault ex= ception. The kernel must remap the PIC to use non-conflicting vectors (typi= cally 0x20=E2=80=930x2F for IRQ0=E2=80=93IRQ15).

    PIC initialization sequence =E2=80=94 Reprogramming the 8259 req= uires sending a specific sequence of "Initialization Command Words" (ICWs) = to the PIC's command and data ports:

    asm
    ; Remap PIC ; Master PIC: IRQ0-7 =E2=86=92 vectors 0x20-0x27 ; Slave PIC: IRQ8-15 =E2=86=92 vectors 0x28-0x2F ; ICW1: Begin initialization, cascade mode, ICW4 needed mov al, 0x11 out 0x20, al ; To master PIC command port out 0xA0, al ; To slave PIC command port ; ICW2: Vector offsets mov al, 0x20 ; Master: IRQ0 =E2=86=92 vector 0x20 out 0x21, al ; To master PIC data port mov al, 0x28 ; Slave: IRQ8 =E2=86=92 vector 0x28 out 0xA1, al ; To slave PIC data port ; ICW3: Cascade wiring mov al, 0x04 ; Master: slave connected to IRQ2 out 0x21, al mov al, 0x02 ; Slave: connected to master's IRQ2 out 0xA1, al ; ICW4: 8086 mode mov al, 0x01 out 0x21, al out 0xA1, al

    LIDT instruction =E2=80=94 Load IDT Register =E2=80=94 loads the= 6-byte base+limit structure (same format as LGDT) into the IDTR (IDT Regis= ter). After LIDT, the CPU uses the new IDT for all exception and interrupt = handling. Setting up IDT entries before LIDT is important =E2=80=94 you do = not want the CPU using an uninitialized IDT.

    EOI =E2=80=94 End of Interrupt =E2=80=94 A signal the kernel mus= t send to the PIC after handling each hardware interrupt. Write 0x20 to por= t 0x20 (master PIC command port) after handling IRQ0=E2=80=93IRQ7. For IRQ8= =E2=80=93IRQ15, must also write 0x20 to port 0xA0 (slave PIC). Without EOI,= the PIC will not send any more interrupts of the same or lower priority = =E2=80=94 your timer or keyboard will appear to stop after the first interr= upt.

    Enabling paging =E2=80=94 Set CR0.PG (bit 31) after loading CR3 = with the page directory address. CR3 must point to a valid page directory b= efore PG is set. Setting PG=3D1 is similar to setting PE=3D1 =E2=80=94 the = next instruction fetch already goes through the MMU, so the page tables mus= t correctly map the currently-executing code.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    The kernel's C e= ntry point

    After = the assembly stub has set up the stack and cleared BSS, it calls the C kern= el:

    c
    //=
 kernel.c
void kernel_main(void) {
    // 1. Set up IDT
    setup_idt();

    // 2. Remap PIC
    pic_remap(0x20, 0x28);

    // 3. Install interrupt handlers
    idt_set_gate(0,  (uint32_t)isr0,  0x08, 0x8E);  // Divis=
ion by zero
    idt_set_gate(13, (uint32_t)isr13, 0x08, 0x8E);  // GPF
    idt_set_gate(14, (uint32_t)isr14, 0x08, 0x8E);  // Page =
fault
    idt_set_gate(32, (uint32_t)irq0,  0x08, 0x8E);  // Timer=
 (IRQ0 =E2=86=92 vector 0x20)
    idt_set_gate(33, (uint32_t)irq1,  0x08, 0x8E);  // Keybo=
ard (IRQ1 =E2=86=92 vector 0x21)

    // 4. Load IDT
    idt_flush((uint32_t)&idt_ptr);

    // 5. Enable interrupts
    asm volatile("sti");

    // 6. Now the kernel is running with full interrupt sup=
port
    // Print something, or start the scheduler, etc.=

    terminal_writestring("Kernel loaded. Interrupts enabled.\n");

    // 7. Idle loop =E2=80=94 keep the CPU busy until inter=
rupted
    for(;;) {
        asm volatile("hlt");   // Pause until next interrupt=

    }
}

    The hlt instruction in the idle loop is important: it tells the CPU to enter a l= ow-power state and wait for the next interrupt. When an interrupt arrives, = the CPU wakes, handles it (runs the ISR), and then the hlt instruction is = re-executed. Without hlt, the idle loop would spin at full CPU speed consu= ming 100% CPU time doing nothing =E2=80=94 burning power and generating hea= t.

    First User= Process =E2=80=94 Ring 0 to Ring 3 =E2=80=94 T+~1800=E2=80=932000ms

    The sectio= n text

    "K= ernel subsystems ready. The kernel creates the first user process =E2=80=94= init on Linux =E2=80=94 dropping from Ring 0 to Ring 3 for the first time.= "

    Every term= , explained from absolute zero

    PID =E2=80=94 Process Identifier =E2=80=94 A unique number assig= ned to each running process by the operating system. PIDs are sequential in= tegers starting from 1 (some OSes reserve PID 0 for the idle process or the= kernel itself). PID 1 is always init (or systemd) on Linux =E2=80=94 the f= irst user-space process. All other processes are descendants of PID 1.

    init =E2=80=94 The first user-space process on Unix/Linux system= s. Named "init" for "initialization." Its job: read the system configuratio= n files (/etc/inittab or systemd unit files), start all system services (ne= tworking, logging, login manager, etc.), and become the "parent of all proc= esses." If init dies, the kernel panics =E2=80=94 the entire process hierar= chy has lost its root.

    systemd =E2=80=94 A modern init replacement used by most Linux d= istributions. Unlike traditional init (which ran shell scripts sequentially= ), systemd manages services as "units," starts them in parallel where possi= ble, tracks their dependencies, and provides extensive logging and process = management.

    smss.exe =E2=80=94 Session Manager Subsystem =E2=80=94 Windows's= equivalent of init. The first user-mode process after the kernel initializ= es. It starts the Windows subsystem (csrss.exe), the service control manage= r (services.exe), and the login process (winlogon.exe).

    IRET to user mode =E2=80=94 The mechanism for transitioning from= Ring 0 to Ring 3 for the first time (and for returning from system calls/i= nterrupts). IRET pops SS, ESP, EFLAGS, CS, and EIP from the kernel stack in= that order (for a ring transition). If the popped CS has RPL=3D3, the CPU = drops to Ring 3. This is covered completely in the privilege levels section= .

    PCB =E2=80=94 Process Control Block =E2=80=94 The kernel's data = structure for tracking one process. Contains: the process's PID, state (run= ning/waiting/sleeping/zombie), saved register values (for context switching= ), virtual address space information (CR3 value / page table root), open fi= le descriptors, signal handlers, priority, accounting data (CPU time used),= and pointers to parent and child processes.

    What actua= lly happened =E2=80=94 from first principles

    The complete seq= uence to launch the first user process

    At thi= s point, the kernel has: a working IDT, working interrupt handlers, a worki= ng timer, possibly a working keyboard handler, and some form of memory mana= gement. It now creates the first user process:

    1 =E2=80=94 Allocate physical frames for the process:

    The ke= rnel's physical memory allocator (a bitmap or free-list tracking which phys= ical frames are in use) allocates frames for:

    • = The kernel binary (already in RAM, mapped in this process's kernel address = space)
    • = The process's code (read from disk =E2=80=94 the /sbin/init executable)
    • = The process's initial stack (one frame =3D 4KB)
    • = The process's page directory and initial page tables

    2 =E2=80=94 Build a page directory for this process:

    The ke= rnel creates a new page directory. It maps:

    • = Kernel virtual address range (0xC0000000=E2=80=930xFFFFFFFF on Linux) =E2= =86=92 the kernel's physical pages. These pages have the User/Supervisor bi= t =3D 0 (supervisor only =E2=80=94 user code cannot access them).
    • = User virtual address range (0x00000000=E2=80=930xBFFFFFFF): maps the proces= s's code, data, and stack to the allocated physical frames, with User/Super= visor bit =3D 1 (user accessible).

    3 =E2=80=94 Set up the TSS (Task State Segment):

    The TS= S's SS0 and ESP0 fields are set to the kernel's stack segment selector and = the address of this process's kernel stack. When this process makes a syste= m call or takes an interrupt while in Ring 3, the CPU uses SS0:ESP0 from th= e TSS as the Ring 0 stack.

    4 =E2=80=94 Prepare the transition:

    The ke= rnel switches to the new process's page directory (writes CR3 with the new = page directory's physical address). Then sets up the kernel stack for the I= RET:

    asm
    ; Push what IRET will pop, in reverse order ; (IRET pops: EIP, CS, EFLAGS, ESP, SS) push user_ss ; SS for Ring 3 (data descriptor with RPL= =3D3, e.g., 0x23) push user_esp ; Initial stack pointer in user space push eflags ; EFLAGS with IF=3D1 (interrupts enabled in= user mode) push user_cs ; CS for Ring 3 (code descriptor with RPL= =3D3, e.g., 0x1B) push user_eip ; Entry point of the process (from the exec= utable's header) iret ; Pop all five, switch to Ring 3, jump to u= ser_eip

    5 =E2=80=94 The IRET executes:

    EIP is= loaded with the process's entry point (0x08048000 on classic Linux x86, or= wherever the executable was mapped). CS is loaded with a selector having R= PL=3D3 =E2=80=94 CPL instantly becomes 3. The CPU is now executing user cod= e. EFLAGS is restored with IF=3D1 =E2=80=94 hardware interrupts are enabled= in user mode, which is necessary for the timer to work.

    6 =E2=80=94 The system is up:

    The fi= rst user process is running. When it makes its first system call (to open a= file, read the configuration, etc.), INT 0x80 (or SYSCALL) fires, the CPU = transitions to Ring 0, the system call handler runs, and returns to Ring 3.= The timer fires 100 times per second, the kernel's timer ISR runs (in Ring= 0), decides to continue running this process (since it's the only one), an= d returns. Eventually, init starts forking child processes for system servi= ces, each fork creating a new PCB, a new page directory, and eventually mor= e IRET-to-Ring-3 transitions.

    The bo= ot process is complete.

    The comple= te map =E2=80=94 what we have traced

    From t= he moment of button press to the first user process, the chain of control i= s:

    Physical switch closes
  =E2=86=92 PWR_BTN# goes low
    =E2=86=92 Chipset detects button press
      =E2=86=92 PS_ON# asserted to PSU
        =E2=86=92 PSU ramps up voltages
          =E2=86=92 PWR_OK asserted
            =E2=86=92 Chipset releases CPU's RESET# pin
              =E2=86=92 CPU initializes registers to reset val=
ues
                =E2=86=92 CPU fetches from 0xFFFFFFF0 (firmwar=
e ROM)
                  =E2=86=92 Firmware far-jumps to BIOS body at=
 0xF0000
                    =E2=86=92 POST: CPU test, RAM init, device=
 enum, IVT setup
                      =E2=86=92 Firmware reads LBA 0 of boot d=
evice
                        =E2=86=92 Checks 0x55AA signature
                          =E2=86=92 Copies MBR to 0x7C00
                            =E2=86=92 Far jump to 0x0000:0x7C0=
0 (your bootloader)
                              =E2=86=92 Stage 1: Set up segmen=
ts, stack, load Stage 2
                                =E2=86=92 Stage 2: Enable A20,=
 load kernel, build GDT
                                  =E2=86=92 LGDT, set CR0.PE=
=3D1, far jump to 32-bit code
                                    =E2=86=92 Reload segment r=
egisters with flat descriptors
                                      =E2=86=92 Call kernel en=
try point
                                        =E2=86=92 Kernel: PIC =
remap, IDT setup, STI
                                          =E2=86=92 Enable pag=
ing (CR0.PG=3D1)
                                            =E2=86=92 Initiali=
ze subsystems
                                              =E2=86=92 Create=
 first process
                                                =E2=86=92 IRET=
 to Ring 3
                                                  =E2=86=92 in=
it runs

    Every = arrow is a hardware or software handoff. Every step has a specific reason. = Every piece of vocabulary in this document corresponds to something real = =E2=80=94 a voltage, a wire, a register value, a specific byte in memory.

    This i= s the complete boot process story, from absolute zero.

    You are = out of free messages until 7:50 PM

} 65%, 100% { background: var(--cds-alpha-3); } } .cds-root .cds-dot { background: var(--cds-alpha-3); border-radius: 9999px;= width: 3px; height: 3px; animation: 1.8s linear 0s infinite normal none ru= nning cds-dot-pulse; } .cds-root .cds-dot:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.2s; } .cds-root .cds-dot:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 0.4s; } @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .cds-root .cds-dot { animation: auto ease 0s 1 normal none running none;
